episode_title: #376 Hormone Therapy for Primary Prevention of Chronic Conditions in Postmenopausal Persons – a USPSTF update with Dr. Carol Mangione
statement: The USPSTF recommends against estrogen alone for primary prevention of chronic conditions in postmenopausal persons who have had a hysterectomy (grade D).
supporting_quote: we recommend against the use of estrogen alone for primary prevention of chronic conditions in postmenopausal persons who've had a hysterectomy, and that's a D recommendation also.

episode_title: #376 Hormone Therapy for Primary Prevention of Chronic Conditions in Postmenopausal Persons – a USPSTF update with Dr. Carol Mangione
statement: Combined estrogen and progestin therapy carries moderate harms including increased risk of invasive breast cancer, stroke, venous thromboembolism, dementia, gallbladder disease, and urinary incontinence.
supporting_quote: for women or people who take combined estrogen and progestin, the moderate harms include increased risk of invasive breast cancer, stroke, venous thromboembolism, dementia, gallbladder disease, and urinary incontinence.

episode_title: #376 Hormone Therapy for Primary Prevention of Chronic Conditions in Postmenopausal Persons – a USPSTF update with Dr. Carol Mangione
statement: The recommendation applies to prevention of chronic conditions, not to treatment of perimenopausal symptoms; hormones are highly effective for hot flashes and insomnia, and should be used at the lowest dose for the shortest time.
supporting_quote: hormones are highly effective for treating those. If you look at the recommendations of other groups such as the American College of Gynecology, what you see is that they recommend that you use the lowest dose for the shortest amount of time to manage those symptoms.

episode_title: #376 Hormone Therapy for Primary Prevention of Chronic Conditions in Postmenopausal Persons – a USPSTF update with Dr. Carol Mangione
statement: Although observational data suggested hormones prevented cardiovascular disease, the randomized Women's Health Initiative found no beneficial effect on coronary heart disease risk.
supporting_quote: it looked like taking hormones actually prevented cardiovascular disease. But then once we did a randomized trial, what we found was that there was no beneficial effect on the risk of coronary heart disease.

episode_title: #376 Hormone Therapy for Primary Prevention of Chronic Conditions in Postmenopausal Persons – a USPSTF update with Dr. Carol Mangione
statement: Hormone therapy provides moderate benefit for reducing fractures and small benefit for reducing diabetes and colon cancer, but safer alternatives exist for these outcomes.
supporting_quote: We know that there is moderate benefit for reducing fractures. We also know that for the combination of estrogen and progestin that there is small benefit for reducing the risk of diabetes and colon cancer. There are a lot of safer choices if you want to reduce those risks.

episode_title: #376 Hormone Therapy for Primary Prevention of Chronic Conditions in Postmenopausal Persons – a USPSTF update with Dr. Carol Mangione
statement: Most of the evidence base derives from the Women's Health Initiative, which used medroxyprogesterone (Provera) and conjugated estrogens (Premarin).
supporting_quote: It used two hormones, one called Provera, which is medroxyprogesterone, and the other called Premarin, which is conjugated estrogens. Most of the evidence base that we have is really about those two preparations.

episode_title: #376 Hormone Therapy for Primary Prevention of Chronic Conditions in Postmenopausal Persons – a USPSTF update with Dr. Carol Mangione
statement: The USPSTF did not evaluate topical hormone therapy such as vaginal estrogens or estrogen patches, as data on their benefits and harms remain relatively limited.
supporting_quote: If you're thinking about vaginal estrogens or estrogen patches, the amount of data or information on the benefits and harms of those preparations is really relatively small at this point.

episode_title: #376 Hormone Therapy for Primary Prevention of Chronic Conditions in Postmenopausal Persons – a USPSTF update with Dr. Carol Mangione
statement: There is insufficient long-term comparative evidence to support the idea that starting hormones right at menopause for a limited duration is safer.
supporting_quote: we really don't have those comparative effectiveness studies with the kind of long-term follow up that one would need to tell if you're increasing or decreasing major chronic conditions.

episode_title: #373 Recap Extravaganza 2022
statement: Most patients with constipation can be managed with a laxative trial alone; colonoscopy is often not part of the workup at all.
supporting_quote:
most patients are going to be okay with just like
a laxative trial. You don't have to go through all that fancy testing. Every patient with constipation
doesn't need a colonoscopy. In fact, that's often not even part of the workup at all.

episode_title: #373 Recap Extravaganza 2022
statement: Suspect defecatory/pelvic floor dysfunction in patients who report excessive straining and a sense of incomplete evacuation; these patients may benefit from pelvic floor physical therapy or a Squatty Potty.
supporting_quote:
The way that
you can recognize patients that have defecatory dysfunction or pelvic floor dysfunction is the
patient that says that they're straining a lot and that you need to describe it as doing toilet yoga.
They're saying they always have this sense of incomplete evacuation. Those are patients who
might benefit from actually pelvic floor physical therapy, or more simply, Paul, the Squatty Potty.

episode_title: #373 Recap Extravaganza 2022
statement: Laxative options for constipation include senna, bisacodyl, polyethylene glycol (PEG), and milk of magnesia; two kiwis a day (without the skin) can also help.
supporting_quote:
Senna and bisacodyl, the PEG polyethylene glycol or milk of magnesia, she

said, are some of the ones. And then Paul, the kiwis, two kiwis a day without the skin. That
works pretty well, too.

episode_title: #373 Recap Extravaganza 2022
statement: Erectile dysfunction can precede cardiovascular disease by up to five years, so it should prompt evaluation of cardiovascular risk factors and a conversation about smoking, lipids, blood pressure, and diabetes control.
supporting_quote:
Erectile dysfunction can precede
cardiovascular disease or another more scary cardiovascular disease by up to five years. This is
something I talk to my patients about all the time, and there's a great body of literature to
support this that if someone presents erectile dysfunction, that really should prompt you to
evaluate their cardiovascular risk factors.

episode_title: #373 Recap Extravaganza 2022
statement: PDE5 inhibitors only work in the setting of arousal, and priapism is a far less common side effect than commonly feared; priapism risk is greater with injectable medications.
supporting_quote:
these PDE5 inhibitors, they're only helpful in the setting of arousal. It's not like you take
them and then you're walking around with priapism, which is the other point that she made that
priapism is actually probably a far less common side effect than we give her credit for

episode_title: #373 Recap Extravaganza 2022
statement: Daily tadalafil is a good option for patients who have both BPH and erectile dysfunction.
supporting_quote:
She was very enthusiastic saying that her patients have had good
results if they have both BPH and erectile dysfunction, that the daily tadalafil has been a good
medication for a lot of them.

episode_title: #373 Recap Extravaganza 2022
statement: Choice of cardiac monitor for palpitations depends on symptom frequency: 24–48 hours for daily symptoms, 30-day patch for weekly symptoms, and implantable loop recorders for infrequent but concerning events like unexplained syncope.
supporting_quote:
If someone's having symptoms
four or five times a day, 24 to 48 hours of like, short-term monitoring with one of those patches is
probably sufficient. There are 30-day patches that you can wear, which would certainly be
appropriate for someone who has symptoms say once a week or once every couple of weeks or
so.

episode_title: #373 Recap Extravaganza 2022
statement: A two-week monitor is often the sweet spot for picking up arrhythmias in palpitations.
supporting_quote:
a two-week
monitor is often the sweet spot for-- you'll pick up a lot of things on a two-week
monitor.

episode_title: #373 Recap Extravaganza 2022
statement: Patients with a PVC burden greater than 10% should be referred to cardiology electrophysiology as they are at higher risk and may need intervention.
supporting_quote:
If the PVC
burden is greater than 10%, those are patients that really should be seeing cardiology
electrophysiology because they might need some intervention, because I think they're just more
at a higher risk group.

episode_title: #373 Recap Extravaganza 2022
statement: Ablation success rates for SVTs are extraordinarily good, upwards of 90%.
supporting_quote:
the success rate for the ablations, especially for the SVTs, is extraordinarily good, if
I remember correctly, depending on who you ask, but we're talking upwards of 90%

episode_title: #373 Recap Extravaganza 2022
statement: Blood pressure targets should not be based on casual office readings (patient on exam table, feet dangling, talking); use proper technique with feet supported, back against chair, and arm at heart level.
supporting_quote:
We don't base our
blood pressure targets on blood pressures that were measured that way. Those would not stand
up for a clinical trial and really making sure that you're getting good blood pressure readings
before you're making changes.

episode_title: #373 Recap Extravaganza 2022
statement: For home blood pressure monitoring, aim for at least six readings over a week, and validatebp.org lists cuffs recommended by experts.
supporting_quote:
Dr. Cohen said she likes to get six readings over the course
of a week, at least six readings.

episode_title: #373 Recap Extravaganza 2022
statement: Low-dose combination antihypertensive pills reduce side effects and better target the mechanism of hypertension by combining agents with different mechanisms.
supporting_quote:
the low dose combination, you're less likely to get side effects, and also, better chance
that you will hit the mechanism of their hypertension if you give them agents that work by
different mechanisms.

episode_title: #373 Recap Extravaganza 2022
statement: ARBs are preferred over ACE inhibitors because they provide the same benefits (renoprotection, systolic heart failure) with a more favorable side effect profile including less angioedema, cough, and GI bleeding.
supporting_quote:
ARBs tend to have a much more favorable side effect
profile compared to ACEs, so you're not going to see angioedema nearly as much. You're not
going to see a cough. I think cough is, astronomically lower with ARBs than are there with
ACEs.

GI bleeding, which was nowhere on my radar, is less common with ARBs than they are with
ACEs.

episode_title: #373 Recap Extravaganza 2022
statement: Losartan should be dosed twice daily because it provides more consistent blood pressure coverage over the course of the day.
supporting_quote:
that really should be a twice-daily medication,
which immediately made me feel stupid. I am now actually being more for patients that I think it
can be adherent with it, I'm now actually prescribing it twice daily when that's the ARB that I
reach for because it does tend to have more consistent coverage over the course of the day

episode_title: #373 Recap Extravaganza 2022
statement: ACE inhibitors and ARBs remain renoprotective in CKD4 and can be continued (with potassium tolerated up to 5.5); watch for a creatinine rise over 30% which raises concern for bilateral renal artery stenosis.
supporting_quote:
She said, "Actually, ACEs and ARBs can still be protective and help prevent the progression of
renal disease." So, you can still use them in patients with CKD4. You do have to watch for that
bump in creatinine. If it goes up more than 30%, you have to worry about bilateral renal artery
stenosis

episode_title: #373 Recap Extravaganza 2022
statement: Per the CLICK trial, chlorthalidone remains effective for hypertension in patients with advanced CKD (stage 3-4), though electrolytes must be monitored carefully.
supporting_quote:
I am now more liberal with chlorthalidone in my more advanced CKD patients. I think we
talked about this a fair amount, and I think that traditional teaching was just not as effective in
patients with more advanced chronic kidney disease. It turns out that it seems to work just fine.

episode_title: #373 Recap Extravaganza 2022
statement: Before testing for H. pylori, patients should be off PPIs, antibiotics, and H2-receptor antagonists; many guidelines advise at least a month off antibiotics, H2 blockers, and PPIs.
supporting_quote:
At least one to two weeks, they should be off all these
medicines, but a lot of the guidelines would tell you at least a month off antibiotics,
H2-receptors, antibiotics, and PPIs.

episode_title: #373 Recap Extravaganza 2022
statement: Do not start clarithromycin-based triple therapy for H. pylori due to resistance; bismuth-based quadruple therapy is often first line, with levofloxacin-based therapy as a next option.
supporting_quote:
just don't start with
the clarithromycin-based triple therapy. I mean, there's lots of resistance to that. That's not

recommended anymore by the guidelines or by Dr. Saffouri. So, what Dr. Saffouri mentioned
was that there's this Bismuth-based quadruple therapy. It can be tough for patients to tolerate,
but that's often the first line that he's going for. If that doesn't work, the next line that he would
use is often levofloxacin-based therapy.

episode_title: #373 Recap Extravaganza 2022
statement: Always retest for cure after treating H. pylori, because untreated infection carries a risk of gastric cancer.
supporting_quote:
don't forget to retest. You want to test for a cure, because if H. pylori
goes untreated, there's a risk for gastric cancer and you really want to treat the patients

episode_title: #373 Recap Extravaganza 2022
statement: Obesity should be conceptualized as a chronic disease often requiring chronic medication and therapy beyond behavioral changes, to prevent downstream metabolic consequences.
supporting_quote:
you could characterize it
and conceptualize it as a chronic disease state, particularly when there are metabolic
consequences.

episode_title: #373 Recap Extravaganza 2022
statement: Phentermine is effective for obesity, and the risk of hypertension and tachycardia is actually higher with bupropion than with phentermine.
supporting_quote:
Dr. Cody Stanford let us know that
actually the risk for hypertension and for I think even tachycardia is higher with bupropion, which
I will prescribe with wild abandon.

episode_title: #373 Recap Extravaganza 2022
statement: Topiramate is a good obesity medication choice for patients who also have migraine headaches.
supporting_quote: If someone has migraine headaches and obesity, it's a good choice.

episode_title: #373 Recap Extravaganza 2022
statement: Cardiorenal syndrome is now believed to be driven by elevated venous pressure and venous congestion rather than poor forward flow, which is why decongestion with diuretics is central to treatment.
supporting_quote:
Elevated venous pressure and venous congestion are actually believed to be the
pathophysiology of cardiorenal syndrome, because I had always been taught, it was poor
forward flow was the cause of cardiorenal syndrome, but it actually seems like it's venous
congestion.

episode_title: #373 Recap Extravaganza 2022
statement: In hospitalized heart failure patients with a small creatinine rise, ACE/ARB/SGLT2 inhibitors do not necessarily need to be stopped as long as the patient is not hypotensive and has no electrolyte disturbances; if stopped, they must be restarted at discharge.
supporting_quote:
our experts did not think that they need to be stopped necessarily, as long as the patient
wasn't hypotensive, they weren't hypertrophic, and didn't have electrolyte disturbances that
warranted discontinuation. I think the prevailing wisdom right now is it's probably okay to
continue those things as long as those parameters are okay.

episode_title: #373 Recap Extravaganza 2022
statement: Permissive hypercreatininemia: a small creatinine bump during diuresis of a volume-overloaded patient should not stop diuresis; assess exam, weight, and symptoms rather than using creatinine alone as the endpoint.
supporting_quote:
That's this concept of permissive
hypercreatininemia, which is hard to say, and we spend a lot of time talking about-- people often
are getting discharged too early, probably, or we're stopping too early because you start the
diuretic, you start to see the creatinine rise, then you go, "Oh, I'm done."

episode_title: #373 Recap Extravaganza 2022
statement: Sequential nephron blockade to augment loop diuretics can include acetazolamide, SGLT2 inhibitors, mineralocorticoid receptor antagonists like spironolactone, and thiazide diuretics.
supporting_quote:
We covered acetazolamide as helping to complement the diuretic
therapy you're giving. Other things you can do are SGLT2 inhibitors, mineralocorticoid receptor
antagonists like spironolactone and thiazide diuretics

episode_title: #373 Recap Extravaganza 2022
statement: Active injection drug use is not a contraindication to hepatitis C treatment; there is arguably a stronger indication to treat given individual and public health benefits.
supporting_quote:
it is not a contraindication. In fact,
nay-nay I would say there's probably an even stronger indication to offer treatment.

episode_title: #373 Recap Extravaganza 2022
statement: Because the hepatitis C antibody can stay positive for life, check a viral load yearly to detect reinfection in patients who continue to inject drugs after treatment.
supporting_quote:
I
believe it is to check a viral load on a yearly basis and patients continue to inject drugs who've
had to treat it for hepatitis C

episode_title: #373 Recap Extravaganza 2022
statement: After confirming active hepatitis C, stage the liver for fibrosis using tools like the FIB-4 calculator, elastography, or proprietary blood tests (FibroSure/FibroTest); patients with cirrhosis need ongoing hepatocellular carcinoma surveillance even after cure.
supporting_quote:
There's the FIB-4
calculator, or the pre-calculator, which uses some lab tests, which you would just readily have
available.

episode_title: #373 Recap Extravaganza 2022
statement: The AASLD/IDSA simplified hepatitis C treatment algorithm (with and without cirrhosis) is a one-page guide covering treatment-naive patients; refer patients needing retreatment to hepatology or infectious disease.
supporting_quote:
AASLD. Thank you. IDSA simplified treatment algorithm. There's both with and without
cirrhosis. It is one page that tells you really all you need to know that will actually get approved
by most insurance companies.

episode_title: #373 Recap Extravaganza 2022
statement: For preventive purposes in healthy adults, most vitamin supplementation shows wishy-washy data; beta carotene and vitamin E are associated with known harms and worse outcomes.
supporting_quote:
There are a couple that seems
to actually have known harms associated with them.
Specifically, I believe it's beta carotene and
vitamin E actually are associated with some worse outcomes.

episode_title: #369 Hand, Foot, Wrist and Ankle Pain, Myositis and Myopathy: A Rapid Review (TFTC)
statement: A positive Finkelstein maneuver (patient tucks thumb in fist, examiner deviates the wrist ulnarly) reproduces pain and suggests de Quervain tenosynovitis, while a positive CMC compression/grind test suggests first CMC joint arthritis.
supporting_quote:
Take your thumb, wrap your fingers
around it, and then the examiner will then score a point down almost like the patient's trying to stab you
with a knife. If you reproduce pain that way, that is positive Finkelstein test. They'll say the patient
probably has a flexor tenosynovitis.

The other test if you're looking for CMC arthritis is the CMC compression test, where basically avoiding
the microphone. He talks about taking the thumb like a joystick pushing down and just moving it around
like a mortar and pestle and seeing if he reproduces the pain that way. And if you do, congratulations,
you've diagnosed him with CMC arthritis

episode_title: #369 Hand, Foot, Wrist and Ankle Pain, Myositis and Myopathy: A Rapid Review (TFTC)
statement: Thumb spica splinting for de Quervain/CMC arthritis should be used for only a couple of weeks; prolonged immobilization risks loss of mobility.
supporting_quote:
A couple of
weeks to allow time for the inflammation to calm down is probably a good place to start. You don't want to do it forever
because you can lose mobility if you keep something immobile for a long period of time.

episode_title: #369 Hand, Foot, Wrist and Ankle Pain, Myositis and Myopathy: A Rapid Review (TFTC)
statement: Trigger finger often responds well to a steroid injection and may even be cured, and surgery to cut the annular pulley is definitive and non-recurring.
supporting_quote:
Most other things in the hand, this responds well to a steroid injection and that may even cure it. I've
had a lot of patients who had a trigger finger, got a steroid injection and just went away. Sometimes, it
comes back and there is a surgery to cut that annular pulley, which then it tends to just go away and
cure it. He said, "It doesn't recur if that's done."

episode_title: #369 Hand, Foot, Wrist and Ankle Pain, Myositis and Myopathy: A Rapid Review (TFTC)
statement: Carpal tunnel symptoms stem from nerve ischemia when the wrist is bent, so nighttime wrist bracing in a neutral position is a first-line treatment.
supporting_quote:
Because a lot of the ischemia occurs when the wrist is bent, you're bracing the person at
nighttime which is when people tend to sleep with their wrist bent for whatever reason

episode_title: #369 Hand, Foot, Wrist and Ankle Pain, Myositis and Myopathy: A Rapid Review (TFTC)
statement: A steroid injection for carpal tunnel is both diagnostic and therapeutic; lack of any response suggests a wrong diagnosis or advanced disease (e.g., thenar wasting).
supporting_quote:
a steroid injection, he mentioned can be both diagnostic and therapeutic because if the
patient doesn't respond to the steroid injection at all, it probably means either you have a wrong
diagnosis or you have to look for things like thenar wasting

episode_title: #369 Hand, Foot, Wrist and Ankle Pain, Myositis and Myopathy: A Rapid Review (TFTC)
statement: Carpal tunnel classically involves the radial 2.5 fingers (thumb, index, middle); other distributions or bilateral symptoms should prompt consideration of alternate causes such as cervical spine or systemic disease.
supporting_quote:
it should be the first two and a half fingers, so the thumb, the pointer finger, and the middle
finger, the radial side. If you see different distributions than that, you should think maybe this is coming
from somewhere else, maybe it's a different nerve impingement, maybe it's coming from the cervical
spine, especially if it's bilateral

episode_title: #369 Hand, Foot, Wrist and Ankle Pain, Myositis and Myopathy: A Rapid Review (TFTC)
statement: Bilateral carpal tunnel syndrome can precede the later development of cardiac amyloidosis by several years.
supporting_quote:
bilateral carpal tunnel syndrome can be a hurled for later development of cardiac amyloidosis. It often precedes it by
several years

episode_title: #369 Hand, Foot, Wrist and Ankle Pain, Myositis and Myopathy: A Rapid Review (TFTC)
statement: For ankle sprains, brace in a way that preserves dorsiflexion and plantar flexion; prolonged full immobilization (e.g., a boot) can impair proprioception and predispose to re-injury.
supporting_quote:
it's probably
best to be able to brace in a way that preserves at least dorsiflexion and plantar flexion. If you have
someone completely immobile or you put someone, say in a boot for an ankle sprain for a prolonged
period of time, they might lose proprioception or you might impair their proprioception. By doing so that
person might be set up for subsequent injury later on.

episode_title: #369 Hand, Foot, Wrist and Ankle Pain, Myositis and Myopathy: A Rapid Review (TFTC)
statement: A stirrup brace allows plantar flexion and dorsiflexion while protecting against the inversion/eversion movements that cause most sprains.
supporting_quote:
The stirrup brace is the one that it's usually white plastic with some green cushion inside
and that allows the patient to continue their plantar flexion and dorsiflexion at the ankle, but it protects
them from the eversion and inversion movements that often result in a lot of the sprains.

episode_title: #369 Hand, Foot, Wrist and Ankle Pain, Myositis and Myopathy: A Rapid Review (TFTC)
statement: Complete Achilles tendon rupture is typically dramatic, with patients describing a pop or feeling like they were kicked or shot in the back of the leg; partial tears are more subtle.
supporting_quote:
The complete Achilles tendon rupture is often very dramatic, at least the way patients describe it.
I think Dr. Ritter talks about patients may just feel like that they got kicked on the basketball court or
someone actually physically attacked them, or I think even if you read the literature, someone says they
felt like they got shot at the back of the leg. It's a discrete point in time that is dramatic and there's a pop
felt is often how it is described.

episode_title: #369 Hand, Foot, Wrist and Ankle Pain, Myositis and Myopathy: A Rapid Review (TFTC)
statement: Achilles tendon injuries require rest and rehab and are NOT amenable to steroid injections, which can increase the risk of rupture.
supporting_quote:
Mostly Achilles tendon injuries require a significant amount of rehab and rest unlike a lot of things in
musculoskeletal stuff are not amenable to steroid injections. That's one thing that you're not going to be
putting steroids into, because you actually can increase the risk of rupture if they haven't ruptured it
already.

episode_title: #369 Hand, Foot, Wrist and Ankle Pain, Myositis and Myopathy: A Rapid Review (TFTC)
statement: Posterior tibial tendon dysfunction presents with burning medial ankle pain and an acquired flatfoot deformity because the tendon supports the arch; it is more common in older, overweight patients.
supporting_quote:
What you're describing is posterior tibial tendon
dysfunction, which is exactly as you say, it happens in older-- I think it's more common to women, if I'm
not mistaken. Overweight can certainly be a risk factor for it. And so, this burning medial ankle pain and
then it's associated with flatfoot because the posterior tibial tendon actually helps support the arch and
keeps things pulled up.

episode_title: #369 Hand, Foot, Wrist and Ankle Pain, Myositis and Myopathy: A Rapid Review (TFTC)
statement: The 'too many toes sign' — seeing more than the usual two to two-and-a-half toes peeking laterally when viewing the foot from behind — suggests posterior tibial tendon dysfunction.
supporting_quote:
you actually look at the patient from behind and typically you should see maybe
two, two and a half toes peeking out laterally there. And someone who does not have a flatfoot or
posterior tibial tendon dysfunction, if they do, you might see more toes than that. So, more toes are
peeking out laterally and that is the so-called too many toes sign

episode_title: #369 Hand, Foot, Wrist and Ankle Pain, Myositis and Myopathy: A Rapid Review (TFTC)
statement: In myositis, weakness (not isolated pain) is the hallmark; you may have weakness without pain or with pain, but pain without weakness is likely not myositis.
supporting_quote:
This is not typically a
painful diagnosis or I should say that you're not going to see pain in isolation. That's not going to be
your presentation of myositis. You can have weakness without pain, you can have weakness with pain,
but you're not going to have pain without weakness, then you're probably outside the realm of myositis.

episode_title: #369 Hand, Foot, Wrist and Ankle Pain, Myositis and Myopathy: A Rapid Review (TFTC)
statement: Test neck flexors supine by gently holding the forehead and having the patient push up, and test hip flexors/extensors with your hand near the knee, to isolate proximal muscle groups and prevent recruitment.
supporting_quote:
with the neck flexors, it's
helpful to actually assess the patient while supine. In that way, they're not using their shoulders, they
actually gently hold down the forehead and ask them to push up. You're not pushing down their neck,
you're not choking off the patient, but you're applying sufficient but [Matt laughs] have to resisted.

episode_title: #369 Hand, Foot, Wrist and Ankle Pain, Myositis and Myopathy: A Rapid Review (TFTC)
statement: In inflammatory myositis, the ANA is often positive, but anemia is uncommon and inflammatory markers are often normal, so normal markers do not rule out the diagnosis.
supporting_quote:
So, the ANA is often positive in the myositis. But anemia is uncommon
and the inflammatory markers are often normal, which is, Paul, I had no idea that would not be what I
expected.

episode_title: #369 Hand, Foot, Wrist and Ankle Pain, Myositis and Myopathy: A Rapid Review (TFTC)
statement: Inclusion body myositis is distinguished by distal muscle weakness, unlike the proximal weakness typical of other inflammatory myopathies.
supporting_quote:
Inclusion body myositis is one of the ones where she said that they can get distal muscle
weakness.

episode_title: #369 Hand, Foot, Wrist and Ankle Pain, Myositis and Myopathy: A Rapid Review (TFTC)
statement: In dermatomyositis the rash is often itchy, and scalp itching/burning may be the most prominent, bothersome symptom—sometimes more so than weakness.
supporting_quote: Dermatomyositis, the rash is often itchy, which I found helpful.

episode_title: #369 Hand, Foot, Wrist and Ankle Pain, Myositis and Myopathy: A Rapid Review (TFTC)
statement: Before starting immunosuppressive therapy in myositis patients, address vaccinations, assess bone health, and counsel sun protection (SPF 70 or above) for photosensitive rashes.
supporting_quote:
you should think about getting
vaccinations before those meds are started assessing their bone health and then sun protection, Paul,
especially if they have some of the conditions that have photosensitive rashes, they need to wear really
heavy, she said SPF70 or above.

episode_title: #367 Weekend Warriors, Fluids for Acute Pancreatitis, Colonoscopy Screening & Mortality, and SPRINT trial Revisited (Hotcakes)
statement: WHO 2020 physical activity guidelines recommend adults perform 150 to 300 minutes of moderate activity or 75 to 150 minutes of vigorous activity per week.
supporting_quote:
adults are supposed to do 150 to 300 minutes of moderate activity or 75 to 150 of vigorous activity per
week.

episode_title: #367 Weekend Warriors, Fluids for Acute Pancreatitis, Colonoscopy Screening & Mortality, and SPRINT trial Revisited (Hotcakes)
statement: Concentrating the recommended weekly physical activity into one to two sessions (weekend warrior pattern) yields similar all-cause mortality reductions as spreading it out over the week, as long as the total volume is met.
supporting_quote:
there were similar mortality outcomes for weekend warriors
and regularly active participants. So, again, this is good news for folks who are planning to exercise on
one to two days.

episode_title: #367 Weekend Warriors, Fluids for Acute Pancreatitis, Colonoscopy Screening & Mortality, and SPRINT trial Revisited (Hotcakes)
statement: In the weekend warrior cohort study, hazard ratios for all-cause mortality were 0.92 for weekend warriors and 0.85 for regularly active participants versus inactive individuals.
supporting_quote:
They looked at this again as hazard ratios for all-cause mortality for weekend warriors
being 0.92 and for regularly active participants 0.85.

episode_title: #367 Weekend Warriors, Fluids for Acute Pancreatitis, Colonoscopy Screening & Mortality, and SPRINT trial Revisited (Hotcakes)
statement: In the WATERFALL trial, aggressive fluid resuscitation in acute pancreatitis used a 20 mL/kg bolus followed by 3 mL/kg/hour, versus moderate resuscitation with a 10 mL/kg bolus (only if hypovolemic) followed by 1.5 mL/kg/hour.
supporting_quote:
aggressive IV fluid resuscitation, which was a bolus of 20 mg/kg followed by a continuous
infusion of 3 mL/kg/hour or moderate IV fluid resuscitation. This was a bolus of 10 mg/kg, so half but
only if patients were hypovolemic followed by a continuous rate of half of the aggressive groups, so 1.5
mL/kg/hour.

episode_title: #367 Weekend Warriors, Fluids for Acute Pancreatitis, Colonoscopy Screening & Mortality, and SPRINT trial Revisited (Hotcakes)
statement: The WATERFALL trial was stopped early for harm; aggressive fluid resuscitation showed no efficacy benefit and caused far more volume overload (21% vs 6%) compared to moderate resuscitation.
supporting_quote:
It was stopped early by the
data safety monitoring board due to evidence of harm with regard to the primary outcome

episode_title: #367 Weekend Warriors, Fluids for Acute Pancreatitis, Colonoscopy Screening & Mortality, and SPRINT trial Revisited (Hotcakes)
statement: In WATERFALL, the primary composite outcome of moderate-to-severe pancreatitis occurred in 22% of the aggressive group versus 17% of the moderate group, with volume overload in 21% vs 6%.
supporting_quote:
occurred in 22% of patients randomized to the aggressive group and only 17% of patients
randomized to the moderate group.

episode_title: #367 Weekend Warriors, Fluids for Acute Pancreatitis, Colonoscopy Screening & Mortality, and SPRINT trial Revisited (Hotcakes)
statement: Evidence now supports moderate rather than aggressive fluid resuscitation in acute pancreatitis, contradicting prior guideline recommendations for aggressive fluids.
supporting_quote:
I really think that this article provides really strong
compelling evidence that there is not really much benefit to flooding people.

episode_title: #367 Weekend Warriors, Fluids for Acute Pancreatitis, Colonoscopy Screening & Mortality, and SPRINT trial Revisited (Hotcakes)
statement: In the SPRINT extended follow-up, the cardiovascular mortality benefit of intensive blood pressure control attenuated once systolic blood pressure returned toward 140 in both the intensive and standard arms after the trial ended.
supporting_quote:
the cardiovascular mortality benefit was
seen in the intensive arm between two to five and a half years and then it attenuated throughout the
observational phase. The systolic blood pressure interestingly also went back to around 140 for both
groups

episode_title: #367 Weekend Warriors, Fluids for Acute Pancreatitis, Colonoscopy Screening & Mortality, and SPRINT trial Revisited (Hotcakes)
statement: The SPRINT extended analysis reinforces that sustained tight blood pressure control is required for mortality benefit; benefits disappear when control lapses.
supporting_quote:
when the blood pressure was well controlled, you saw the
benefits. When it was less controlled those benefits went away. I think the bigger takeaway from this is
that-- There're lots of reasons for why it went that direction.

episode_title: #367 Weekend Warriors, Fluids for Acute Pancreatitis, Colonoscopy Screening & Mortality, and SPRINT trial Revisited (Hotcakes)
statement: In the Nordic colonoscopy screening trial, an invitation to screening reduced colorectal cancer diagnosis by 18% at 10 years but did not lower colorectal cancer-related or overall mortality.
supporting_quote:
The invitation to screen group had a lower risk of
colorectal cancer at 10 years.

Now that didn't translate to a lower risk of colorectal cancer-related mortality or a lower risk of overall
mortality. And so, that's an important discrimination there. It was an 18% lower risk of colorectal cancer

diagnosis over those 10 years.

episode_title: #367 Weekend Warriors, Fluids for Acute Pancreatitis, Colonoscopy Screening & Mortality, and SPRINT trial Revisited (Hotcakes)
statement: In the Nordic trial, the number needed to invite to colonoscopy screening to prevent one colorectal cancer case was 455.
supporting_quote:
The number needed to invite of the screening test was 455 individuals
to prevent one case of colorectal cancer.

episode_title: #367 Weekend Warriors, Fluids for Acute Pancreatitis, Colonoscopy Screening & Mortality, and SPRINT trial Revisited (Hotcakes)
statement: Only 42% of people invited to colonoscopy in the Nordic trial actually underwent one, which biases intention-to-treat results toward the null and limits interpretation of colonoscopy efficacy.
supporting_quote:
Overall, only 42% of the people in the intervention
group actually ended up getting a colonoscopy, which is a big drop off between the number of people
who were emailed or who were snail mailed an invitation and who ultimately got a colonoscopy. So,
that's an important source of bias towards the no finding.

episode_title: #367 Weekend Warriors, Fluids for Acute Pancreatitis, Colonoscopy Screening & Mortality, and SPRINT trial Revisited (Hotcakes)
statement: In the Nordic trial, higher colonoscopy uptake by country (33% in Poland to 61% in Norway) correlated with a larger reduction in colorectal cancer mortality in the per-protocol analysis, suggesting a dose-response effect.
supporting_quote:
the rates of uptake differed quite a lot from a low of
33% of people in Poland to a high of 61% of people in Norway. And so, in the adjusted-per-protocol
analysis, the effect size on colorectal cancer mortality was bigger in Norway than in Poland

episode_title: #361 Advanced Lipidology
statement: Four statin benefit groups exist in primary prevention: LDL above 190, diabetes over age 40 (moderate-intensity, or high-intensity if risk factors), 10-year risk above 20%, and selected patients after risk discussion.
supporting_quote:
so in primary
prevention, individuals with LDLs above 190, this group is enriched with those who have familial
hypercholesterolemia. So, they would benefit from a high-intensity statin. Patients with diabetes over
the age of 40 should be at least on a moderate-intensity statin. And then if they have risk factors,
high-intensity statin. And then individuals who are above 20% 10-year risk score, those also are
indication for a high-intensity statin.

episode_title: #361 Advanced Lipidology
statement: A coronary artery calcium score can help refine risk and guide shared decision-making when uncertainty remains after the 10-year risk estimate and risk-enhancing factors.
supporting_quote:
there can still be uncertainty about an individual's risk. And in those cases, a
coronary artery calcium score can be performed to help refine risk a little better and guide shared
decision-making about preventive therapies like statins and possibly aspirin.

episode_title: #361 Advanced Lipidology
statement: Any nonzero calcium score indicates plaque and disease and warrants starting a statin; statins are recommended for scores above 100, above the 75th percentile for age/gender, or can be considered for any nonzero score.
supporting_quote:
the guidelines had said, a
statin is recommended if the calcium score is above a 100, but it could be considered for any nonzero
score. So, that's how I practice certainly any nonzero score, they have plaque, they have a disease so,
I start a statin.

episode_title: #361 Advanced Lipidology
statement: A zero calcium score is more reassuring in older adults; in younger patients plaque may not have had time to calcify, so it should not supersede clinical judgment, especially if symptoms of ischemia are present.
supporting_quote:
that should not
supersede clinical judgment. If a patient's actually having symptoms of ischemic heart disease, you
wouldn't necessarily be reassured about a zero score. So, I put less stock in a zero score in a younger
person, they might not have had time to calcify their plaque yet. But it is reassuring in an older adult, if
their score is still zero.

episode_title: #361 Advanced Lipidology
statement: Cardiovascular risk relates to cumulative LDL exposure (cholesterol-years, analogous to pack-years), so treating a younger patient with elevated LDL early is favored even if 10-year risk is low.
supporting_quote:
it's not just the magnitude of elevation of LDL, but the duration of exposure akin to we
think about pack years of smoking. If there's this theoretical threshold, some have said about 5000
milligrams per deciliters, a milligram per deciliter years

episode_title: #361 Advanced Lipidology
statement: Lipoprotein(a) is highly genetic (about 90% determined by the Lp(a) gene), proatherogenic, prothrombotic, and causally linked to ASCVD and aortic stenosis; elevation above 50 mg/dL affects about one in five people.
supporting_quote:
Lipoprotein(a) was highly genetic. About 90%
of it is related to the Lipoprotein(a) gene. So, it's highly heritable and it runs in families.

episode_title: #361 Advanced Lipidology
statement: When measuring Lp(a), know whether the value is in mass (mg/dL) or molar (nmol/L) units because cut points differ; there is no direct conversion, though multiplying mg/dL by 2 to 2.5 gives a rough estimate of nmol/L.
supporting_quote:
It is important to know the lab differences between whether it's measured in mass by milligram per
deciliter or molar, which is nano mils per liter, it's important to know the difference because the cut
points are different based on the assay.

episode_title: #361 Advanced Lipidology
statement: Statins do not lower Lp(a) and may even increase it, but are still used because of overwhelming evidence they reduce major adverse vascular events.
supporting_quote:
as I mentioned statins don't lower and they may even increase Lipoprotein(a) but we still
use statin therapy primarily because there's overwhelming evidence that statins reduce major adverse
vascular events.

episode_title: #361 Advanced Lipidology
statement: PCSK9 monoclonal antibodies and inclisiran lower Lp(a) by about 20 to 25%, so measuring Lp(a) may favor earlier use of a PCSK9 inhibitor, though they are not FDA-approved specifically for Lp(a) lowering.
supporting_quote:
both PCSK9 Inhibitor, monoclonal antibodies, as
well as inclisiran this new agent we can talk about, small interfering RNA against PCSK9, those agents
do lower Lipoprotein(a) about 20 to 25%.

episode_title: #361 Advanced Lipidology
statement: Niacin and estrogen can lower Lp(a) but are not recommended for that purpose due to lack of demonstrated outcome benefit.
supporting_quote:
even though niacin can lower Lipoprotein(a), we don't recommend that given the lack of
clinical benefit and two outcome trials. Estrogen can lower Lipoprotein(a) as well but hormone
replacement therapy is not recommended for the sole purpose of Lipoprotein(a) lowering because it
also hasn't been demonstrated to reduce outcomes.

episode_title: #361 Advanced Lipidology
statement: Roughly every 10 mg/dL of Lp(a) raises LDL by about 3 to 4 mg/dL, so patients with low LDL but high Lp(a) may have substantial residual risk from the Lp(a) component.
supporting_quote:
on
average every 10 mg/dL of Lipoprotein(a) in the blood increases LDL by about 3-4 mg/dl.

episode_title: #361 Advanced Lipidology
statement: Ezetimibe and bempedoic acid lower LDL but do not lower Lp(a); LDL remains the primary treatment target.
supporting_quote:
Ezetimibe and bempedoic acid don't lower Lipoprotein(a), but they certainly lower LDL. And
we absolutely use those agents in appropriate candidates who need additional LDL lowering and LDL
still remains the primary target.

episode_title: #361 Advanced Lipidology
statement: Pelacarsen, an antisense oligonucleotide targeting Lp(a), lowered Lp(a) by 80% in phase 2 and is being studied for cardiovascular outcomes in the phase 3 HORIZON trial.
supporting_quote:
the agent that's the farthest along in trials is already in phase three cardiovascular outcome
trials is pelacarsen. So, this is the antisense oligonucleotide targeting Lipoprotein(a). And in phase two
trials, it was shown to reduce Lipoprotein(a) by 80%.

episode_title: #361 Advanced Lipidology
statement: Olpasiran, a small interfering RNA targeting Lp(a) mRNA, lowered Lp(a) by 90% in the phase 2 OCEAN trial with no safety concerns.
supporting_quote:
the top-line results from the OCEAN study, which is a phase two trial that enrolled 281 individuals,
so small numbers because it's a phase two, but in this phase two trial, this agent also lowered
Lipoprotein(a) by 90%. So, again, very effective, and in this smaller phase two trial there were no safety
concerns.

episode_title: #361 Advanced Lipidology
statement: For high-risk patients where the target LDL is known, combination lipid therapy should be started early rather than using sequential single-agent titration, since there is no risk of LDL being too low.
supporting_quote:
I would move early with
combination therapy, and I just wanted to make the plug that if you know where you need to go and
where you're starting, I think we waste too much time with sequential therapy, just like blood pressure,
you start combination therapy early

episode_title: #361 Advanced Lipidology
statement: Evolocumab and alirocumab (PCSK9 monoclonal antibodies) are FDA approved with outcome data showing at least a 15% reduction in major adverse cardiovascular events, with a legacy benefit favoring earlier treatment.
supporting_quote:
we have two monoclonal
antibodies that are already FDA approved evolocumab and aliracumab and those both have clinical
outcome data. So not only do they lower LDL, but they also reduce major adverse cardiovascular
events by at least 15%.

episode_title: #361 Advanced Lipidology
statement: Inclisiran is a small interfering RNA against PCSK9 that lowers LDL by about 50%, is given initially, again at 3 months, then every 6 months, and is FDA approved for ASCVD or FH not at LDL goal, though outcome data (ORION-4) are pending.
supporting_quote:
After you do the initial injection and once again at three months,
then you go to every six months. And it's been shown in the ORION trials to lower LDL about 50%

episode_title: #361 Advanced Lipidology
statement: PCSK9 inhibitors are meant to be added on a background of statins, as in the FOURIER and ODYSSEY OUTCOMES trials; you would not combine inclisiran with a PCSK9 monoclonal antibody.
supporting_quote:
In
fact, both in the FOURIER and the ODYSSEY OUTCOMES trials, which were
the outcome trials for PCSK9 Inhibitors. These were patients with ASCVD who were all on a
background of statins.

episode_title: #361 Advanced Lipidology
statement: Bempedoic acid is an oral first-in-class ATP citrate lyase inhibitor that lowers LDL about 18% and, combined with ezetimibe, about 36%; because it is a prodrug activated only in the liver it lacks statin-associated muscle symptoms and does not worsen glycemia.
supporting_quote:
bempedoic acid is a prodrug that requires activation and
an enzyme to activate it is only in the liver, it's not in muscle. So, it does not have these
muscle-associated symptoms that have been reported in statins.

episode_title: #361 Advanced Lipidology
statement: Bempedoic acid can raise uric acid levels and has a signal for tendon rupture; a baseline uric acid may be checked especially in patients with gout history, though routine monitoring is not required.
supporting_quote:
they can elevate uric acid levels. So, if you have a patient who has had gout or that's
something to be mindful of and discuss with patients for that. It's not necessarily required that you
measure uric acid to follow on this therapy.

episode_title: #361 Advanced Lipidology
statement: In REDUCE-IT, icosapent ethyl (high-dose EPA 4 grams daily) in statin-treated patients with residual triglycerides of 135 to 500 reduced major adverse cardiovascular events by 25%, whereas combined EPA/DHA in STRENGTH showed no benefit.
supporting_quote:
High-dose EPA 4 grams a day compared to placebo and showed a
really dramatic 25% reduction in major adverse cardiovascular events. We did not see this with other
agents that were combined EPA and DHA. Notably, the STRENGTH trial, which also studied a
high-dose omega 3 formulation did not produce cardiovascular events

episode_title: #361 Advanced Lipidology
statement: Triglycerides are far more responsive than LDL to lifestyle measures such as weight loss, reducing saturated fats and simple carbohydrates, limiting alcohol, and increasing physical activity.
supporting_quote:
much more so than LDL, triglycerides are very responsive
to weight loss, even modest amounts of weight loss can influence triglyceride levels, and dietary
changes, reducing saturated fats, having more polyunsaturated fats, reducing simple carbs, reducing
alcohol, increasing physical activity is all really important for management of elevated triglycerides.

episode_title: #361 Advanced Lipidology
statement: Reported statin-associated muscle symptoms are largely a nocebo effect, with a Lancet meta-analysis showing about 90% of symptoms also elicited by placebo, and severe muscle injury being rare (about 1 in 100,000).
supporting_quote:
There was just a paper out in Lancet, again a
meta-analysis, really showing this nocebo effect that 90% of these reported statin-associated symptoms
are elicited by the placebo too that are not true drug effects

episode_title: #361 Advanced Lipidology
statement: Starting a PCSK9 inhibitor in the hospital immediately after an acute coronary syndrome is safe and can show plaque reduction on IVUS, supporting early initiation.
supporting_quote:
There's now data showing benefit If you get PCSK9 in the
hospital immediately after acute coronary syndrome event not only is that safe, but you can show
plaque reduction on IVUS.

episode_title: #360 Disease-Modifying Nutrition with Dr. Michelle McMacken
statement: Substituting even small amounts (as little as 3% of calories) of animal protein with plant protein is associated with a mortality benefit of up to 35%, particularly when replacing processed meat, red meat, or eggs.
supporting_quote:
We have at least five very, very large prospective cohort studies from around the world showing that even just substituting very small amounts, sometimes as little as 3% of your calories
of animal protein with a plant source buys you an associated mortality benefit of up to 35%, if what
you're dropping is processed meat, if what you're dropping is red meat, if what you're dropping is eggs

episode_title: #360 Disease-Modifying Nutrition with Dr. Michelle McMacken
statement: Processed meats have strong evidence for increasing risk of colorectal cancer, heart disease (coronary and heart failure), and type 2 diabetes, with all guidelines in consensus.
supporting_quote:
There is an extraordinary amount of
evidence that these increase the risk of a variety of different types of cancer, particularly colorectal
cancer, they increase the risk of heart disease whether it's coronary heart disease or heart failure, they
markedly increase the risk of type 2 diabetes. That's quite clear and all the guidelines are in consensus
about that.

episode_title: #360 Disease-Modifying Nutrition with Dr. Michelle McMacken
statement: Guidelines such as the American College of Cardiology recommend consuming one or two servings a week of fatty fish.
supporting_quote:
It is in the
guidelines for, say, the American College of Cardiology and numerous other guidelines to consume one
or two servings a week of fatty fish.

episode_title: #360 Disease-Modifying Nutrition with Dr. Michelle McMacken
statement: A diet free of animal foods but high in juice, sugar-sweetened beverages, white flour, and desserts carries a coronary heart disease and diabetes risk just as high as an animal-based diet, so not all plant-based diets are healthy.
supporting_quote:
when you focus on nonanimal foods, but you're
consuming juice and sugar sweetened beverages, and white flour and desserts, but no animal foods,
your coronary heart disease risk, your diabetes risk is just as high as if you were eating an
animal-based diet. So, that's not a good swap.

episode_title: #360 Disease-Modifying Nutrition with Dr. Michelle McMacken
statement: Diet is the leading risk factor for dying of chronic disease in the United States and globally.
supporting_quote:
what we eat is the leading
risk factor for dying of a chronic disease in the United States and globally.

episode_title: #360 Disease-Modifying Nutrition with Dr. Michelle McMacken
statement: A 24-hour dietary recall is a quick, useful clinical tool that provides a concrete snapshot of a patient's typical eating pattern, and is preferred over asking generally what patients like to eat.
supporting_quote:
I do like to do a 24-hour recall, it is far from accurate compared to
what the person actually ate for the last 24 hours, but it does give you a quick snapshot of the types of
foods that they probably are eating typically

episode_title: #360 Disease-Modifying Nutrition with Dr. Michelle McMacken
statement: When counseling on diet, always circle back to ask about beverages, including ginger ale, which patients frequently do not consider to be soda.
supporting_quote:
you always have to make sure to
swing back and ask about beverages. And ask about ginger ale because nobody thinks that that's soda
for some reason. They think it's good for your health.

episode_title: #360 Disease-Modifying Nutrition with Dr. Michelle McMacken
statement: A 'crowding out' strategy of adding healthy foods a patient already likes is more effective and less blaming than telling patients to stop eating certain foods.
supporting_quote:
the crowding out strategy is much
more effective than the stop-eating meat strategy and stop eating this and stop doing that. That's just a
really negative downer message that nobody wants to hear.

episode_title: #360 Disease-Modifying Nutrition with Dr. Michelle McMacken
statement: The ADA defines diabetes remission as an A1c less than 6.5% off medications for more than three months.
supporting_quote:
he's technically in remission,
now. So, his is A1c is less than 6.5 off medications for
more than three months. That's the ADA definition of remission from, the most recent one.

episode_title: #360 Disease-Modifying Nutrition with Dr. Michelle McMacken
statement: There is strong evidence for a causal relationship between saturated fat intake and increased LDL; fat quality (source) matters more than total amount.
supporting_quote:
there is so
much evidence to support a causal relationship between saturated fat intake and LDL increase that
right there should stop you in your tracks. If nothing else, you can literally watch yourself if you start
consuming say a lot of coconut oil, you can just watch your LDL go up linearly.

episode_title: #360 Disease-Modifying Nutrition with Dr. Michelle McMacken
statement: Lifestyle medicine practice should focus on behavior change with minimal lab ordering; a minimalist approach (A1c, lipid panel, basic metabolic panel, occasional TSH) is appropriate rather than checking inflammatory markers, hormone levels, or extra vitamin levels.
supporting_quote:
I'm a minimalist,
it sounds like you are too. And I will just send literally what is very obviously needed, repeating in an
A1c when needed, lipid panel, basic metabolic if I need to monitor their potassium for medication
reasons or what have you, and every now and then a TSH.

episode_title: #360 Disease-Modifying Nutrition with Dr. Michelle McMacken
statement: Patients on a fully plant-based (vegan) diet require a B12 supplement, as B12 is not obtained from plant foods.
supporting_quote:
for sure you're going to need to
take a B12 supplement, there is no question that we don't get B12 from plant foods and we get B12
from fortified plant foods, and that's an option but I just recommend a supplement.

episode_title: #360 Disease-Modifying Nutrition with Dr. Michelle McMacken
statement: Calcium from low-oxalate leafy greens (kale, bok choy, broccoli, collard greens) is absorbed at a higher rate than from dairy, and fortified plant-based milks are absorbed comparably to dairy.
supporting_quote:
a lot of the low oxalate leafy greens so the kales, the bok choy that broccoli, collard greens we absorb
calcium from those foods at a much higher rate than we do say from dairy.
And then all of the plant, the non-dairy milks, the plant-based milks that are on the market, the vast
majority are fortified in calcium and we absorb it the same as we would from dairy.

episode_title: #360 Disease-Modifying Nutrition with Dr. Michelle McMacken
statement: Healthy carbohydrates (legumes, fruits, whole grains) are not the cause of type 2 diabetes and can actually improve insulin resistance due to their fiber, antioxidants, and weight-loss effects.
supporting_quote:
if we're
talking about healthy carbs, those are not the cause of type 2 diabetes, and they're actually part of the
solution. The evidence is very clear that when you're consuming a diet that has healthy carbohydrate
foods in it, you can actually help improve insulin resistance.

episode_title: #360 Disease-Modifying Nutrition with Dr. Michelle McMacken
statement: Adding healthy protein or fat to a food can blunt the blood sugar response, so pairing a sweet fruit with nuts lowers the glycemic spike.
supporting_quote:
you can also lower your blood sugar response to any food by adding some healthy
protein or fat. So that's a trick that I like to tell patients if you're having a really sweet fruit, you're having
mangoes and you know your sugar is going to go up, eat it with some-- eat a few peanuts with your
mango and your sugar won't go as high.

episode_title: #360 Disease-Modifying Nutrition with Dr. Michelle McMacken
statement: Before counseling on nutrition, clinicians must screen for food insecurity to establish whether a patient has enough food at all.
supporting_quote:
if we're going to be counseling
on nutrition, we have to ask about food insecurity, like that's the first thing you have to ask about. And
you can't assume anything and you have to find out if someone actually needs food.

episode_title: #357 Hotcakes: Ultra-short Antibiotics for Pneumonia, Acetazolamide for CHF, Psilocybin for AUD, and Asymptomatic Kidney Stone Removal
statement: In hospitalized patients coded for suspected pneumonia who maintain oxygen saturation ≥95% on room air, an ultra-short antibiotic course (1-2 days) was not associated with increased mortality compared to a 5-8 day course, and yielded shorter hospital stays and less antibiotic exposure.
supporting_quote:
they found that the patients who were given the shorter
courses versus the longer courses, they did not have increased mortality. They had maybe even a little
bit less shorter duration of hospital stay, which makes sense, and less exposure to antibiotics.

episode_title: #357 Hotcakes: Ultra-short Antibiotics for Pneumonia, Acetazolamide for CHF, Psilocybin for AUD, and Asymptomatic Kidney Stone Removal
statement: This ultra-short antibiotic study applies to patients on the edge of the pneumonia diagnosis (sats ≥95% on room air), many of whom likely did not have bacterial pneumonia—only 4% had bacteria identified on sputum culture versus a typical ~15% in community-acquired pneumonia.
supporting_quote:
this is kind of corroborated by the fact that only 4% of patients in this study had any bacteria identified on a sputum
culture at all. And even with the limitations of diagnostics in pneumonia, we know that in
community-acquired pneumonia, the rate of culture for bacterial organisms is closer to like 15%.

episode_title: #357 Hotcakes: Ultra-short Antibiotics for Pneumonia, Acetazolamide for CHF, Psilocybin for AUD, and Asymptomatic Kidney Stone Removal
statement: This does not mean everyone with pneumonia should get 2 days of antibiotics; the word 'suspected' is key—it applies to suspected pneumonia, not confirmed pneumonia with dense infiltrate and hypoxemia.
supporting_quote:
this is not telling us that we should just be giving everyone with pneumonia, 2
days of antibiotics instead of 5, 7, 10 days.

episode_title: #357 Hotcakes: Ultra-short Antibiotics for Pneumonia, Acetazolamide for CHF, Psilocybin for AUD, and Asymptomatic Kidney Stone Removal
statement: In adults with alcohol use disorder, psilocybin-assisted psychotherapy produced a greater decrease in percentage of heavy drinking days over 32 weeks compared to diphenhydramine plus psychotherapy, though both arms improved.
supporting_quote:
over the course of that 32 weeks,
which was the length of the study, there was a greater decrease in alcohol use in the psilocybin arm
compared to the diphenhydramine arm.

episode_title: #357 Hotcakes: Ultra-short Antibiotics for Pneumonia, Acetazolamide for CHF, Psilocybin for AUD, and Asymptomatic Kidney Stone Removal
statement: The psilocybin AUD trial used psilocybin dosed by weight (25 mg/70 kg first session) with a higher dose at the second session if the mystical-experience score was low; the control group received diphenhydramine 50 mg then 100 mg, with patients supervised for eight hours each session.
supporting_quote:
so 25 mg/70 kg for the first session, in the study group and then it for the second
administration that medication weeks later if they scored low on this test that they gave-- It was actually
called the Panhnke, and forgive if I mispronounced this, Richards Mystical Experience Questionnaire.
So, if they didn't have a sufficiently mystical experience during the first session, then they got a higher
dose for the second session. And then, the control group received diphenhydramine 50 mg the first
session and 100 mg the second dose of medications, both groups had to be supervised for eight hours.

episode_title: #357 Hotcakes: Ultra-short Antibiotics for Pneumonia, Acetazolamide for CHF, Psilocybin for AUD, and Asymptomatic Kidney Stone Removal
statement: The psilocybin AUD trial is resource-intensive—patients were supervised for eight hours per session with intensive psychotherapy—making it hard to operationalize; it is not something the average primary care office could deliver.
supporting_quote:
these
patients were sat with for eight hours at a time for safety's sake. There was fairly intensive
psychotherapy that happened over the course of time. So, it involves a lot of resources that I even now
don't have access to.

episode_title: #357 Hotcakes: Ultra-short Antibiotics for Pneumonia, Acetazolamide for CHF, Psilocybin for AUD, and Asymptomatic Kidney Stone Removal
statement: In the ADVOR trial, adding IV acetazolamide 500 mg once daily to loop diuretics in acute decompensated heart failure increased successful decongestion by day three (42.2% vs 30.5% placebo), an absolute difference of 11.7% and number needed to treat of nine.
supporting_quote:
successful decongestion by day three, the primary
outcome, happened in 42.2% of patients who were randomized to acetazolamide in comparison with
30.5% of patients randomized to placebo. So, that is an absolute difference of 11.7% and that
corresponds to a number needed to treat of nine patients to achieve decongestion over three days.

episode_title: #357 Hotcakes: Ultra-short Antibiotics for Pneumonia, Acetazolamide for CHF, Psilocybin for AUD, and Asymptomatic Kidney Stone Removal
statement: In ADVOR, acetazolamide shortened length of stay by about a day and increased decongestion at discharge, but there was no difference at three months in death, heart failure rehospitalization, and no glaring safety signals.
supporting_quote:
length of stay was shorter by about
a day, more patients randomized to acetazolamide were decongested at discharge. But there was no
difference in endpoints at three months that included death, rehospitalization for heart failure, and there
were no glaring safety signals.

episode_title: #357 Hotcakes: Ultra-short Antibiotics for Pneumonia, Acetazolamide for CHF, Psilocybin for AUD, and Asymptomatic Kidney Stone Removal
statement: In ADVOR, patients were on background oral furosemide at least 40 mg/day for at least a month, could not be on acetazolamide or an SGLT2 inhibitor, and received loop diuretics at twice their usual home dose along with the study drug.
supporting_quote:
they had to be on the equivalent of an oral dose of furosemide at
least 40 mg a day for at least a month. Couldn't have been on acetazolamide or an HLT2 inhibitor.
Couple other exclusions in there too. Patients were randomized in a one-to-one fashion to get either
acetazolamide 500 mg IV once daily or placebo, plus IV loop diuretics in the total daily amount of two
times the patient's usual home dose.

episode_title: #357 Hotcakes: Ultra-short Antibiotics for Pneumonia, Acetazolamide for CHF, Psilocybin for AUD, and Asymptomatic Kidney Stone Removal
statement: Subgroup analyses suggest acetazolamide may work better in less severe heart failure (LVEF >40, less than 60 mg furosemide maintenance daily, baseline congestion score <4), and its benefit may reflect more effective diuresis and natriuresis.
supporting_quote:
the
magnitude of the treatment effect was greater in people with LV ejection fractions greater than 40,
people who are on less than 60 mg of furosemide for maintenance daily, in people with a baseline
congestion score of less than four.

episode_title: #357 Hotcakes: Ultra-short Antibiotics for Pneumonia, Acetazolamide for CHF, Psilocybin for AUD, and Asymptomatic Kidney Stone Removal
statement: In a small randomized trial, removal of small (≤6 mm) asymptomatic kidney stones led to a 75% lower incidence of relapsing stone disease over 4.2 years (about 16% relapse in the treatment group vs 63% in controls).
supporting_quote:
there was 75% lower incidence of relapsing stone removal at this 4.2-year follow-up. There was about 16% of
patients in the treatment group had a relapse versus 63% of patients in the control group.

episode_title: #357 Hotcakes: Ultra-short Antibiotics for Pneumonia, Acetazolamide for CHF, Psilocybin for AUD, and Asymptomatic Kidney Stone Removal
statement: Removing small asymptomatic kidney stones added about 25 minutes (a ~38% increase) of OR time on average, but cost analysis suggested it was cheaper than ER visits and recurrent stone procedures.
supporting_quote:
There's about 25 minutes extra time in
the OR on average for patients who are having these asymptomatic stones removed, and they
mentioned that was about a 38% increase in time.

episode_title: #349 Hotcakes: Tirzepatide for weight loss, Personalized Antidepressants, Seasonal Edema, Nirmatrelvir for low-risk COVID-19
statement: In the SURMOUNT-1 trial, tirzepatide produced dose-dependent weight loss of about 15% at the lowest dose up to nearly 21% at the 15mg dose, versus only about 3% in the placebo group, in patients with obesity or overweight without diabetes.
supporting_quote: they had a dose-dependent weight loss, that the average weight loss in the lower dose group was somewhere around 15%, all the way up to almost 21% weight loss from baseline in the 15mg group of tirzepatide. And in the placebo group, there was only a 3% weight loss.

episode_title: #349 Hotcakes: Tirzepatide for weight loss, Personalized Antidepressants, Seasonal Edema, Nirmatrelvir for low-risk COVID-19
statement: Roughly half of patients on the 10mg dose and about 57% on the highest dose achieved over 20% weight reduction in SURMOUNT-1.
supporting_quote: about half the patients in a 10mg group and about 57% of patients in the 50mg group had over 20% weight reduction

episode_title: #349 Hotcakes: Tirzepatide for weight loss, Personalized Antidepressants, Seasonal Edema, Nirmatrelvir for low-risk COVID-19
statement: Tirzepatide is titrated slowly, starting at 2.5mg weekly and increasing by 2.5mg every four weeks, so it takes time to reach higher doses.
supporting_quote:
it seems like it comes, you start them out at 2.5mg weekly, and then you go up by
2.5mg every four weeks. So, it takes quite a while to get to the fifth 10, or the 15mg dose

episode_title: #349 Hotcakes: Tirzepatide for weight loss, Personalized Antidepressants, Seasonal Edema, Nirmatrelvir for low-risk COVID-19
statement: Clinical trials with a run-in period tend to underestimate adverse events, because patients who don't tolerate the treatment during the run-in drop out, making post-marketing surveillance important.
supporting_quote:
clinical trials will
generally tend to underestimate adverse events from treatments, especially if run-in period where the treatment is given to all
patients was used, because patients who don't tolerate a treatment during the run-in period tend to drop out of the study.

episode_title: #349 Hotcakes: Tirzepatide for weight loss, Personalized Antidepressants, Seasonal Edema, Nirmatrelvir for low-risk COVID-19
statement: In the VA PRIME pharmacogenomic antidepressant trial, remission of depression symptoms was slightly higher in the pharmacogenomic testing group at 12 weeks, but the difference did not persist at 24 weeks.
supporting_quote:
at 12 weeks that there was a slightly higher proportion of patients
in the pharmacogenomic testing group than in the standard of care group that had remission of symptoms, but that actually that
difference did not persist at 24 weeks at the end of the trial.

episode_title: #349 Hotcakes: Tirzepatide for weight loss, Personalized Antidepressants, Seasonal Edema, Nirmatrelvir for low-risk COVID-19
statement: Pharmacogenomic testing did reduce the proportion of patients prescribed medications with predicted drug-gene interactions, but this is essentially a surrogate outcome, and the patient-centered outcome of remission at 24 weeks showed no difference.
supporting_quote:
patients
were more likely to be free of drug-gene interactions. I almost consider that a surrogate outcome in that we don't really know if
patients who got drugs with a high likelihood of drug-gene interactions ended up having more side effects or worse control of their
depression. And the only patient centered outcome in the trial of remission at 24 weeks really did not show any difference.

episode_title: #349 Hotcakes: Tirzepatide for weight loss, Personalized Antidepressants, Seasonal Edema, Nirmatrelvir for low-risk COVID-19
statement: Google Trends searches for ankle/foot swelling peak in summer (June) and trough in winter (December), the opposite of heart failure readmissions which are higher in winter—suggesting seasonal dependent edema is often benign.
supporting_quote:
people are looking, they're google searching ankle edema, that peaks in June, and it's probably at its lowest in
December. And the other thing that they said in the article that I just I love that they even thought of this. Heart failure readmissions,
by the way, the exact opposite trend. You see a much higher rate of heart failure readmissions in the wintertime

episode_title: #349 Hotcakes: Tirzepatide for weight loss, Personalized Antidepressants, Seasonal Edema, Nirmatrelvir for low-risk COVID-19
statement: The EPIC-HR trial established that nirmatrelvir/ritonavir (Paxlovid) is highly effective at reducing hospitalization and death among high-risk unvaccinated outpatients with COVID-19, and NIH recommends it first line in this group.
supporting_quote:
We know with a high degree of confidence from the EPIC-HR study, which was published in in April issue of The New England
Journal of Medicine that Nirmatrelvir/Ritonavir, or Paxlovid is highly effective at reducing hospitalization and death. Among high-risk
unvaccinated outpatients with COVID. And NIH in response to that currently recommends this as the first line treatment in this group.

episode_title: #349 Hotcakes: Tirzepatide for weight loss, Personalized Antidepressants, Seasonal Edema, Nirmatrelvir for low-risk COVID-19
statement: The EPIC-SR study in low-risk patients was a negative trial: nirmatrelvir/ritonavir did not hasten time to sustained resolution of symptoms, so most young healthy vaccinated/boosted patients are unlikely to benefit.
supporting_quote:
we know that Nirmatrelvir/Ritonavir in low-risk
patients did not hasten the time to resolution of symptoms.

episode_title: #349 Hotcakes: Tirzepatide for weight loss, Personalized Antidepressants, Seasonal Edema, Nirmatrelvir for low-risk COVID-19
statement: In EPIC-HR, seropositive patients had a markedly attenuated benefit from nirmatrelvir/ritonavir compared with those who had not yet mounted an antibody response, likely reflecting lower viral loads once an immune response is present.
supporting_quote:
the subset of patients who
were seropositive, they had a markedly attenuated benefit from Nirmatrelvir/Ritonavir. In comparison with patients who had not yet
mounted an antibody response.

episode_title: #349 Hotcakes: Tirzepatide for weight loss, Personalized Antidepressants, Seasonal Edema, Nirmatrelvir for low-risk COVID-19
statement: Nirmatrelvir/ritonavir is not benign—dysgeusia (altered taste) is a fairly common adverse effect, and there are many drug interactions requiring dose adjustment or holding medications for the treatment course.
supporting_quote: this drug is not totally benign dysgeusia, or an altered sense of taste seems to be happening fairly commonly.

episode_title: #349 Hotcakes: Tirzepatide for weight loss, Personalized Antidepressants, Seasonal Edema, Nirmatrelvir for low-risk COVID-19
statement: Choosing Wisely recommendations from hospital medicine include not raising oxygen saturation above 96%, letting stable patients sleep instead of waking them for routine vitals, avoiding daily CBC and chemistry in stable patients, and avoiding urinary catheters for convenience or incontinence.
supporting_quote:
Don't artificially raise the oxygen saturation to above 96% on Peripheral capillary oxygen sets. We talked about that before, it's good
to say it again. In the hospital, let the patient sleep, especially if the patient is doing well. Just let them sleep. Don't wake them up for
routine vitals. Further, don't just automatically order a CBC and a chemistry every day, especially if the patient's stable

episode_title: #348 Myositis and Myopathy, LIVE!
statement: Inflammatory myopathies typically present as a subacute (over weeks to months), painless proximal weakness; pain in the absence of weakness is highly atypical.
supporting_quote: if you're thinking about the inflammatory myopathy is myositis, it's generally a subacute process. Six months would not be unusual.

episode_title: #348 Myositis and Myopathy, LIVE!
statement: Having pain in the absence of weakness is highly atypical for an inflammatory myopathy; patients are generally weak without pain or weak with a little pain.
supporting_quote: having pain in the absence of weakness, highly, highly atypical of an inflammatory myopathy, so you are generally weak without pain, maybe weak with a little pain, not pain on its own

episode_title: #348 Myositis and Myopathy, LIVE!
statement: Distinguish true weakness from fatigue/asthenia by asking whether the patient must use chair arms to physically push off or needs help getting up, versus just feeling generally tired.
supporting_quote:
Have you had to use chair arms to
physically push off the chair? That's definitely different than just overall fatigue.

episode_title: #348 Myositis and Myopathy, LIVE!
statement: Myopathy patients struggle with lower chairs (low couches, toilet seats) rather than higher chairs; ask specifically about deep-seated chairs.
supporting_quote:
most people can get off a higher
seated chair, where they get into trouble with myopathy is lower chairs, so a low couch, toilet seats

episode_title: #348 Myositis and Myopathy, LIVE!
statement: High-yield rashes to ask about in inflammatory myopathy include the eyelids, the MCP/PIP joints, and the scalp; scalp involvement is often missed because it is under the hair.
supporting_quote:
high yield
rashes would be the eyelids or the MCP and PIP joints

episode_title: #348 Myositis and Myopathy, LIVE!
statement: Neck flexors and hip flexors should be tested supine, and hip extensors prone, because a seated patient can recruit muscles and cheat, masking true weakness.
supporting_quote:
The two muscle groups that I think we forget to examine

supine are the neck flexors and the hip flexors. The reason that is is that if you are seated, you can
recruit and cheat a bit.

episode_title: #348 Myositis and Myopathy, LIVE!
statement: When testing a muscle group, position your hand closest to the far joint (near the knee when testing the hip) to gain leverage and bring out true weakness.
supporting_quote:
you want to
be closest to the next, that the furthest joint, because you want to give yourself and you want the unfair
advantage.

episode_title: #348 Myositis and Myopathy, LIVE!
statement: To detect subtle forearm flexor weakness (as in inclusion body myositis), have the patient curl fingers to the first palmar crease and try to pry the fingers up, rather than testing a true grip, which allows recruitment of intrinsics.
supporting_quote:
So, forearm flexors are the most common distal group of
muscles that's affected especially with IBM, inclusion body myositis. So, I teach trainees now to reach
fingers for the first palmar crease.

episode_title: #348 Myositis and Myopathy, LIVE!
statement: In myositis, neck flexor weakness is often out of proportion to neck extensor weakness; test neck flexors supine by having the patient flex their neck off the table against resistance.
supporting_quote:
that's supine, you ask the patient to flex their neck up off the bed off the
examiner's table.

episode_title: #348 Myositis and Myopathy, LIVE!
statement: Dermatomyositis rashes include the heliotrope (eyelids), Gottron's sign/papules over extensor surfaces (MCPs, PIPs, elbows, knees, ankles), V-neck sign, shawl sign, and Holster sign on the lateral hips.
supporting_quote:
we look for V-neck sign, so on the chest, the back which
across the shoulders is a shawl sign. Some people also have arm erythema. We look for what's called
a Holster sign where obviously hold a gun holster, and that's on the lateral hips.

episode_title: #348 Myositis and Myopathy, LIVE!
statement: Dermatomyositis rashes are typically pruritic and often photosensitive, whereas lupus rashes tend not to itch; scalp itch can be the most debilitating symptom for patients.
supporting_quote:
the thing that gets
most confused with these rashes is lupus, and lupus tends to not itch. And dermatomyositis is an
incredibly pruritic rash.

episode_title: #348 Myositis and Myopathy, LIVE!
statement: The nasolabial folds are involved (not spared) in dermatomyositis but are almost always spared in lupus, which helps distinguish the two.
supporting_quote:
the nasolabial folds are included and not spared in
dermatomyositis, and they are almost always spared in lupus.

episode_title: #348 Myositis and Myopathy, LIVE!
statement: Atorvastatin can cause direct myotoxicity (myalgia to rhabdomyolysis) but is also associated with an autoimmune statin myopathy that typically develops after about 30 to 36 months on the drug.
supporting_quote:
the patient
generally on a statin on average for about 30 to 36 months, it's almost three years in.

episode_title: #348 Myositis and Myopathy, LIVE!
