statement: Patients on atorvastatin are typically monitored for liver function but not CK; a rising transaminase in a statin patient may reflect muscle rather than liver, so check a CK before pursuing liver biopsy.
supporting_quote:
if I can just publicly announce please don't do two liver biopsies
before you check a CK

episode_title: #348 Myositis and Myopathy, LIVE!
statement: Unlike most rheumatic diseases, inflammatory myopathies usually do not cause an anemia of chronic disease; when anemia is present, consider blood loss, iron deficiency, or occult cancer.
supporting_quote:
When I see an anemia, I'm
wondering about blood loss and iron deficiency, and then I'm going down the cancer route

episode_title: #348 Myositis and Myopathy, LIVE!
statement: A negative ANA does not exclude myositis; most autoimmune myopathies have cytoplasmic myositis antibodies that yield a normal ANA, though dermatomyositis is usually ANA positive except in at least one subset.
supporting_quote:
most of the other autoimmune myopathies, the myositis antibodies are actually cytoplasmic. So, we'll see a normal
ANA.

episode_title: #348 Myositis and Myopathy, LIVE!
statement: ESR and CRP are often normal in isolated muscle and skin disease; a normal sed rate does not exclude myositis, though these markers rise with lung or severe joint involvement.
supporting_quote:
Isolated muscle disease and skin tends not to
raise your sed rate or CRP. So, once again, you don't have to think you've missed the diagnosis
because the sed rate is four.

episode_title: #348 Myositis and Myopathy, LIVE!
statement: The CK can be completely normal in dermatomyositis, which is when EMG/nerve conduction studies become especially valuable to confirm a muscle component.
supporting_quote:
in
dermatomyositis the CK can be rock solid, normal, very confusing. Those cases, EMG/nerve
conduction is invaluable.

episode_title: #348 Myositis and Myopathy, LIVE!
statement: The preferred noninvasive imaging for muscle in myositis is a non-contrast MRI of the thighs (T1 and STIR/T2), looking at architecture and for muscle edema; thighs are chosen because involvement is symmetric.
supporting_quote:
the best noninvasive way to see muscle is with an MRI. It's a
non-contrasted MRI of the thighs.

episode_title: #348 Myositis and Myopathy, LIVE!
statement: Cancer risk in dermatomyositis is front-loaded, greatest in the year before and after diagnosis; screen with CT chest/abdomen/pelvis (for ILD and solid tumors) plus age-appropriate cancer screening, but do not repeat scanning annually.
supporting_quote:
The first year,
one year prior and one year after, if you look very carefully at the time of diagnosis, that time period is
the most critical for cancer.

episode_title: #348 Myositis and Myopathy, LIVE!
statement: Ovarian cancer is overrepresented in dermatomyositis relative to the general population, so transvaginal ultrasound and careful screening for it are warranted in women.
supporting_quote:
ovarian cancer is overrepresented in dermatomyositis for ways we really don't
understand and this is not a cancer you see in the general population as often. So, we, for sure in
women often do a transvaginal ultrasound

episode_title: #348 Myositis and Myopathy, LIVE!
statement: A positive anti-HMGCR antibody indicates autoimmune statin myopathy and makes the statin absolutely contraindicated; consider PCSK9 inhibitors and other alternatives instead.
supporting_quote:
If that HMGCR antibodies
positive, no how, no way, we go talking about PCSK inhibitors and all other ways that are safer. If we do
have that as an antibody, the statin is absolutely contraindicated

episode_title: #348 Myositis and Myopathy, LIVE!
statement: Exercise is helpful and not contraindicated in myositis; favor cardio and isometric exercises with more reps and less weight, and avoid lifting heavy weights.
supporting_quote:
Lifting weights, however, so heavier weights, I don't
recommend. I recommend more reps, less weight.

episode_title: #348 Myositis and Myopathy, LIVE!
statement: When restarting a statin in a myositis patient without anti-HMGCR antibody, start low and slow (e.g., Monday/Wednesday/Friday dosing) with monthly CK monitoring, then gradually increase.
supporting_quote:
So start slow and low. Always start every other day. So, I do a Monday, Wednesday, Friday for at least
a month, checking a CK carefully monthly, and then gradually increase that.

episode_title: #348 Myositis and Myopathy, LIVE!
statement: Patients with dermatomyositis should be counseled on photoprotection with high-SPF sunscreen (over 50) and wide-brim hats due to the photosensitivity of the rash.
supporting_quote:
the sun because of the photosensitivity of it to
remind patients to use. We're talking about high level 7,500 SPF. Once that really is anything over 50,
it's really helpful

episode_title: #348 Myositis and Myopathy, LIVE!
statement: Dysphagia in dermatomyositis is usually upper pharyngeal (speech and swallow) weakness rather than lower GI disease, and can be evaluated with an esophagram and speech/swallow referral.
supporting_quote:
it's almost always upper pharyngeal weakness rather than lower
GI

episode_title: #348 Myositis and Myopathy, LIVE!
statement: Early muscle atrophy (e.g., at six months) is unusual for inflammatory myopathy and should prompt consideration of an adult-onset muscular dystrophy or another neuromuscular process.
supporting_quote:
So early atrophy is pretty unusual,
inclusion body myositis, we can see atrophy but at six months

episode_title: #348 Myositis and Myopathy, LIVE!
statement: In inclusion body myositis the quadriceps (knee extensors) are almost exclusively affected while knee flexors remain strong, and forearm finger flexors are involved, distinguishing it from other myopathies.
supporting_quote:
the knee extensors are
almost exclusively affected all the time. Yet knee flexors remain strong until nearly the day people die of
something else with IBM.

episode_title: #348 Myositis and Myopathy, LIVE!
statement: Patients on prednisone 2.5 mg or more for three months should be screened with a baseline DEXA, and those at moderate or higher risk may warrant a bisphosphonate for bone protection.
supporting_quote:
Anyone on 2.5 milligrams of prednisone or more for three months should really be screened
with a baseline DEXA and if they're moderate risk or more, you might put them on bisphosphonate to
protect their bones.

episode_title: #345 Vitamin and Mineral supplementation: A USPSTF Update with Dr. Michael Barry
statement: The USPSTF concluded there is insufficient evidence to recommend for or against multivitamin supplementation to prevent cardiovascular disease and cancer (an 'I statement').
supporting_quote: The evidence for multivitamins is copious. We found about nine trials with over 50,000 people, including a relatively new trial, the COSMOS Trial, which added another 20,000 people to the N. And that evidence looking at it all together, was not enough to recommend for or against multivitamin supplements. We call that an ‘I statement.’

episode_title: #345 Vitamin and Mineral supplementation: A USPSTF Update with Dr. Michael Barry
statement: An 'I statement' means there is not enough evidence to recommend for or against the intervention—it is neither a recommendation to do it nor to avoid it.
supporting_quote: an ‘I statement’ means we don't have enough evidence to recommend for or against the intervention, in this case, a multivitamin, we just can't say. We're not saying not to do it. We're not saying to do it.

episode_title: #345 Vitamin and Mineral supplementation: A USPSTF Update with Dr. Michael Barry
statement: The USPSTF recommends against vitamin E supplementation for prevention of cardiovascular disease and cancer due to lack of benefit.
supporting_quote: One is Vitamin E supplementation. The evidence there was enough to say there's just not enough signal of benefit here, we recommend that people don't take it.

episode_title: #345 Vitamin and Mineral supplementation: A USPSTF Update with Dr. Michael Barry
statement: The USPSTF recommends against beta-carotene supplementation because trials suggested a higher risk of lung cancer in higher-risk people (e.g., smokers, asbestos exposure).
supporting_quote: The other is a supplement called beta-carotene that had been studied in terms of trying to reduce lung cancer risk. And paradoxically, the many trials that were done, actually suggested in higher risk people, the risk of lung cancer might be higher with beta-carotene rather than lower. We recommend against beta-carotene supplements.

episode_title: #345 Vitamin and Mineral supplementation: A USPSTF Update with Dr. Michael Barry
statement: These recommendations do not apply to people who are or may become pregnant, who should take a folic acid supplement (alone or in a multivitamin) to prevent neural tube defects.
supporting_quote: for people who might become pregnant or who are pregnant, these recommendations wouldn't apply because we have a separate, A recommendation that those people should take a folic acid supplement either alone or in combination in a multiple vitamin to prevent the risk of neural tube defects

episode_title: #345 Vitamin and Mineral supplementation: A USPSTF Update with Dr. Michael Barry
statement: These recommendations apply to asymptomatic primary prevention and not to people with chronic illness, known vitamin deficiencies, or malabsorption conditions such as celiac disease.
supporting_quote: people who have chronic illnesses have known vitamin deficiencies, have an illness that could affect a vitamin absorption. I might think of celiac disease or something like that. Those would be conditions that we're not talking about. This is for asymptomatic people or at least asymptomatic in terms of risk of having a vitamin deficiency.

episode_title: #345 Vitamin and Mineral supplementation: A USPSTF Update with Dr. Michael Barry
statement: High-dose vitamins can cause harm: high-dose vitamin D can be related to kidney stones, and large doses of vitamin A can cause headaches and liver problems.
supporting_quote: large doses of vitamins can cause specific problems. For example, vitamin D, taking at a higher dose over long time can be related to kidney stones, large doses of vitamin A, can cause problems with headaches and liver problems.

episode_title: #345 Vitamin and Mineral supplementation: A USPSTF Update with Dr. Michael Barry
statement: The 2022 recommendations align with the 2014 recommendations despite 52 new studies (84 total, over 700,000 people), reflecting variability rather than lack of evidence.
supporting_quote: it lined up pretty much exactly with our 2014 recommendations, which might come as a surprise because actually, there were 52 new studies. You'd say, "Gosh, you looked at 84 studies all together in almost over 700,000 folks, how come you're still uncertain?"

episode_title: #345 Vitamin and Mineral supplementation: A USPSTF Update with Dr. Michael Barry
statement: Clinicians should ensure vitamin use does not distract from higher-value interventions; statins reduce cardiovascular outcomes by roughly 30%, far exceeding any possible small benefit from vitamins.
supporting_quote: The benefit of statins in general on cardiovascular disease outcomes are roughly 30%. For vitamins we're debating, gee, might there still be a few percent decrease if we did more studies. We do have more effective interventions that we don't want to forget.

episode_title: #343 Hypertension FAQ and Cardiorenal: A rapid review (TFTC)
statement: Confirm office hypertension with out-of-office readings; Dr. Cohen recommends averaging two readings in the morning and two in the evening over three days (at least six values) before making decisions.
supporting_quote:
Dr. Cohen said she likes to
take at least a couple readings over three days, usually the average of two readings in the morning and
two readings in the evening over three days, at least six values to determine what you're going to do.

episode_title: #343 Hypertension FAQ and Cardiorenal: A rapid review (TFTC)
statement: Masked hypertension (normotensive in office but hypertensive at home) is common and not benign; it warrants aggressive risk factor modification. Classic patients are men who smoke tobacco, with alcohol use, impaired glucose tolerance, and central obesity as risk factors.
supporting_quote:
this is the case, where you have someone who is normotensive in the office, but then actually hypertensive at
home, which is the opposite of what we-- I think we have been trained to recognize and this has--
[crosstalk]

episode_title: #343 Hypertension FAQ and Cardiorenal: A rapid review (TFTC)
statement: ARBs match ACE inhibitors for nephroprotection and systolic heart failure but have fewer adverse effects (cough, angioedema, even GI bleed), so ARBs can be favored when initiating therapy.
supporting_quote:
in this meta-analysis, the ARBs were less likely to have
adverse effects than the ACEs. Things like the ACE inhibitor associated cough, or angioedema, or even
weirdly GI bleed

episode_title: #343 Hypertension FAQ and Cardiorenal: A rapid review (TFTC)
statement: Losartan does not last a full 24 hours and is ideally dosed twice daily; for suboptimal control, use 25 mg twice daily rather than increasing to 50 mg once daily.
supporting_quote:
The medication losartan is one of the ones that's often cheap. It's on the $4
formulary. I'm using a lot of losartan but it doesn't last a full 24 hours. Ideally, you dose it twice a day

episode_title: #343 Hypertension FAQ and Cardiorenal: A rapid review (TFTC)
statement: Olmesartan is a long-acting ARB preferred by Dr. Cohen and available in combination with amlodipine; be aware it can cause olmesartan-induced enteropathy mimicking celiac disease.
supporting_quote:
olmesartan, she likes it because it's long acting and it does come
in combination with amlodipine, which is a calcium channel blocker.

episode_title: #343 Hypertension FAQ and Cardiorenal: A rapid review (TFTC)
statement: Starting two antihypertensive agents at low dose (quarter or half maximum) gives good control with fewer side effects; most patients need two medications, and maxing out one agent yields diminishing returns and more side effects.
supporting_quote:
if someone's really far off target, if they're 20 points above
goal, I'm going to start them on two medicines in combination right from the get go rather than start just
one agent and take six months to get them to go. I'll try to get them to go right away with a low-dose
combination.

episode_title: #343 Hypertension FAQ and Cardiorenal: A rapid review (TFTC)
statement: Hydrochlorothiazide 12.5 mg is nearly a homeopathic dose for many patients; 25 mg is a more appropriate starting dose for the majority.
supporting_quote:
she said that's practically a homeopathic dose for a lot of
patients, probably, 25 milligrams is a starting dose of hydrochlorothiazide for the majority of patients.

episode_title: #343 Hypertension FAQ and Cardiorenal: A rapid review (TFTC)
statement: Chlorthalidone is roughly twice as potent as hydrochlorothiazide (25 mg are not equivalent) and carries a risk of hypokalemia requiring close electrolyte monitoring.
supporting_quote:
it's more potent, so typically I almost think of chlorthalidone is
being twice as potent.

episode_title: #343 Hypertension FAQ and Cardiorenal: A rapid review (TFTC)
statement: Contrary to older teaching, the CLICK trial (Agarwal et al., 2021) showed chlorthalidone effectively lowered blood pressure (~10 points) in patients with CKD, even those already on a loop diuretic.
supporting_quote:
the CLICK trial came out in late 2021, I believe. It
was by Agarwal, et al. We talked about this with Dr. Cohen and this was also covered, I believe in the
Digest, our newsletter. Chlorthalidone, actually was effective in lowering blood pressure. I think it

lowered blood pressure by about 10 points in these patients with CKD.

episode_title: #343 Hypertension FAQ and Cardiorenal: A rapid review (TFTC)
statement: Torsemide 20 mg is a preferred loop diuretic because it is longer acting and can be dosed once daily, unlike bumetanide or furosemide which are dosed twice daily.
supporting_quote:
she
mentioned torsemide 20 milligrams is probably her go to because it's a longer acting loop and it could
be dosed once a day as opposed to bumetanide or furosemide, which have to be dosed twice a day.

episode_title: #343 Hypertension FAQ and Cardiorenal: A rapid review (TFTC)
statement: ACE inhibitors and ARBs are appropriate in CKD; expect a creatinine rise of up to 30%, which is acceptable, but a rise of ~50% should prompt consideration of bilateral renal artery stenosis.
supporting_quote: you would expect a creatinine bump of up to 30% and that's okay if it happens.

episode_title: #343 Hypertension FAQ and Cardiorenal: A rapid review (TFTC)
statement: In CKD patients on ACE/ARB, a potassium up to 5.5 is tolerated; Dr. Cohen does not panic until potassium exceeds 5.5.
supporting_quote:
She allows a
K of up to 5.5. She's fairly comfortable with letting patients ride it. She doesn't panic until over five and a
half

episode_title: #343 Hypertension FAQ and Cardiorenal: A rapid review (TFTC)
statement: Cardiorenal syndrome is driven more by venous congestion (venous back pressure on the kidney) than poor forward flow, since cardiac index is often normal; this supports diuretic therapy as treatment.
supporting_quote:
it turns out that venous congestion, this venous back pressure on the kidney
is actually, that seems to be more important because the cardiac index is often normal in these patients
or not poor enough to explain the rise in creatinine in cardiorenal syndrome.

episode_title: #343 Hypertension FAQ and Cardiorenal: A rapid review (TFTC)
statement: In acute decompensated heart failure, permissive hypercreatinemia is expected during diuresis and should not automatically stop therapy; the creatinine bump in the DOSE trial was associated with better outcomes.
supporting_quote:
the
dose trial, they actually showed improved outcomes. When
they did a postdoc analysis, they saw that the patients who actually had a creatinine bump seemed
they had better outcomes and the speculation was probably, because we did a better job diuresing
these patients

episode_title: #343 Hypertension FAQ and Cardiorenal: A rapid review (TFTC)
statement: If a heart failure patient is not hypotensive, do not necessarily stop the ACE, ARB, or SGLT2 inhibitor for a slight creatinine bump; if stopped, resume them on discharge as these are important long-term.
supporting_quote:
if the patient's not hypotensive, you don't
necessarily need to stop the ACE, the ARB, the SGLT2 inhibitor, if someone comes in with a slight
creatinine bump

episode_title: #343 Hypertension FAQ and Cardiorenal: A rapid review (TFTC)
statement: Assess diuretic response promptly: IV loop diuretics should produce urine within ~30 minutes and oral within 1-2 hours if the threshold dose is met; if not making urine, increase the dose rather than just the frequency.
supporting_quote:
For IV versions, it should be within
minutes, certainly, within 30 minutes that they start to put out urine if you hit the right dose. If you're
giving it orally, it should be within hour, two hours depending on the agent.

episode_title: #343 Hypertension FAQ and Cardiorenal: A rapid review (TFTC)
statement: If a patient does not respond to 40 mg furosemide, giving 40 mg twice daily will not work; you must reach the threshold dose to produce urine, then increase frequency.
supporting_quote:
If you have someone who's not making urine with 40 milligrams of
furosemide, doing 40 milligrams furosemide twice daily is still not going to achieve what you're trying to
do. You have to get them peeing and then you can increase the frequency.

episode_title: #343 Hypertension FAQ and Cardiorenal: A rapid review (TFTC)
statement: The benefit of a furosemide drip is achieving higher diuretic doses that are hard to reach with intermittent dosing, not anything magical about continuous delivery.
supporting_quote:
there's
nothing magical in the mode of delivery other than the fact that can get the patients to a much higher
dose of diuretic

episode_title: #343 Hypertension FAQ and Cardiorenal: A rapid review (TFTC)
statement: For persistent diuretic resistance, use sequential nephron blockade by adding a thiazide, acetazolamide, or an MRA like spironolactone to block a different part of the nephron.
supporting_quote:
then we're in sequential nephron blockade territory, Paul, where you're adding on
things like a thiazide diuretic to block a different part of the nephron. Other things you can think about

are acetazolamide, the mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist like spironolactone

episode_title: #343 Hypertension FAQ and Cardiorenal: A rapid review (TFTC)
statement: Bumetanide has high, consistent oral bioavailability whereas furosemide oral bioavailability ranges widely (~10-90%); bumetanide may be a better oral choice in patients with gut edema, and its levels are less GFR-dependent.
supporting_quote:
furosemide in terms of oral bioavailability
is wild. It's anywhere between 10 and 90 something percent. It is not very consistent as opposed to
bumetanide, which has fairly high bioavailability that is consistent.

episode_title: #343 Hypertension FAQ and Cardiorenal: A rapid review (TFTC)
statement: An elevated troponin in CKD should not be dismissed; it is a poor prognostic marker even if not immediately actionable, useful for risk stratification.
supporting_quote:
But it's also not meaningless. It's a poor prognostic indicator. We know that patients with chronic kidney
disease, cardiovascular diseases is the major cause of death in these patients. I think it's helpful in
terms of least risk stratifying.

episode_title: #343 Hypertension FAQ and Cardiorenal: A rapid review (TFTC)
statement: For BNP in CKD patients, where baseline levels may be chronically elevated, look at the relative change compared to prior values rather than the absolute number.
supporting_quote:
Dr. Khan mentioned that she actually likes to look at
the relative change compared to prior values that she has and she finds that helpful.

episode_title: #337 Palpitations, Erectile Dysfunction: A rapid review (TFTC)
statement: When taking a palpitations history, ask the patient to tap out the strength and rhythm of what they feel on the back of their hand to better characterize the arrhythmia.
supporting_quote:
he likes to ask the patient to tap out on the back of their hand, the strength of what
they're feeling, the rhythm of what they're feeling

episode_title: #337 Palpitations, Erectile Dysfunction: A rapid review (TFTC)
statement: Tachyarrhythmias like SVT tend to have abrupt onset, whereas palpitations from an anxiety disorder tend to have a slow build.
supporting_quote:
tachyarrhythmias like an SVT, they tend to be very abrupt onset
versus something like an anxiety disorder, causing the palpitations that tends to have a slow build

episode_title: #337 Palpitations, Erectile Dysfunction: A rapid review (TFTC)
statement: Red flag palpitation symptoms include chest pain and lightheadedness, but syncope is most worrisome as it may represent a lethal arrhythmia and warrants urgent workup.
supporting_quote:
chest pain is not normal and we should be little bit more excited about. Even
feeling lightheaded is a little bit unusual and then syncope is the one that you should really worry,
because it might represent a lethal or malignant arrhythmias.

episode_title: #337 Palpitations, Erectile Dysfunction: A rapid review (TFTC)
statement: Women are probably underdiagnosed with SVTs and tachyarrhythmias, often incorrectly attributed to anxiety; endorsing anxiety does not complete the workup.
supporting_quote:

statement: When a patient goes truly from zero to 100 (a transient event with no residual scar on exam), the two possibilities are vasovagal syncope or a malignant arrhythmia.
supporting_quote:
When you truly establish
that somebody went from zero to 100 as
we seem to have done here, there's only
two possibilities. You either have
vasovagal syncope or you have a
malignant arrhythmia.

episode_title: #529 A Case of Syncope with the Clinical Problem Solvers
statement: In evaluating syncope, clarify the time course of the illness—a decline in exercise tolerance may reveal a smoldering process, since going truly from zero to 100 is rare.
supporting_quote:
It is exceptionally rare to go from zero
to 100 as she appears to be, super,
super rare. And so, odds are we're
missing a big chunk of the history, and
odds are that she has had a decline in
exercise tolerance to the point now
where her EF is 45%.

episode_title: #528 Hospital Addiction Medicine 3.0 with Dr. Maggie Lowenstein
statement: When verifying a patient's methadone dose from an OTP, the two most important pieces of information are the milligram dose and when they last received a dose.
supporting_quote:
but I think the two
most important things are what
milligrams and when did they last
receive it.

episode_title: #528 Hospital Addiction Medicine 3.0 with Dr. Maggie Lowenstein

hey, if this
person was just walking in today to

our
our PMP has no records of methadone on

episode_title: #528 Hospital Addiction Medicine 3.0 with Dr. Maggie Lowenstein

recommending dose
reductions pretty quickly when people

basically restart them

episode_title: #528 Hospital Addiction Medicine 3.0 with Dr. Maggie Lowenstein
statement: In patients who missed methadone doses but continue non-prescribed opioid use and lack risk factors (older age, concurrent benzodiazepine use), it is reasonable to be more aggressive in maintaining or only slightly reducing the methadone dose.

In patients who
missed doses but continued to use and

episode_title: #528 Hospital Addiction Medicine 3.0 with Dr. Maggie Lowenstein

you can always give more, you

episode_title: #528 Hospital Addiction Medicine 3.0 with Dr. Maggie Lowenstein

the first step
is really just like repeating the EKG.
Um, you know, maybe fixing fixable
things like giving them some K and some

episode_title: #528 Hospital Addiction Medicine 3.0 with Dr. Maggie Lowenstein

if it's in the low 500s and they're in

supporting_quote:
friendly reminder, you can use the

episode_title: #528 Hospital Addiction Medicine 3.0 with Dr. Maggie Lowenstein

we're starting clonedine doses often kind of

episode_title: #528 Hospital Addiction Medicine 3.0 with Dr. Maggie Lowenstein

the one that I had

episode_title: #528 Hospital Addiction Medicine 3.0 with Dr. Maggie Lowenstein

we're using doses of one starting doses

the

episode_title: #528 Hospital Addiction Medicine 3.0 with Dr. Maggie Lowenstein

we often

episode_title: #526 DIGEST – game changers in pancreatic cancer, lipid guideline updates, and GLP-1/GIP endoscopy holds
statement: The 2026 ACC/AHA lipid guideline pushes most patients with established atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease to an LDL goal of less than 55 mg/dL and a non-HDL cholesterol goal of less than 85 mg/dL.
supporting_quote: these new guidelines are really pushing for most people are going to have enough risk factors of one kind or another to push their LDL down to less than 55.

episode_title: #526 DIGEST – game changers in pancreatic cancer, lipid guideline updates, and GLP-1/GIP endoscopy holds
statement: Non-HDL cholesterol equals total cholesterol minus HDL, and the non-HDL goal is set approximately 30 points higher than the LDL goal.
supporting_quote: the non-HDL cholesterol, which is your total cholesterol minus HDL. So, basically, whatever your LDL goal is, your non-HDL goal is like 30 points higher.

episode_title: #526 DIGEST – game changers in pancreatic cancer, lipid guideline updates, and GLP-1/GIP endoscopy holds
statement: ASCVD is defined in the guidelines as acute coronary syndrome, MI, angina, arterial revascularization, stroke, TIA, or peripheral arterial disease.
supporting_quote: atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease in the guidelines is defined as like acute coronary syndrome, MI, angina, arterial revascularization, stroke, TIA, or peripheral arterial disease.

episode_title: #526 DIGEST – game changers in pancreatic cancer, lipid guideline updates, and GLP-1/GIP endoscopy holds
statement: New LDL targets of less than 55 and non-HDL less than 85 are difficult to reach even on high-dose statin, typically requiring statin plus ezetimibe or statin plus PCSK9 inhibitor, with bempedoic acid or inclisiran as additional options.
supporting_quote: these new targets of like less than 55 for LDL and less than 85 for non-HDL are hard to hit even on a max like high-dose statin. So, you're it's really you're talking about like statin plus ezetimibe or statin plus PCSK9 inhibitor. And then the guidelines mention like adding bempedoic acid or inclisiran

episode_title: #526 DIGEST – game changers in pancreatic cancer, lipid guideline updates, and GLP-1/GIP endoscopy holds
statement: A coronary artery calcium score can reclassify a patient's risk; a score over 100 falls in the moderately high category and should prompt more aggressive lipid management.
supporting_quote: it was over their calcium score was over 100, which is in the moderately high category, and which would that should prompt you to be like being more aggressive about lipid management

episode_title: #526 DIGEST – game changers in pancreatic cancer, lipid guideline updates, and GLP-1/GIP endoscopy holds

supporting_quote: the cost is like between, I would say, $75 and $300 out of pocket. So, a lot of people can just afford that and they they will just get them.

episode_title: #526 DIGEST – game changers in pancreatic cancer, lipid guideline updates, and GLP-1/GIP endoscopy holds
statement: Ordering LP(a) with 'elevated LP little A' as the indication is usually covered by most insurances, and apoB can often be ordered alongside a routine lipid panel.
supporting_quote: if you just put elevated LP little A as like the reason for ordering it, it's usually covered by most insurances. And for apoB, I've been I've just been ordering that along with a lipid panel

episode_title: #526 DIGEST – game changers in pancreatic cancer, lipid guideline updates, and GLP-1/GIP endoscopy holds
statement: Daraxonrasib is a KRAS inhibitor that covers multiple KRAS mutations and also inhibits wild-type KRAS, and is taken as a once-daily oral pill.
supporting_quote: This drug is a KRAS inhibitor. Um it's it covers multiple different mutations in KRAS

episode_title: #526 DIGEST – game changers in pancreatic cancer, lipid guideline updates, and GLP-1/GIP endoscopy holds

episode_title: #526 DIGEST – game changers in pancreatic cancer, lipid guideline updates, and GLP-1/GIP endoscopy holds
statement: Skin toxicities are the most notable side effect of KRAS inhibitors like daraxonrasib; they are on-target effects and may correlate with response to therapy.
supporting_quote: the skin toxicities are probably the most notable um they are on-target toxic effects. So, this is like a known side effect from KRAS inhibition um and they may correlate with response to therapy.

episode_title: #526 DIGEST – game changers in pancreatic cancer, lipid guideline updates, and GLP-1/GIP endoscopy holds
statement: Standard-of-care chemotherapy for metastatic pancreatic cancer includes taxanes and FOLFIRINOX-type regimens.
supporting_quote: which is what we use right now for standard of care pancreatic cancer that's with taxanes and with FOLFIRINOX, those sorts of drugs.

episode_title: #526 DIGEST – game changers in pancreatic cancer, lipid guideline updates, and GLP-1/GIP endoscopy holds
statement: At high semaglutide doses (25 mg and up oral, or 2.4 mg and up injectable), dysesthesia occurs, and at the 7.2 mg dose almost one in five patients reported it.
supporting_quote: at high the highest doses, so the 25 mg and up of the oral semaglutide or the 2.4 and up of the uh injectable, you do see dysesthesia. And at this 7.2 mg dose, almost one in five patients reported dysesthesia.

episode_title: #526 DIGEST – game changers in pancreatic cancer, lipid guideline updates, and GLP-1/GIP endoscopy holds
statement: In the Oculus randomized trial, continuing GLP-1 or GLP-1/GIP agonists before upper endoscopy significantly increased residual gastric volume compared to skipping a dose, but produced no unplanned aspiration events, intubations, or hospitalizations.
supporting_quote: It certainly found that continuing these GLP-1 and JP agonist before the upper endoscopy significantly increased the residual gastric contents, but despite the retained gastric contents there were the increase in retained gastric contents, there was no cases of unplanned aspiration events, intubations, or hospitalizations.

episode_title: #526 DIGEST – game changers in pancreatic cancer, lipid guideline updates, and GLP-1/GIP endoscopy holds
statement: Requiring a clear liquid diet the day before (as when endoscopy is combined with colonoscopy) appeared to abolish the difference in residual gastric contents between continuing and holding the GLP-1 agonist.
supporting_quote: if patients were getting an endoscopy with a colonoscopy, and they therefore had to do a clear liquid diet for the day before, there did not seem to be any differences in this clinical in the in the surrogate endpoint between the two groups.

episode_title: #526 DIGEST – game changers in pancreatic cancer, lipid guideline updates, and GLP-1/GIP endoscopy holds
statement: In an open-label study of adults aged 60 or older on stable low-dose levothyroxine with TSH under 10, over a quarter successfully discontinued the medication at one year while maintaining TSH under 10 and free T4 within range and no worsening quality of life.
supporting_quote: over 1/4 of the participants were able to stop levothyroxine at 1 year while maintaining a TSH less than 10 and a free T4 within reference uh range without any worsening quality of life.

episode_title: #526 DIGEST – game changers in pancreatic cancer, lipid guideline updates, and GLP-1/GIP endoscopy holds
statement: Levothyroxine de-prescribing success rates were higher at lower doses, with over 63% of patients taking 50 micrograms successfully discontinuing at one year.
supporting_quote: over 63% of patients that were taking 50 micrograms of last successfully discontinued the medication within at the year mark.

episode_title: #526 DIGEST – game changers in pancreatic cancer, lipid guideline updates, and GLP-1/GIP endoscopy holds
statement: The levothyroxine de-prescribing study excluded patients on doses above 150 micrograms, those with prior thyroidectomy, radioactive iodine treatment, or congenital hypothyroidism, as these conditions require permanent replacement.
supporting_quote: they excluded people people that were taking high dose of levothyroxine to above 150 micrograms, had a history of any thyroidectomy or like a radioactive iodine treatment, congenital hypothyroidism

episode_title: #526 DIGEST – game changers in pancreatic cancer, lipid guideline updates, and GLP-1/GIP endoscopy holds

supporting_quote: it is not like the safest thing. It's not good for your bones. It's not good for the heart. Can increase your risk of for arrhythmias. So, I think we really have to have a high threshold to start it in that person

episode_title: #526 DIGEST – game changers in pancreatic cancer, lipid guideline updates, and GLP-1/GIP endoscopy holds
statement: When attempting levothyroxine de-prescribing, closely follow the patient's symptoms and check TSH every 6 to 8 weeks.
supporting_quote: you would really need to closely follow how they're feeling and follow their their leva you know, their TSH every 6 to 8 weeks, something like that. That's what I would do if I was trying to de-prescribe for somebody.

episode_title: #525 Multi-Cancer Early Detection Testing

we only have um recommendations from the US Preventive Services Task Force for four. There's a guarded recommendation for a fifth and that's for prostate cancer, but we do have recommendations for

episode_title: #525 Multi-Cancer Early Detection Testing

episode_title: #525 Multi-Cancer Early Detection Testing

supporting_quote:
If the multi-cancer detection tests reach their potential, they're an addition

episode_title: #525 Multi-Cancer Early Detection Testing
statement: The main concern with multi-cancer early detection tests is false positives, which lead to unnecessary imaging and procedures.
supporting_quote:
we try to educate them about the
the chance of a false positive. That's what we're really most worried about because that leads to unnecessary imaging and sometimes unnecessary procedures.

episode_title: #525 Multi-Cancer Early Detection Testing

supporting_quote:
their most recent large study in the United States, Pathfinder 2 study,
uh showed a false positivity rate of 30%. So, of their positive signals, 30% were false positive.

episode_title: #525 Multi-Cancer Early Detection Testing
statement: The Grail (Galleri) test is based on methylated DNA, providing a methylation pattern and a tissue of origin that indicates where to look.
supporting_quote: the Grail test is based on methylated DNA, which gives a you methylation pattern and a tissue of origin. So you kind of know where to look with that particular assay.

episode_title: #525 Multi-Cancer Early Detection Testing
statement: Pancreatic cancer is a poor shedder of DNA, and even in metastatic disease analyzable blood material is often not detectable, making it poorly suited to these blood-based assays.
supporting_quote:
which is always almost always detected too late.
And it's a very poor shedder. Even patients who have metastatic disease, very rarely can we pick up not very rarely, but in maybe 20% of the cases we can we can't pick up analyzable material from the blood.

episode_title: #525 Multi-Cancer Early Detection Testing
statement: The only blood-based cancer detection test reimbursed by insurance is Shield from Exact Sciences; the others are self-pay.

the only blood-based test for detection that I know of that is reimbursed by insurance is Shield.
Um and that's uh from Exact Sciences.
Um the others are all self-pay.

episode_title: #525 Multi-Cancer Early Detection Testing

supporting_quote:
the the positivity rate is about 1 to 2%.

episode_title: #525 Multi-Cancer Early Detection Testing
statement: A key limitation is overdiagnosis, since detecting indolent thyroid cancer, low-risk prostate cancer, or indolent lymphoma may not help the patient.
supporting_quote:
if you're finding an indolent thyroid cancer or a prostate cancer that doesn't need treatment

episode_title: #525 Multi-Cancer Early Detection Testing
statement: Whole body MRI is not recommended for average-risk asymptomatic patients, and there is no evidence it improves detection of important cancers or mortality outside hereditary cancer syndromes.
supporting_quote: we we don't recommend them, first of all.

episode_title: #525 Multi-Cancer Early Detection Testing
statement: Whole body MRIs done at direct-to-consumer centers are often lower quality (thicker slices, lesser read quality), and academic centers typically repeat the MRI to obtain the best quality imaging.

when we're presented with a patient who um

episode_title: #525 Multi-Cancer Early Detection Testing
statement: For a positive multi-cancer signal without a tissue of origin, the recommended approach is a whole body PET CT, though some tumors are not FDG avid or too small to register.

episode_title: #525 Multi-Cancer Early Detection Testing

Well, it doesn't mean that he doesn't have cancer because it's possible that the test missed something.

episode_title: #525 Multi-Cancer Early Detection Testing
statement: Most experts in the field consider a stage shift (fewer advanced-stage cancers) a sufficient endpoint, since mortality endpoints would take so long the technology may be obsolete before data mature.

episode_title: #525 Multi-Cancer Early Detection Testing
statement: Patients who elect non-traditional screening tests should be counseled that the tests aren't perfect, false positives can lead to unnecessary procedures, radiation exposure, and significant anxiety.

that the tests aren't perfect,
that you may have a false positive, that that false positive could lead to unnecessary procedures and exposure to radiation and cause a lot of anxiety. So I think it's really the cons that we need to emphasize
in our messaging to patients.

episode_title: #503: Falls with Dr. Alyson Michener and Suzanne Zukoski, MSPT
statement: A fall is defined simply as coming to rest unintentionally at a lower surface, and every fall deserves a workup regardless of whether syncope was involved.
supporting_quote:
All a fall is is coming to rest
unintentionally at a lower surface, the
ground or the floor.

episode_title: #503: Falls with Dr. Alyson Michener and Suzanne Zukoski, MSPT
statement: Avoid the term 'mechanical fall'; it is used inconsistently and often leads clinicians to prematurely stop working up the fall.
supporting_quote:
I really really
think we should
shy away from the term mechanical.

episode_title: #503: Falls with Dr. Alyson Michener and Suzanne Zukoski, MSPT
statement: The 5Ms framework (multimorbidity, medications, mind, mobility, matters most) is a useful way to dissect geriatric syndromes like falls when a classic organ-based approach fails.
supporting_quote:
The 5M are a
really good way to dissect geriatric
syndromes when you can't use that kind
of classic um disease or organbased
approach.

episode_title: #503: Falls with Dr. Alyson Michener and Suzanne Zukoski, MSPT
statement: About a quarter of older adults fall every year, and having one fall is the biggest risk factor to predict future falls.
supporting_quote:
About a quarter
of older adults fall every year.

episode_title: #503: Falls with Dr. Alyson Michener and Suzanne Zukoski, MSPT
statement: Having had one fall is the strongest predictor of future falls.
supporting_quote:
We know that one
having one fall is the biggest risk factor to
produce f to predict future falls.

episode_title: #503: Falls with Dr. Alyson Michener and Suzanne Zukoski, MSPT
statement: High-risk medications for falls include psychoactive drugs, antidepressants, antipsychotics, benzodiazepines, opioids, and loop diuretics; overtreating hypertension or diabetes can also cause falls.
supporting_quote:
psychoactive
medications, anti-depressants,
antiscychotics, benzoazipines, kind of
the usual players.

episode_title: #503: Falls with Dr. Alyson Michener and Suzanne Zukoski, MSPT
statement: Always ask specifically about over-the-counter diphenhydramine, since it is often taken without being on the medication list and few older patients should be on it.
supporting_quote:
I always have to ask
specifically about dyen hydramine
because it's not on the medication list.

episode_title: #503: Falls with Dr. Alyson Michener and Suzanne Zukoski, MSPT
statement: For the 'mind' component, consider the three Ds — dementia, depression, and delirium — as contributors to falls; an acute URI or UTI causing delirium can present as a fall.

So for um
mind we think of the
three Ds dementia, depression and
delirium.

episode_title: #503: Falls with Dr. Alyson Michener and Suzanne Zukoski, MSPT
statement: For a fall workup, targeted testing based on history and exam may include an EKG and telemetry for arrhythmia, a hemoglobin A1C to assess diabetes control, and a good exam of affected joints.

it would be
reasonable to do an EKG and maybe put
her on telemetry to see whether her
atrial fibrillation um, you know, if if

episode_title: #503: Falls with Dr. Alyson Michener and Suzanne Zukoski, MSPT
statement: Screening tests like the timed up and go are less useful in the acute hospital setting because a patient hospitalized for a fall is already known to be at highest risk; these tests are more for outpatient risk stratification.
supporting_quote:
If you have someone in the hospital that's
there for a fall, you already know that

because they had a major injury or or

screening tests are a little bit less

episode_title: #503: Falls with Dr. Alyson Michener and Suzanne Zukoski, MSPT
statement: There are three evidence-based arms of fall prevention: multifactorial risk factor assessment and management, exercise, and home modifications.
supporting_quote:
there's actually three main arms of
evidence-based fall prevention. The
first one is multiffactorial risk factor
assessment and management

episode_title: #503: Falls with Dr. Alyson Michener and Suzanne Zukoski, MSPT
statement: Multifactorial risk factor assessment and management yields about a 25% relative risk reduction in falls in meta-analyses and is recommended for the highest-risk patients (hospitalized for a fall, major injury, one or more falls in the past year, or unsteady on their feet).
supporting_quote:
in meta analyses, we've
seen about a 20 around a 25% relative
risk reduction with with those
strategies.

episode_title: #503: Falls with Dr. Alyson Michener and Suzanne Zukoski, MSPT
statement: About half of falls are not reported to physicians because patients assume falls are a normal part of aging or fear loss of independence.
supporting_quote:
we know that about
half of falls are not reported to
physicians.

episode_title: #503: Falls with Dr. Alyson Michener and Suzanne Zukoski, MSPT
statement: In a bradycardic patient on both metoprolol and donepezil, consider that donepezil may cause bradycardia and can be deprescribed if not clearly effective; back off metoprolol depending on the indication.

when I look at her medication list that

episode_title: #503: Falls with Dr. Alyson Michener and Suzanne Zukoski, MSPT
statement: For a patient with atrial fibrillation who falls, unless there is an immediate contraindication, continue anticoagulation in the hospital; the decision is nuanced and best made with the patient's PCP based on trajectory and goals rather than scores alone.

usually in

episode_title: #503: Falls with Dr. Alyson Michener and Suzanne Zukoski, MSPT
statement: Exercise—specifically balance and strength training—reduces the risk of falls and is recommended for all patients, both low-risk and those who have had major falls.

episode_title: #503: Falls with Dr. Alyson Michener and Suzanne Zukoski, MSPT
statement: Tai Chi is a specific evidence-based exercise program shown to be beneficial for fall prevention and can be adapted from a seated position up to standing.
supporting_quote:

episode_title: #503: Falls with Dr. Alyson Michener and Suzanne Zukoski, MSPT
statement: Vitamin D is no longer thought to prevent falls in and of itself; supplement only for a low level, not routinely for fall prevention.

we we don't

episode_title: #503: Falls with Dr. Alyson Michener and Suzanne Zukoski, MSPT
statement: Home modifications reduce falls by about a 23% relative risk reduction in meta-analyses and can be guided by occupational therapist home safety evaluations or the CDC STEADI home safety checklist.

home modifications also

episode_title: #503: Falls with Dr. Alyson Michener and Suzanne Zukoski, MSPT

episode_title: #503: Falls with Dr. Alyson Michener and Suzanne Zukoski, MSPT
statement: Physical therapy assistance levels: minimal assist is about 25% help from the therapist, moderate assist is about 50/50, and maximal assist is about 75% therapist and 25% patient.

a minist would be about

episode_title: #503: Falls with Dr. Alyson Michener and Suzanne Zukoski, MSPT
statement: For a fallen patient, PT assessment starts with orthostatic blood pressures and functional testing of bed mobility, sitting balance, and leg strength against gravity before ambulation.

episode_title: #501 GERD vs NERD: Reflux Updates with Dr. James Callaway

episode_title: #501 GERD vs NERD: Reflux Updates with Dr. James Callaway
statement: Alarm/red flag symptoms to screen for in reflux patients include dysphagia, bleeding, anemia, vomiting, and weight loss.

specifically difficulty

episode_title: #501 GERD vs NERD: Reflux Updates with Dr. James Callaway
statement: The vast majority of reflux events are an upright, postprandial phenomenon occurring in the first 90 minutes after meals, and reflux typically decreases when lying recumbent at night.

episode_title: #501 GERD vs NERD: Reflux Updates with Dr. James Callaway
statement: The primary mechanism of reflux is transient lower esophageal sphincter relaxations occurring with a mismatch of elevated intragastric pressure, with anatomical causes such as a hiatal hernia displacing the EG junction defense mechanism.

episode_title: #501 GERD vs NERD: Reflux Updates with Dr. James Callaway
statement: Common trigger foods (the 'five C's') include citrus, carbonated beverages, caffeine, and cocktails/alcohol, but there is no strong objective evidence they worsen acid exposure; patients should avoid foods that trigger their own symptoms.

people typically
will say citrus, carbonated beverages,

episode_title: #501 GERD vs NERD: Reflux Updates with Dr. James Callaway
statement: For classic heartburn without alarm symptoms, an 8-week trial of once-daily PPI at 40 mg omeprazole equivalent, taken 30 minutes before the first meal of the day, is the recommended initial treatment.

typically
we will say 40 milligrams if we're

episode_title: #501 GERD vs NERD: Reflux Updates with Dr. James Callaway
statement: PPIs are preferred over H2 receptor antagonists for reflux because H2RAs develop tachyphylaxis after about 4 weeks, and PPIs are superior at improving heartburn symptoms and healing erosive esophagitis.

the the H2 receptor
antagonist most
of the studies were four weeks in

episode_title: #501 GERD vs NERD: Reflux Updates with Dr. James Callaway
statement: Prescribing PPIs with an exit strategy—e.g., 8 weeks with no refills requiring a call-back—prevents patients from remaining on indefinite therapy inadvertently.

I will
often just give PPIs with like eight

episode_title: #501 GERD vs NERD: Reflux Updates with Dr. James Callaway
statement: Potassium-competitive acid blockers (PCABs/'P-CABs') reversibly inhibit the H/K ATPase, act within hours (versus 2-3 days for PPIs), and are FDA-approved for non-erosive reflux disease but currently mainly used for severe erosive esophagitis and H. pylori.

they are
PEABS which are potassium competitive

ATPAS pump.

episode_title: #501 GERD vs NERD: Reflux Updates with Dr. James Callaway
statement: Patients with high-risk complicated reflux disease—class C or D esophagitis, Barrett's esophagus, or peptic stricture—should be kept on long-term PPI therapy.

for instance class C
or D esophagitis which is more severe

episode_title: #501 GERD vs NERD: Reflux Updates with Dr. James Callaway
statement: Patients with non-erosive reflux disease are the ones to consider periodically trialing off PPIs to see if their symptom burden allows discontinuation.

there is
even more data that says that patients

episode_title: #501 GERD vs NERD: Reflux Updates with Dr. James Callaway
statement: When stopping PPIs, counsel patients about rebound acid hypersecretion and encourage them to push through the first 10 days, after which symptom trajectory often improves.
supporting_quote:
we kind of talk about the phenomenon of
rebound acid production that can occur.
I usually tell patients if they can get

episode_title: #501 GERD vs NERD: Reflux Updates with Dr. James Callaway
statement: A PPI trial is pragmatic but not diagnostically conclusive: sensitivity is about 71% and specificity only about 44%, with ~35% of patients with completely normal testing still improving on PPI.
supporting_quote:
that gave an overall sensitivity of
71% and a specificity of only 44% for

episode_title: #501 GERD vs NERD: Reflux Updates with Dr. James Callaway
statement: The 2022 ACG guidelines recommend objective testing (rather than simply resuming a PPI) when symptoms recur after a trial, performing endoscopy 2-4 weeks off PPI so erosive esophagitis has time to recur.
supporting_quote:
Our newest recommendation is
actually to do objective testing at this

and this in saying that so this would

which gives erosive esophagitis enough

episode_title: #501 GERD vs NERD: Reflux Updates with Dr. James Callaway
statement: Non-erosive reflux disease cannot be diagnosed if the endoscopy is performed while the patient is on a PPI, since about 90% of endoscopies done on PPI appear normal.

You really can't make a

episode_title: #501 GERD vs NERD: Reflux Updates with Dr. James Callaway
statement: Stopping PPI for 2-4 weeks before endoscopy also helps unmask eosinophilic esophagitis, a mimicker of persistent reflux disease that can be treated by PPIs, allowing biopsy at endoscopy.
supporting_quote:
one of the mimickers of you know persistent reflux disease is

esophagitis and being off PPI which is a

episode_title: #501 GERD vs NERD: Reflux Updates with Dr. James Callaway
statement: Among reported PPI adverse effects, only increased risk of enteric (non-C. diff) infections had the most conclusive association; the others (CKD, osteoporosis, dementia) are association studies without established cause-and-effect.
supporting_quote:
the only thing that was felt to have the

infection so non CEDIFF interic

episode_title: #501 GERD vs NERD: Reflux Updates with Dr. James Callaway
statement: Routine monitoring of bone density, magnesium, or B12 is not recommended for patients on PPIs without other risk factors; those with CKD can safely use PPIs but should have creatinine monitored at least yearly.
supporting_quote:
our guidelines don't
think that we need to check bone density

episode_title: #501 GERD vs NERD: Reflux Updates with Dr. James Callaway
statement: Reflux hypersensitivity is when patients have normal acid exposure on testing but remain symptomatic from weekly acidic/non-acidic reflux, whereas functional heartburn shows no pathologic reflux at all—neither responds well to further acid suppression.
supporting_quote:
patients that have kind of
persistent reflux symptoms despite now

reflux testing and they're still very
symptomatic from that weekly acidic
reflux. That's actually what we term

episode_title: #501 GERD vs NERD: Reflux Updates with Dr. James Callaway
statement: PPIs are not effective at treating regurgitation, which is often the reason regurgitation-predominant symptoms get grouped into 'refractory GERD'.
supporting_quote:
especially in
the setting of regurgitation. That's a

episode_title: #501 GERD vs NERD: Reflux Updates with Dr. James Callaway
statement: Alginates form a raft/matrix over the acid pocket in the stomach acting as a physical barrier against reflux, are taken after meals and at bedtime, and are available over the counter.

they they kind of they form this

episode_title: #501 GERD vs NERD: Reflux Updates with Dr. James Callaway
statement: Surgical anti-reflux evaluation is most appropriate for patients with regurgitation-predominant symptoms and an anatomical cause (e.g., hiatal hernia); heartburn-predominant patients well-controlled on PPI generally do not need surgery.

if it's regurgitation

episode_title: 500th Ep Bonus AMA: Diabetes Meds, AI in Medicine, Thyroid Antibodies, Sleep Meds, Top 5 Lists, Favorite Failures, and More!

episode_title: 500th Ep Bonus AMA: Diabetes Meds, AI in Medicine, Thyroid Antibodies, Sleep Meds, Top 5 Lists, Favorite Failures, and More!

episode_title: 500th Ep Bonus AMA: Diabetes Meds, AI in Medicine, Thyroid Antibodies, Sleep Meds, Top 5 Lists, Favorite Failures, and More!

episode_title: 500th Ep Bonus AMA: Diabetes Meds, AI in Medicine, Thyroid Antibodies, Sleep Meds, Top 5 Lists, Favorite Failures, and More!

Um, when I hear about acute onset, this can be related to trauma or sometimes overuse like this gentleman going up and downstairs. And if you don't hear the history, ask about it, especially about falls. Um, acute onset can also be infection or septic arthritis.

episode_title: #493 Joint Pain in Older Adults with Dr. Una Makris
statement: Joint distribution offers diagnostic clues: MCPs and PIPs suggest RA, while DIP involvement suggests psoriatic arthritis or erosive osteoarthritis.
supporting_quote: If we have MCPS and PIPs, I think of RA. If we have DIPs, the distal interfallangial, I sometimes think of seroriatic arthritis, sometimes erosive osteoarthritis.

episode_title: #493 Joint Pain in Older Adults with Dr. Una Makris
statement: Refer for joint replacement when conservative management (PT, injections, topicals) has failed and function is declining; do not wait too long, since post-op rehabilitation is critical before contractures develop.
supporting_quote: so I refer to orthopedics to discuss knee or hip replacement when conservative management including PT, injections, topicals and so forth have have failed and also when the function is declining and I think that's the key and I don't like to wait too long because rehabilitation posttop for a replacement is so important.

episode_title: #493 Joint Pain in Older Adults with Dr. Una Makris
statement: Up to one-third of rheumatoid arthritis patients have onset over age 60-65 (late onset RA/LORA), which is often missed and tends to present with acute onset, more systemic and PMR-like features, large proximal joint (shoulder) involvement, seronegativity, and high ESR/CRP.
supporting_quote: up to you know onethird of rheumatoid arthritis patients have an onset of RA over the age of 60 or 65. So that would be late onset and these patients um tend to have more acute onset, more systemic features which you described. Uh they can have more PMR- like symptoms. They can have large proximal joints. I really see a lot of the shoulder involvement. These folks can be sergative meaning negative rheumatoid factor or anti-CCCP. They tend to have high ESR and CRP.

episode_title: #493 Joint Pain in Older Adults with Dr. Una Makris
statement: When PMR is on the differential, giant cell arteritis must be considered immediately, so ask about headaches, visual loss, jaw claudication, and scalp tenderness.
supporting_quote:
whenever PMR is on my differential, giant cellaritis has to come right after. So I think that it's important to start asking questions about headaches, visual loss,

um jaw claudication, scalp tenderness

episode_title: #493 Joint Pain in Older Adults with Dr. Una Makris
statement: Refer suspected RA to rheumatology as early as possible to initiate disease-modifying therapy, because prednisone is not disease modifying.
supporting_quote: So I think the key message here is um refer as early as possible so we can initiate disease modifying therapy because predinazone is not disease modifying.

episode_title: #493 Joint Pain in Older Adults with Dr. Una Makris
statement: Up to 70% of patients with late onset RA have not been initiated on appropriate treatment within the first 12 months, reflecting undertreatment of older adults that may stem from agism and biases.
supporting_quote:
some of her work highlights that up to 70%

of patients with late onset RA have not been initiated on appropriate treatment after the first 12 months and that's astounding.

episode_title: #493 Joint Pain in Older Adults with Dr. Una Makris
statement: Methotrexate is typically first-line DMARD for RA if renal function is normal, but should be avoided in patients with impaired renal function.
supporting_quote:
if they have normal renal function, I would say that my first line is typically methtoresate.

With this patient's renal function, I would avoid methtoresate.

episode_title: #493 Joint Pain in Older Adults with Dr. Una Makris
statement: Polypharmacy (5 or more medications) is common in older RA patients, with a prevalence around 50% in those over 65; use Beer's criteria to identify potentially inappropriate medications given frequent use of NSAIDs, opioids, glucocorticoids, and antidepressants.
supporting_quote:
I believe the prevalence of polyfarm pharmacy among patients with RA over the age of 65 is 50%.

Um, and so I do suggest looking at beer's criteria. Beer's criteria is very useful to highlight some potentially inappropriate medications in older adults

episode_title: #493 Joint Pain in Older Adults with Dr. Una Makris
statement: There is a higher risk of all-cause dementia in RA versus non-RA patients, even after adjusting for age, sex, education, and cardiovascular risk factors, likely reflecting the impact of chronic inflammation ('inflammaging').
supporting_quote:
we do know that um there appears to be a higher risk of all cause dementia in RA versus nonr

even after adjusting for age sex education cardiovascular disease risk factors and events.

episode_title: #493 Joint Pain in Older Adults with Dr. Una Makris
statement: Use the CDC screening questions to assess fall risk in older adults: whether they have fallen in the past year, feel unsteady standing or walking, or worry about falling.
supporting_quote: So I usually use the CDC questions. Have you fallen in the past year? or do you feel unsteady when standing or walking? Are you worried about falling?

episode_title: #483: Diabetic Dilemmas in Older Adults, Pregnancy and Corticosteroid Use with Jeff Colburn, MD

episode_title: #483: Diabetic Dilemmas in Older Adults, Pregnancy and Corticosteroid Use with Jeff Colburn, MD

episode_title: #483: Diabetic Dilemmas in Older Adults, Pregnancy and Corticosteroid Use with Jeff Colburn, MD

episode_title: #483: Diabetic Dilemmas in Older Adults, Pregnancy and Corticosteroid Use with Jeff Colburn, MD

episode_title: #463 Tofurkey-cakes: A Hypertension Triple Pill, Arm Position & BP, early TAVR, Liberal vs Restrictive Transfusions, New Periop Guidelines

episode_title: #463 Tofurkey-cakes: A Hypertension Triple Pill, Arm Position & BP, early TAVR, Liberal vs Restrictive Transfusions, New Periop Guidelines

supporting_quote: if your is sitting your lap at your side it is statistically higher than it if was resting at Heart level on a desk so the the arm positioning matters

episode_title: #463 Tofurkey-cakes: A Hypertension Triple Pill, Arm Position & BP, early TAVR, Liberal vs Restrictive Transfusions, New Periop Guidelines

episode_title: #463 Tofurkey-cakes: A Hypertension Triple Pill, Arm Position & BP, early TAVR, Liberal vs Restrictive Transfusions, New Periop Guidelines

episode_title: #463 Tofurkey-cakes: A Hypertension Triple Pill, Arm Position & BP, early TAVR, Liberal vs Restrictive Transfusions, New Periop Guidelines

episode_title: #463 Tofurkey-cakes: A Hypertension Triple Pill, Arm Position & BP, early TAVR, Liberal vs Restrictive Transfusions, New Periop Guidelines

episode_title: #463 Tofurkey-cakes: A Hypertension Triple Pill, Arm Position & BP, early TAVR, Liberal vs Restrictive Transfusions, New Periop Guidelines

episode_title: #461: Facial Nerve Follies: Understanding Bell’s Palsy
statement: Bell's palsy is defined as spontaneous peripheral impairment of the seventh cranial nerve occurring in isolation of any other neurologic or systemic symptoms; involvement of any other cranial nerve or systemic signs argues against the diagnosis.
supporting_quote: it is defined as spontaneous peripheral impairment of the seventh cranial nerve and this needs to occur in isolation of any neurologic or systemic symptoms

episode_title: #461: Facial Nerve Follies: Understanding Bell’s Palsy
statement: Peripheral seventh nerve lesions (Bell's palsy) involve the forehead, whereas central lesions spare the forehead; flattening and diminished wrinkling of the forehead strongly suggests a peripheral lesion.
supporting_quote:
there's forehead involvement so if you if you see
somebody you know their forehead is is
flattened and they don't wrinkle nearly
as much that very strongly suggests
peripheral seventh

episode_title: #461: Facial Nerve Follies: Understanding Bell’s Palsy
statement: The seventh cranial nerve innervates the stapedius muscle which dampens sound, so Bell's palsy causes hyperacusis (sounds are louder on the affected side).
supporting_quote:
It also inates the
stiped muscle in the inner ear which
actually dampens sound so if you have a
Bells you'll have impairment of that and
they'll actually have what's called
hyperacusis meaning it's louder on that

episode_title: #461: Facial Nerve Follies: Understanding Bell’s Palsy
statement: Bell's palsy reaches maximum severity gradually within 48 to 72 hours, whereas a stroke is at maximum intensity at onset; this temporal pattern helps distinguish the two.

a Bells
by definition is going to reach maximum

that over a period of usually hours

episode_title: #461: Facial Nerve Follies: Understanding Bell’s Palsy
statement: Because brain stem cranial nerve nuclei are packed closely together, a brain stem lesion causing seventh nerve involvement usually affects neighboring nerves (V, VI, VIII); look specifically for facial sensation changes, extraocular movement problems, and hearing loss.

it's really hard to

episode_title: #461: Facial Nerve Follies: Understanding Bell’s Palsy
statement: For subtle motor weakness suggesting a central cause, high-yield exam maneuvers are the pronator drift and 'orbiting' (rapidly rotating tightly clenched fists around each other), where the weaker side moves slower and the stronger side orbits around it.

the

episode_title: #461: Facial Nerve Follies: Understanding Bell’s Palsy

episode_title: #461: Facial Nerve Follies: Understanding Bell’s Palsy

episode_title: #461: Facial Nerve Follies: Understanding Bell’s Palsy

episode_title: #459 DIGEST: Finerenone for Heart Failure, PCV21 and the latest Pneumonia Vaccine Recs, 24-hour vs 15-hour Oxygen Therapy, and PREVENT vs PCE for Cardiovascular Risk Estimation

episode_title: #459 DIGEST: Finerenone for Heart Failure, PCV21 and the latest Pneumonia Vaccine Recs, 24-hour vs 15-hour Oxygen Therapy, and PREVENT vs PCE for Cardiovascular Risk Estimation
statement: The CDC updated its guidance to recommend pneumococcal vaccination for all adults aged 50 and older (previously 65), with options of PCV20, PCV21, or PCV15 followed by PPSV23 one year later.
supporting_quote: the CDC updated its vaccine guidance to include pneumonia vaccination for all adults aged 50 years and older so it used to be age 65 and older you only got it under 65 if you had risk factors well now they lowered that age to 50 years and older they should receive either PC V 20 PCV 21 or the combination of pcv15 followed by ppsv23 one year later

episode_title: #459 DIGEST: Finerenone for Heart Failure, PCV21 and the latest Pneumonia Vaccine Recs, 24-hour vs 15-hour Oxygen Therapy, and PREVENT vs PCE for Cardiovascular Risk Estimation

episode_title: #459 DIGEST: Finerenone for Heart Failure, PCV21 and the latest Pneumonia Vaccine Recs, 24-hour vs 15-hour Oxygen Therapy, and PREVENT vs PCE for Cardiovascular Risk Estimation
statement: In the STRIDE-3 trial, PCV21 was compared to PCV20 and covers 11 new serotypes; PCV21 covers about 85% of the strains that cause invasive pneumococcal disease in adults.

episode_title: #459 DIGEST: Finerenone for Heart Failure, PCV21 and the latest Pneumonia Vaccine Recs, 24-hour vs 15-hour Oxygen Therapy, and PREVENT vs PCE for Cardiovascular Risk Estimation

episode_title: #459 DIGEST: Finerenone for Heart Failure, PCV21 and the latest Pneumonia Vaccine Recs, 24-hour vs 15-hour Oxygen Therapy, and PREVENT vs PCE for Cardiovascular Risk Estimation

episode_title: #459 DIGEST: Finerenone for Heart Failure, PCV21 and the latest Pneumonia Vaccine Recs, 24-hour vs 15-hour Oxygen Therapy, and PREVENT vs PCE for Cardiovascular Risk Estimation

episode_title: #459 DIGEST: Finerenone for Heart Failure, PCV21 and the latest Pneumonia Vaccine Recs, 24-hour vs 15-hour Oxygen Therapy, and PREVENT vs PCE for Cardiovascular Risk Estimation

episode_title: #459 DIGEST: Finerenone for Heart Failure, PCV21 and the latest Pneumonia Vaccine Recs, 24-hour vs 15-hour Oxygen Therapy, and PREVENT vs PCE for Cardiovascular Risk Estimation

episode_title: #459 DIGEST: Finerenone for Heart Failure, PCV21 and the latest Pneumonia Vaccine Recs, 24-hour vs 15-hour Oxygen Therapy, and PREVENT vs PCE for Cardiovascular Risk Estimation

supporting_quote: I may check LP little a which we've talked about on other episodes at least one time is it's recommended in a lot of guidelines to check that at least one time for all adults to help risk stratify them

episode_title: #452 NAFLD/MASLD with Dr. Elliot Tapper
statement: Use ALT upper limits of ~20 for women and ~30 for men, since lab-reported 'normal' values are derived from population medians that often include people with liver disease.
supporting_quote: so the numbers that you came up with 20 for a woman uh 30 for a man that is that is perfect for determining abnormal

episode_title: #452 NAFLD/MASLD with Dr. Elliot Tapper
statement: An AST higher than the ALT should raise concern for chronic alcohol use or cirrhosis, since AST can rise as liver disease progresses.
supporting_quote: the ratio has the as being higher than the alt and this tips me off two concerns where my concern about this young man is is immediately greater than it would be had that ratio been reversed because now I'm starting to think about one Chron IC alcohol use or two curosis because in fact as the liver disease progresses that as can rise

episode_title: #452 NAFLD/MASLD with Dr. Elliot Tapper
statement: Phosphatidylethanol (PEth) is effectively the 'A1C of alcohol,' reflecting blood alcohol exposure over the last few weeks; counsel patients transparently before ordering it.
supporting_quote: I'm going to check their blood uh for the presence of alcohol use by using a test called phospha tidal ethanol it's effectively the A1C of alcohol tells you about blood exposure to alcohol over the last few weeks

episode_title: #452 NAFLD/MASLD with Dr. Elliot Tapper
statement: A PEth cutoff of 20 captures significant alcohol use; daily one-to-two drinks yields roughly 20-50, and false positives at high levels (e.g., 500) are effectively case-reportable.
supporting_quote: if they are drinking every day one or two drinks is going to be something on the order of 20 to 50 or so but you're never going to miss somebody with alcohol use disorder unless they stop drinking 3 to four weeks ago

episode_title: #452 NAFLD/MASLD with Dr. Elliot Tapper
statement: Ethyl glucuronide is a urine metabolite of ethanol that captures alcohol consumption within the last 3 to 5 days, a shorter window than PEth.
supporting_quote: ethylglucuronide is a good test it's a urine metabolite of ethanol metabolism and it will capture alcohol consumption within the last like three to five days

episode_title: #452 NAFLD/MASLD with Dr. Elliot Tapper
statement: The FIB-4 (age, platelets, ALT/AST) risk-stratifies for advanced fibrosis: >2.67 is higher risk, <1.3 is very low risk, and for patients over 65 the low-risk cutoff is <2.0 rather than <1.3.
supporting_quote: if that patient has a fib4 greater than 2.67 and you don't have to remember that number all the websites will will tell you then that is a person who is at higher risk of having significant fibrosis uh in their liver if that number is less than 1.3 then their risk of fibrosis is super low

episode_title: #452 NAFLD/MASLD with Dr. Elliot Tapper
statement: For patients over age 65, the FIB-4 low-risk cutoff should be less than 2.0, not less than 1.3, to avoid over-referring older patients.
supporting_quote: for people over the age of uh 65 the cut off is should be less than two not not less than 1.3 so you're not just referring everybody who with a F4 of two because they're 60

episode_title: #452 NAFLD/MASLD with Dr. Elliot Tapper
statement: The most common cause of an elevated ferritin in the United States is MASLD, not hemochromatosis, because the inflamed liver raises ferritin.
supporting_quote: the most common cause of an elevated feragen in the United States is not hemocromatosis but it is MLD and so it's elevated because the liver is inflate

episode_title: #452 NAFLD/MASLD with Dr. Elliot Tapper
statement: The platelet count is the most important test in liver disease work-up because it forms the core of FIB-4 and informs the prognosis regarding advanced fibrosis.
supporting_quote: the most important test when considering liver disease is is going to be that platelet count that forms the the core the F four because it informs you about the prognosis the presence of advanced fibrosis which is the most important thing

episode_title: #452 NAFLD/MASLD with Dr. Elliot Tapper
statement: A positive FIB-4 cannot diagnose cirrhosis by itself; because pre-test probability of cirrhosis is only ~3% even in diabetics, sequential testing (next being transient elastography/FibroScan) is needed.
supporting_quote: if that FIB four is positive if it's elevated you cannot make a diagnosis of therosis based on that it's just not good as good of a test and to begin with the pre-test probability that your patient had therosis even if they have diabetes walking into your clinic is no more than three or so percent so we need sequential tests

episode_title: #452 NAFLD/MASLD with Dr. Elliot Tapper
statement: Transient elastography (FibroScan) has replaced liver biopsy for diagnosing advanced fibrosis; it has good negative predictive value but middling positive predictive value because inflammation, right heart congestion, or a recent fatty meal can transiently stiffen the liver.
supporting_quote: it has for all intents and purposes replaced liver biopsy for the diagnosis of advanced fibrosis and curosis so we're not doing transion listography willy-nilly

episode_title: #452 NAFLD/MASLD with Dr. Elliot Tapper
statement: As BMI rises above 40, transient elastography failure rate increases due to fixed measurement depth, so magnetic resonance elastography is preferred in high-BMI patients.
supporting_quote: as the BMI rises above 40 the failure rate of this test starts de Cline and that's that's when we reach for magnetic resonance elastography

episode_title: #452 NAFLD/MASLD with Dr. Elliot Tapper
statement: Use the 'rule of fives' for liver stiffness on FibroScan: 5 is normal, 10 confers ~40% chance of advanced fibrosis, 15 makes cirrhosis very likely, 20 nearly definite, and 25 suggests portal hypertension/varices.
supporting_quote: a five is Stone Cold normal 10 is someone who is there's a probability about a 40% chance they have advanced fibrosis so they could have therosis but many of them do not 15 the likelihood of therosis is very high it 20 the likelihood of therosis is nearly definite and 25 the patient probably has portal hypertension

episode_title: #452 NAFLD/MASLD with Dr. Elliot Tapper
statement: MR elastography values are about a third to a quarter lower than transient/shear-wave values, so a 5 on MRE is very stiff (cirrhosis) whereas 5 on FibroScan is normal.
supporting_quote: the number you get on an m is like a third to a quarter lower than the number you get on the other elastographic test so you have to be careful how you interpret it because Normal on a Fiers scan is five but five on an m is is very very stiff that is curosis

episode_title: #452 NAFLD/MASLD with Dr. Elliot Tapper
statement: A liver stiffness <20 with platelets >150 makes varices probability low enough to avoid screening endoscopy, while stiffness >25 is high enough to start beta blockers for portal hypertension without endoscopy.
supporting_quote: if you have a liver stiffness measure less than 20 and a PL count greater than 150 you don't need to have an endoscopy because the probability you have vares is so low conversely if that liver stiffness is greater than 25 we can just have a conversation in clinic about starting beta blockers to treat portal hypertension and VAR es without an endoscopy

episode_title: #452 NAFLD/MASLD with Dr. Elliot Tapper
statement: Weight loss thresholds in fatty liver disease: ~5% reduces steatosis, ~7.5% shuts off inflammation (resolves NASH/MASH), and ~10% starts to improve fibrosis.
supporting_quote: 5% body weight loss is sufficient to reduce steatosis in the liver around seven 7.5% is when you start to shut off the inflammation you resolve the Nash the mash and around 10% is when you start to resolve the fibrosis

episode_title: #452 NAFLD/MASLD with Dr. Elliot Tapper
statement: GLP-1 receptor agonists are best positioned for patients who do not yet have cirrhosis (low FIB-4 or low-to-medium liver stiffness) where the main goal is weight loss.
supporting_quote: these drugs are probably best positioned for the person who doesn't have therosis yet all right the person who has a low fit4 or a low to medium range liver stiffness measure the main goal there is weight loss

episode_title: #452 NAFLD/MASLD with Dr. Elliot Tapper
statement: Resmetirom is a thyroid mimetic and the first drug FDA-approved to reduce fibrosis more than placebo in a phase 3 trial, notably doing so without weight loss; it is best suited for patients with F3 fibrosis.

hours is probably ideal again the
