DOA don't have any form of reversal
that is easily accessible or cheap

episode_title: #425 Perioperative Management of Antithrombotics with Dr. Poorvi Hardman
statement: Aspirin for secondary prevention can almost always be continued perioperatively except for neurosurgical procedures, certain urological cases, and posterior chamber ophthalmic cases requiring a retrobulbar block.
supporting_quote:
if aspirin is used for
secondary prevention you can almost
always continue it except for cases um
that involve Neurosurgical procedures
certain Urological cases and then
posterior chamber Opthalmic cases that
require a retro ballbar block

episode_title: #425 Perioperative Management of Antithrombotics with Dr. Poorvi Hardman
statement: Aspirin used for primary prevention is generally held before high bleeding risk surgeries, based on POISE-2 data showing aspirin did not reduce death or MI but increased bleeding risk.
supporting_quote:
the um aspirin was not
necessarily beneficial in reducing the
incidence of death or Mi but it did
increase the risk of bleeding so in
extrapolating this data I usually tend
to hold Act aspirin if it is used for
primary prevention in patients that are
getting surgeries that are at high
bleeding risk

episode_title: #425 Perioperative Management of Antithrombotics with Dr. Poorvi Hardman
statement: Clopidogrel should be stopped 5 days before surgery; P2Y12 monotherapy patients (e.g., for recurrent strokes) can be transitioned to aspirin only during the perioperative period.
supporting_quote:
if a patient is on
let's say cped gral you will need to
stop that five days prior to
surgery now if they're on cigal
monotherapy for let's say recurrent
Strokes we do have the option to
transition the patient off of the P2 y12
Inhibitors and onto aspirin only

episode_title: #425 Perioperative Management of Antithrombotics with Dr. Poorvi Hardman
statement: P2Y12 inhibitors can be restarted greater than 48 hours after surgery, while aspirin (reversible) can be restarted within 12 hours after surgery.
supporting_quote:
greater than
48 hours after surgery again discussion
with a surgeon is necessary here because
you can't start a P2 y12 inhibitor and
then reverse them as easily

episode_title: #425 Perioperative Management of Antithrombotics with Dr. Poorvi Hardman
statement: For elective surgery, wait 6 weeks after bare metal stent placement and a minimum of 6 months after drug-eluting stent placement, since major adverse cardiovascular outcomes are highest in the first 6 weeks and plateau after 6 months.
supporting_quote:
in patients
that are getting elective surgeries it's
ideal to wait 6 weeks after bare metal
stent placement and then 6 months
minimum after drug alluding stun
placement

episode_title: #424 Hotcakes: Transfusion in Acute MI, COVID-19 Rebound after Paxlovid, Every-other-day Iron Revisited, Oral Phenylephrine Doesn’t Work, and Stop Correcting Serum Calcium
statement: In a NHANES sample of US females aged 12-21, using a ferritin cutoff of 25, about 40% were iron deficient, and using a cutoff of 50, about 75% were iron deficient, yet only 6% were anemic.
supporting_quote:
And basically what they found was that using a sort of conservative ferritin cutoff of 25, 40% of
young women in the United States were iron deficient. And using a slightly less conservative
cutoff of a ferritin of 50, 75% of the women in this sample were iron deficient. And despite that,
only 6% of women in the sample were anemic.

episode_title: #424 Hotcakes: Transfusion in Acute MI, COVID-19 Rebound after Paxlovid, Every-other-day Iron Revisited, Oral Phenylephrine Doesn’t Work, and Stop Correcting Serum Calcium
statement: Among iron-deficient young women, nearly 85% had a normal hemoglobin, raising the question of whether hemoglobin is an adequate screening tool for iron deficiency.
supporting_quote:
one of the features of this
study that stuck out the most to me was that among iron deficient young women, almost 85% of
them had a normal hemoglobin.

episode_title: #424 Hotcakes: Transfusion in Acute MI, COVID-19 Rebound after Paxlovid, Every-other-day Iron Revisited, Oral Phenylephrine Doesn’t Work, and Stop Correcting Serum Calcium
statement: Randomized trials in the 2020s show no clear benefit of every-other-day versus daily oral iron dosing for treating anemia, including on side effects and hepcidin levels; either strategy is reasonable, though every-other-day may raise iron levels more slowly.
supporting_quote:
there's been a couple more randomized trials in the 2020s, or
a couple randomized trials in the 2020s, all showing sort of no clear benefit for treating anemia
with daily versus every other day dosing, even on side effects and even on Hepsodin levels,
Paul.

episode_title: #424 Hotcakes: Transfusion in Acute MI, COVID-19 Rebound after Paxlovid, Every-other-day Iron Revisited, Oral Phenylephrine Doesn’t Work, and Stop Correcting Serum Calcium
statement: Corrected calcium (Payne's formula) performs worse the lower the albumin, which is exactly the setting where you would want it to perform well.
supporting_quote:
The problem is when they've actually looked at this
corrected calcium formula and how it performs, Paul, it actually performs worse the lower the
albumin is.

episode_title: #424 Hotcakes: Transfusion in Acute MI, COVID-19 Rebound after Paxlovid, Every-other-day Iron Revisited, Oral Phenylephrine Doesn’t Work, and Stop Correcting Serum Calcium
statement: Uncorrected total calcium correlates with ionized (free) calcium only 70-80% of the time, and corrected calcium performs even worse; if worried about a calcium disorder, use the uncorrected value or obtain an ionized calcium.
supporting_quote:
so just the calcium that you just get correlates 70 to 80 percent of the time with
ionized, also called free calcium. That's not great, but actually the corrected calcium does worse
than that. So there's no point in correcting the calcium because it's going to perform worse.

episode_title: #424 Hotcakes: Transfusion in Acute MI, COVID-19 Rebound after Paxlovid, Every-other-day Iron Revisited, Oral Phenylephrine Doesn’t Work, and Stop Correcting Serum Calcium
statement: Ionized (free) calcium samples are sensitive to temperature, air, and pH, and should be run within about 30 minutes; if delayed, place the sample in an ice slurry.
supporting_quote: You want to run the sample pretty quickly, like within 30 minutes.

episode_title: #424 Hotcakes: Transfusion in Acute MI, COVID-19 Rebound after Paxlovid, Every-other-day Iron Revisited, Oral Phenylephrine Doesn’t Work, and Stop Correcting Serum Calcium
statement: In an observational cohort, nirmatrelvir-ritonavir (Paxlovid) was associated with significantly more virologic rebound than no treatment, about 20% versus 2%.
supporting_quote:
It did show significantly more biologic rebound in patients who took the antiviral than those who
did not. We're talking 20% versus 2%.

episode_title: #424 Hotcakes: Transfusion in Acute MI, COVID-19 Rebound after Paxlovid, Every-other-day Iron Revisited, Oral Phenylephrine Doesn’t Work, and Stop Correcting Serum Calcium
statement: In this rebound study, no one was hospitalized or died from virologic rebound, and only about half of patients with rebound had symptoms.
supporting_quote:
Nobody was hospitalized for this rebound, right? No one died from this. And only
about half the patients with rebound actually had symptoms of rebound

episode_title: #424 Hotcakes: Transfusion in Acute MI, COVID-19 Rebound after Paxlovid, Every-other-day Iron Revisited, Oral Phenylephrine Doesn’t Work, and Stop Correcting Serum Calcium
statement: NIH guidelines state that recurrence of COVID symptoms and viral detection after Paxlovid treatment has not been associated with progression to severe disease, and recommend against using concern about rebound as a reason to avoid treatment in high-risk patients.
supporting_quote:
they basically say, as you pointed out, the recurrence of COVID symptoms and
viral detection after treatment has not been associated with progression to severe disease. And
so they recommend against using concern about recurrence or rebound as a reason to avoid
treatment.

episode_title: #424 Hotcakes: Transfusion in Acute MI, COVID-19 Rebound after Paxlovid, Every-other-day Iron Revisited, Oral Phenylephrine Doesn’t Work, and Stop Correcting Serum Calcium
statement: In the standard-risk group, Paxlovid did not shorten the duration of symptoms (an unpublished trial known only from a press release), while it does prevent hospitalization and death in high-risk patients.
supporting_quote:
in the standard risk group, they did not even go for that endpoint because they
knew that people at standard risk are not getting hospitalized or dying from COVID, but they
tried to go for, does it shorten the duration of symptoms? Because that's probably a reason a lot
of people call and ask for it. And actually it did not, and they never published that study.

episode_title: #424 Hotcakes: Transfusion in Acute MI, COVID-19 Rebound after Paxlovid, Every-other-day Iron Revisited, Oral Phenylephrine Doesn’t Work, and Stop Correcting Serum Calcium
statement: An FDA advisory committee recommended removing oral phenylephrine from the OTC 'generally recognized as safe and effective' monograph because it has been convincingly shown to be ineffective, largely due to poor bioavailability from deactivation in the gut and liver.
supporting_quote:
this FDA advisory
committee recommended that oral phenylephrine be removed from the OTC monograph of
drugs that have this grace.
designation, which stands for generally regarded as safe and effective because essentially oral
phenylephrine has been proven to be ineffective

episode_title: #424 Hotcakes: Transfusion in Acute MI, COVID-19 Rebound after Paxlovid, Every-other-day Iron Revisited, Oral Phenylephrine Doesn’t Work, and Stop Correcting Serum Calcium
statement: For nasal/sinus symptoms, topical decongestants such as oxymetazoline (Afrin), intranasal phenylephrine, and saline irrigation work better and are safer than oral phenylephrine.
supporting_quote:
the topical decongestants, you
know, they do seem to work better.
Uh—certainly safer than the oral ones. So you can give the Oxymetazolam, which uh—Brandon
is Afrin or the generic version of it. You can give if there's a—a intranasal phenolephrine.

episode_title: #424 Hotcakes: Transfusion in Acute MI, COVID-19 Rebound after Paxlovid, Every-other-day Iron Revisited, Oral Phenylephrine Doesn’t Work, and Stop Correcting Serum Calcium
statement: In the MINT trial of acute MI and anemia, the primary outcome of death or MI at 30 days occurred in 16.9% (restrictive, transfuse <7) versus 14.5% (liberal, transfuse <10), a non-significant result (rate ratio 1.15, CI 0.99-1.34, p=0.07).
supporting_quote:
at 30 days, death or MI
happened in 16.9% of patients in the restrictive group and 14.5% of patients in the liberal group.
And that corresponded to a rate ratio of 1.15 with a confidence interval of 0.99 to 1.34, okay?
The P value of 0.07.

episode_title: #424 Hotcakes: Transfusion in Acute MI, COVID-19 Rebound after Paxlovid, Every-other-day Iron Revisited, Oral Phenylephrine Doesn’t Work, and Stop Correcting Serum Calcium
statement: In the MINT trial subgroup of type 1 MI (plaque rupture/vessel occlusion), a liberal transfusion strategy showed benefit with a rate ratio of 1.32, an absolute risk reduction in death or MI of 4.4%, and a number needed to treat of 23 over 30 days.
supporting_quote:
among patients with a type 1 MI,
meaning an MI due to vessel occlusion from plaque rupture, a benefit of liberal transfusion
strategy was seen. The rate ratio there was 1.32 and the confidence interval did not include
one.
And that was, uh, corresponded to an absolute risk reduction in death, uh, or MI of 4.4% and
number needed to treat of 23 over 30 days.

episode_title: #424 Hotcakes: Transfusion in Acute MI, COVID-19 Rebound after Paxlovid, Every-other-day Iron Revisited, Oral Phenylephrine Doesn’t Work, and Stop Correcting Serum Calcium
statement: Despite MINT being a negative/inconclusive primary trial, UpToDate authors now recommend a liberal transfusion strategy for acute MI with anemia, whereas prior guidelines took no stance due to lack of evidence.
supporting_quote:
in response to this study, the up-to-date authors on the relevant topic actually now
recommend a liberal transfusion strategy in this setting, even though this was a negative study.

episode_title: #424 Hotcakes: Transfusion in Acute MI, COVID-19 Rebound after Paxlovid, Every-other-day Iron Revisited, Oral Phenylephrine Doesn’t Work, and Stop Correcting Serum Calcium
statement: In MINT, a liberal transfusion strategy required about three and a half times as many units of blood as the restrictive strategy, and transfusion reactions (TACO and febrile non-hemolytic reactions) were more common in the liberal group.
supporting_quote:
People in the liberal group required about
three and a half times as many units of blood as people in the restrictive group.

episode_title: #423 Dental Pain, Caries, Gingivitis, and Oral Care Tips & Tactics for Primary Care With Dr Lisa Simon
statement: Sustained tooth pain that wakes a patient from sleep indicates infection has reached the neurovascular bundle (pulp), meaning the tooth will need a root canal or extraction rather than a filling.
supporting_quote: if someone is like in pain when they wake up for some sustained period of time, that is a tooth that is going to need either a root canal or an extraction. Because that tells me that the infection is already in the neurovascular bundle of the tooth. A filling only replaces the enamel and dentin.

episode_title: #423 Dental Pain, Caries, Gingivitis, and Oral Care Tips & Tactics for Primary Care With Dr Lisa Simon
statement: For a well-appearing patient with stable vital signs and focal dental pain, avoid ordering a CBC or CT face; a dental x-ray (or panorex) is more appropriate and useful to the treating dentist.
supporting_quote: you do not need to get a CBC. It does not matter what their white count is. And you do not need to get the kind of imaging that is generally accessible to you as an internist. It is very costly and unnecessary to get something like a CT face.

episode_title: #423 Dental Pain, Caries, Gingivitis, and Oral Care Tips & Tactics for Primary Care With Dr Lisa Simon
statement: Tooth extraction is much cheaper than a root canal (low hundreds vs often several thousand dollars), and in most states adult Medicaid covers extractions but not root canals.
supporting_quote:
a tooth extraction's going to be in the hundreds of dollars range, usually the low hundreds, and a root canal can be is almost certainly upwards of a thousand dollars, often several thousands.
It's also important to note that in most states, an adult on Medicaid will have a tooth extraction covered. They won't have to pay any of it. And in most states, a root canal will not be covered.

episode_title: #423 Dental Pain, Caries, Gingivitis, and Oral Care Tips & Tactics for Primary Care With Dr Lisa Simon
statement: For acute dental pain, a 2% lidocaine injection near the tooth roots (using a 25- or 27-gauge needle) can provide immediate local anesthesia; avoid injecting directly into an abscess as this worsens pain.
supporting_quote: I highly encourage people to think about using like a 2% lidocaine injection to provide some local anesthesia.

episode_title: #423 Dental Pain, Caries, Gingivitis, and Oral Care Tips & Tactics for Primary Care With Dr Lisa Simon
statement: For short-term dental pain control, ibuprofen 800 mg TID plus up to 3 g of acetaminophen daily is recommended, and this combination gives pain control equivalent to opioids without the risks.
supporting_quote: I would recommend 800 mg of ibuprofen TID and then up to 3 g of of acetaminophen on on a daily basis with with the proviso that that would be for a short-term pain control

episode_title: #423 Dental Pain, Caries, Gingivitis, and Oral Care Tips & Tactics for Primary Care With Dr Lisa Simon
statement: Opioids should generally be avoided for dental pain since ibuprofen and acetaminophen give equivalent control and opioids pose risks to patients and their families.
supporting_quote: we know that the science shows that we can get equivalent pain control with ibuprofen and acetaminophen. And we also know that there are demonstrable risks not just to the patient but to their whole families with having those opioids in the house.

episode_title: #423 Dental Pain, Caries, Gingivitis, and Oral Care Tips & Tactics for Primary Care With Dr Lisa Simon
statement: For a dental infection in a primary care patient without dental access, prescribing an antibiotic is a form of harm reduction that buys roughly 4-6 weeks of pain control, though it does not cure the infection without source control (dental treatment).
supporting_quote: I would really view an antibiotic prescription for your patient as a form of harm reduction. You are treating their pain until they can get to the definitive treatment.

episode_title: #423 Dental Pain, Caries, Gingivitis, and Oral Care Tips & Tactics for Primary Care With Dr Lisa Simon
statement: First-line antibiotics for a routine dental infection are penicillin VK or amoxicillin (augmentin is unnecessary); clindamycin is second-line for penicillin-allergic patients but carries C. diff risk.
supporting_quote: The one I tend to to use as amoxicillin. You don't need to go with augmentin. There is no resistance uh for these likely polymicrobial infections.

episode_title: #423 Dental Pain, Caries, Gingivitis, and Oral Care Tips & Tactics for Primary Care With Dr Lisa Simon
statement: Clindamycin is the recommended second-line agent for penicillin-allergic patients with dental infection, so the penicillin allergy should be confirmed given the C. diff risk.
supporting_quote: Clindamycin is actually the recommended second line for penicillin allergic people which I think sort of stands to your point like really make sure they're penicillin allergic cuz we don't want to throw that around for C. diff risk.

episode_title: #423 Dental Pain, Caries, Gingivitis, and Oral Care Tips & Tactics for Primary Care With Dr Lisa Simon
statement: Ludwig's angina is a true dental emergency involving infection spreading around the airway (often from a mandibular molar), presenting with trismus, tripoding, and marked submandibular swelling, and requires an immediate surgical airway.
supporting_quote: Ludwig's angina, which is sort of this classic spread. It's usually from a mandibular molar cuz the roots are very close to these fascial planes that can cut off the airway.

episode_title: #423 Dental Pain, Caries, Gingivitis, and Oral Care Tips & Tactics for Primary Care With Dr Lisa Simon
statement: Cavernous sinus thrombosis is a dental emergency, often arising from a canine tooth root, causing ascending infection with intracranial thrombosis, focal neurologic deficits, nasolabial fold flattening, and difficulty fully opening the eye.
supporting_quote: The other emergency I would mention is cavernous sinus thrombosis which is often from the root of a canine. They're the longest root so they kind of get up close to where your cavernous sinus can extend.

episode_title: #423 Dental Pain, Caries, Gingivitis, and Oral Care Tips & Tactics for Primary Care With Dr Lisa Simon
statement: Dry socket typically presents 3-4 days after extraction with worsening pain after initial improvement, is not an infection, will not respond to antibiotics, and is treated with topical measures such as iodoform gauze.
supporting_quote: someone will be feeling a little bit better and starting to heal. And maybe three or four days after they have the tooth extraction is when they experience this horrible pain. They feel a lot worse. So that sort of story makes me concerned about dry socket. It is not an infection and it won't get better with antibiotics.

episode_title: #423 Dental Pain, Caries, Gingivitis, and Oral Care Tips & Tactics for Primary Care With Dr Lisa Simon
statement: Frequency of eating acidifies the mouth, so constant sipping of sugary drinks all day increases caries risk; sugar-sweetened beverage decay appears near the gumline across all teeth.
supporting_quote: someone has a bottle of soda next to them all day long, they're going to be constantly acidifying their mouth and increasing their carries risk. Often in someone who has sort of the signs of sugar sweetened beverage decay, you will see the tooth decay on the sort of part of their teeth that's closer to the gums.

episode_title: #423 Dental Pain, Caries, Gingivitis, and Oral Care Tips & Tactics for Primary Care With Dr Lisa Simon
statement: Plain seltzer/carbonated water is generally fine for teeth because the main driver of decay is sugar being fermented into acid by bacteria, not the carbonic acid of carbonation.
supporting_quote: It's true that the carbonation in water is carbonic acid. It is acidified relative to regular water, but it's really the sugar in soda that is then fermented into acid that is like right on your tooth surface by these bacteria that is going to cause the problem. So generally speaking, I think it's totally fine

episode_title: #423 Dental Pain, Caries, Gingivitis, and Oral Care Tips & Tactics for Primary Care With Dr Lisa Simon
statement: Xerostomia can be caused by Sjogren's, smoking, anticholinergic medications (e.g., amitriptyline, antihistamines), and especially polypharmacy in older patients, warranting consideration of de-prescribing.
supporting_quote: polypharmacy is is of the biggest causes of xerostomia. So, you may not be able to find a single offending medication, but if this is something that's genuinely affecting your patient's quality of life, it's probably worth taking a step back and thinking if you want to do any de-prescribing.

episode_title: #423 Dental Pain, Caries, Gingivitis, and Oral Care Tips & Tactics for Primary Care With Dr Lisa Simon
statement: Dry mouth patients are at higher caries risk (decay near the gumline) due to lack of basic saliva; management ranges from frequent water sips, Biotene gels/rinses, and sugar-free candy up to cholinergic agents like pilocarpine, which is poorly tolerated due to severe sweating.
supporting_quote: at the most extreme end would be something like a cholinergic medication like pilocarpine, um which absolutely works, but has a number of very undesirable off-target effects, and frankly, I have never seen someone stick with taking pilocarpine because it causes such severe sweating.

episode_title: #423 Dental Pain, Caries, Gingivitis, and Oral Care Tips & Tactics for Primary Care With Dr Lisa Simon
statement: Gingivitis is inflammation of the gums that can be cleared with improved oral hygiene and needs no specialized treatment; higher estrogen levels (pregnancy, puberty) are associated with transiently increased gingivitis.
supporting_quote: having improved dental hygiene can perfectly clear up gingivitis. There is usually no need for like a specialized treatment.

episode_title: #423 Dental Pain, Caries, Gingivitis, and Oral Care Tips & Tactics for Primary Care With Dr Lisa Simon
statement: Gingival hyperplasia is most commonly caused by calcium channel blockers (e.g., nifedipine) and anti-epileptic drugs; it is painless and enlarged, and can regress with dose reduction or stopping the medication.
supporting_quote: the one that I've actually seen in clinical practice is um nifedipine and some of the other calcium channel blockers. Um anti-epileptic drugs are the other more common cause.

episode_title: #423 Dental Pain, Caries, Gingivitis, and Oral Care Tips & Tactics for Primary Care With Dr Lisa Simon
statement: For suspected TMJ dysfunction, a $20 over-the-counter night guard is as effective as the $200 dentist version, and jaw physical therapy exercises (progressively stretching mouth opening) plus heat/cold therapy can genuinely help.
supporting_quote: there is no real evidence that the $200 one from your dentist is better than the $20 one from the drugstore

episode_title: #423 Dental Pain, Caries, Gingivitis, and Oral Care Tips & Tactics for Primary Care With Dr Lisa Simon
statement: For patients on oral bisphosphonates for osteoporosis, the risk of osteonecrosis of the jaw is low and only relevant to invasive procedures like extractions; do not delay starting or withhold a needed bisphosphonate while waiting for often-inaccessible dental clearance.
supporting_quote: if you think that this is a patient who would just not have dental access for whatever reason, go ahead and start them on the bisphosphonate if that is a medication that they need to be taking for their fracture risk.

episode_title: #423 Dental Pain, Caries, Gingivitis, and Oral Care Tips & Tactics for Primary Care With Dr Lisa Simon
statement: A bisphosphonate drug holiday before dental procedures is pointless because bisphosphonates have a half-life of about 6 years; patients should continue taking them as prescribed, and fillings/cleanings do not increase osteonecrosis risk.
supporting_quote: bisphosphonates have a half-life of 6 years. So, taking a drug holiday for a week is not going to do anything um except provide a soothing placebo effect to the dentist.

episode_title: #423 Dental Pain, Caries, Gingivitis, and Oral Care Tips & Tactics for Primary Care With Dr Lisa Simon
statement: For most patients on anticoagulants or antiplatelets undergoing dental procedures, do not hold the medication; the clotting risk outweighs bleeding risk, and localized bleeding can be controlled with local hemostasis measures.
supporting_quote: with very few exceptions, the answer is don't hold anything. Keep taking it. Um there absolutely is a higher bleeding risk performing a procedure on someone who's anticoagulated, but the clotting risk for that patient is much much higher.

episode_title: #423 Dental Pain, Caries, Gingivitis, and Oral Care Tips & Tactics for Primary Care With Dr Lisa Simon
statement: For patients on warfarin, an INR should be checked within 48-72 hours before a dental procedure to ensure it is not supertherapeutic.
supporting_quote: For people who are on warfarin, um there is a recommendation to try and get an INR within 48 to 72 hours uh because if it happens to be very supertherapeutic, that that is a higher risk.

episode_title: #423 Dental Pain, Caries, Gingivitis, and Oral Care Tips & Tactics for Primary Care With Dr Lisa Simon
statement: Endocarditis antibiotic prophylaxis before dental work is indicated only for a narrow group: prosthetic cardiac valve, prosthetic material in valve repair, prior endocarditis, cardiac transplant with valvular regurgitation, or unrepaired cyanotic congenital heart disease.
supporting_quote: any prosthetic cardiac valve, a prosthetic material in cardiac valve repair, a history of endocarditis, a cardiac transplant with valvular regurgitation, or an unrepaired cyanotic congenital heart disease. So, it's pretty bad valvular things.

episode_title: #423 Dental Pain, Caries, Gingivitis, and Oral Care Tips & Tactics for Primary Care With Dr Lisa Simon
statement: There are no guidelines recommending antibiotic prophylaxis before dental work for patients with joint replacements, and it is almost certainly unnecessary and not recommended.
supporting_quote: It almost certainly is not necessary, and I would not recommend it.

episode_title: #423 Dental Pain, Caries, Gingivitis, and Oral Care Tips & Tactics for Primary Care With Dr Lisa Simon
statement: Patients on methadone or buprenorphine for opioid use disorder are at higher risk of dental decay, but this is likely due to prior substance use and lifestyle rather than the medications themselves; counsel supportively and prioritize getting them dental access.
supporting_quote: the idea that it's these medications and the transition to a life-saving medication that's causing the problem is a really frustrating one to me. That having been said, I would certainly validate patients' experiences.

episode_title: #423 Dental Pain, Caries, Gingivitis, and Oral Care Tips & Tactics for Primary Care With Dr Lisa Simon
statement: A subacute or chronic tooth abscess may drain via a sinus tract that fistulizes to the gum surface, appearing as a bubble or 'zit' whose popping relieves pain and produces a bad taste from pus.
supporting_quote: what that is is actually a sinus tract that's fistulizing from the abscess inside the bone to the outside of the mouth and that produces a huge amount of relief when it fistulizes cuz it can give that drainage and relieve the pressure.

episode_title: #418 DIGEST (Tofurkey Edition): Semaglutide for HFpEF,  TCAs for IBS, Aspirin vs Clopidogrel after PCI, Pitavastatin for HIV, Starchy Vegetables & Weight Gain, Heavy Metals in Cannabis
statement: In patients with HFpEF and obesity, weekly semaglutide improved heart failure symptoms (KCCQ-CSS) and produced greater weight loss versus placebo, though the trial was not powered for hard outcomes like heart failure hospitalization or death.
supporting_quote:
we saw that there was an improvement in
symptom and physical limitation in the
patient population that received
semaglutide greater than with placebo.
Um this was based on a Kansas City
Cardiomyopathy Theory Questionnaire
clinical summary score or the KCCQ CSS.
There were also improvements in uh body
weight change greater in the semaglutide
group than in the placebo group and that
was um minus 13.3%
with semaglutide compared to minus 2.6%
with placebo.

episode_title: #418 DIGEST (Tofurkey Edition): Semaglutide for HFpEF,  TCAs for IBS, Aspirin vs Clopidogrel after PCI, Pitavastatin for HIV, Starchy Vegetables & Weight Gain, Heavy Metals in Cannabis
statement: In the STEP-HFpEF trial, the between-group difference in KCCQ-CSS was 7.8 points, which falls within the range considered clinically significant for HFpEF.
supporting_quote:
There was a difference of 7.8 points
between the semaglutide and the placebo
group
and that was representative of an
improvement of 16 and a half points with
semaglutide compared to 8.7 points with
placebo

episode_title: #418 DIGEST (Tofurkey Edition): Semaglutide for HFpEF,  TCAs for IBS, Aspirin vs Clopidogrel after PCI, Pitavastatin for HIV, Starchy Vegetables & Weight Gain, Heavy Metals in Cannabis
statement: In the ATLANTIS trial, low-dose amitriptyline (started at 10 mg, titrated up to 30 mg) for IBS as second-line therapy in primary care showed a statistically significant but not clinically significant benefit, with a between-group difference of 27 points on the IBS-SSS (they expected 35+ for clinical significance).
supporting_quote:
The amitriptyline was
started at 10 mg and over the over 3
weeks it could be it could go up to a 30
mg as the as the maximum dose in this
trial.

episode_title: #418 DIGEST (Tofurkey Edition): Semaglutide for HFpEF,  TCAs for IBS, Aspirin vs Clopidogrel after PCI, Pitavastatin for HIV, Starchy Vegetables & Weight Gain, Heavy Metals in Cannabis
statement: In the ATLANTIS trial, baseline depression had no impact on amitriptyline treatment response and treatment did not improve depression scores, supporting the concept that TCAs work as neuromodulators reducing visceral hypersensitivity rather than via antidepressant effects.
supporting_quote:
the baseline presence of depression
had no impact on treatment response and
that that also treatment did not improve
the somatic form or the depression
score.

episode_title: #418 DIGEST (Tofurkey Edition): Semaglutide for HFpEF,  TCAs for IBS, Aspirin vs Clopidogrel after PCI, Pitavastatin for HIV, Starchy Vegetables & Weight Gain, Heavy Metals in Cannabis
statement: First-line therapies to try before second-line amitriptyline in IBS include laxatives, dietary changes, antispasmodics, and peppermint oil.
supporting_quote:
first-line therapies being things like
laxatives, um changes in diet,
antispasmodics, and peppermint oil was
also listed there.

episode_title: #418 DIGEST (Tofurkey Edition): Semaglutide for HFpEF,  TCAs for IBS, Aspirin vs Clopidogrel after PCI, Pitavastatin for HIV, Starchy Vegetables & Weight Gain, Heavy Metals in Cannabis
statement: In the HOST-EXAM trial, clopidogrel 75 mg monotherapy after DAPT following PCI had a lower rate of the primary composite endpoint (5.7% vs 7.7%) than aspirin 100 mg monotherapy, with a hazard ratio of 0.73.

episode_title: #363 Afib: Rhythm Control, Catheter Ablation, Afib in the hospital, and Left Atrial Appendage Closure
statement: The best rate control is rhythm control, so a patient presenting with newly diagnosed afib and rapid rate warrants an approach toward restoring sinus rhythm.
supporting_quote: in this case she has afib with a rapid rate the best rate control is rhythm control

statement: The longer a patient is in afib, the harder it is to restore sinus rhythm, and success of treatment such as catheter ablation drops about 20% once afib becomes long-standing persistent; early treatment offers the best chance.

episode_title: #363 Afib: Rhythm Control, Catheter Ablation, Afib in the hospital, and Left Atrial Appendage Closure
statement: The EAST trial randomized patients with early afib and risk factors to rate control versus rhythm control (mainly with drugs) and found the combined endpoint of stroke, heart attack, and death was better in the rhythm control arm, reversing the impact of the AFFIRM study.

episode_title: #363 Afib: Rhythm Control, Catheter Ablation, Afib in the hospital, and Left Atrial Appendage Closure
statement: Obesity is profoundly linked to afib, so weight loss is a critical part of the treatment strategy, and sleep apnea should be screened for.

episode_title: #363 Afib: Rhythm Control, Catheter Ablation, Afib in the hospital, and Left Atrial Appendage Closure
statement: Afib alone is not a presentation of myocardial ischemia, so routinely sending first-diagnosed afib to the ER for an ischemic workup wastes resources; escalate for extreme symptoms, syncope, chest pain, or concern for heart failure.

episode_title: #363 Afib: Rhythm Control, Catheter Ablation, Afib in the hospital, and Left Atrial Appendage Closure

episode_title: #363 Afib: Rhythm Control, Catheter Ablation, Afib in the hospital, and Left Atrial Appendage Closure

episode_title: #325 Hotcakes: Fluid Wars revisited, Sleep & Weight Loss, Vitamin D, Pneumonia and HBV Vaccine Updates
statement: The PLUS trial (~5000 ICU patients, double-blind RCT) found no significant difference between Plasma-Lyte 148 and normal saline for 90-day mortality, initiation of renal replacement therapy, or increases in creatinine.
supporting_quote: the plus trial um and uh the plus trial looked at about 5 000 patients and was double blind and randomized controlled and compared patients receiving plasmalite to patients receiving normal saline in the icu setting they followed patients over approximately 90 days

episode_title: #325 Hotcakes: Fluid Wars revisited, Sleep & Weight Loss, Vitamin D, Pneumonia and HBV Vaccine Updates
statement: In the PLUS trial, death within 90 days occurred in 21.8% of the balanced solution group and 22% of the saline group, with a point estimate close to zero, making an underpowered false-negative less likely.
supporting_quote: 21.8 percent of the balanced solution group and 22 percent of the saline group so that difference is really close to zero and the confidence interval is pretty symmetric on both sides

episode_title: #325 Hotcakes: Fluid Wars revisited, Sleep & Weight Loss, Vitamin D, Pneumonia and HBV Vaccine Updates
statement: The PLUS trial had substantial differential crossover: about 63% of patients in the balanced electrolyte group received at least a half liter of saline versus about 4% of the saline group receiving balanced electrolytes, which could bias toward a negative result.
supporting_quote: like 63 percent of patients in the balanced electrolyte group got at least a half liter of saline compared with only about four percent of patients in the normal saline group ended up getting some balanced electrolytes

episode_title: #325 Hotcakes: Fluid Wars revisited, Sleep & Weight Loss, Vitamin D, Pneumonia and HBV Vaccine Updates
statement: In an RCT of adults with overweight and short sleep (<6.5 hours/night), a sleep-hygiene counseling intervention increased sleep by about 1.2 hours, reduced energy intake by about 270 kcal/day, and produced weight loss compared with controls.
supporting_quote: they slept by about 1.2 hours more than the control group those patients consumed less energy about 270 kilocalories per day on average and then there actually was some weight loss in the group that actually had the sleep extension

episode_title: #325 Hotcakes: Fluid Wars revisited, Sleep & Weight Loss, Vitamin D, Pneumonia and HBV Vaccine Updates

episode_title: #325 Hotcakes: Fluid Wars revisited, Sleep & Weight Loss, Vitamin D, Pneumonia and HBV Vaccine Updates

episode_title: #325 Hotcakes: Fluid Wars revisited, Sleep & Weight Loss, Vitamin D, Pneumonia and HBV Vaccine Updates

episode_title: #325 Hotcakes: Fluid Wars revisited, Sleep & Weight Loss, Vitamin D, Pneumonia and HBV Vaccine Updates
statement: In the VITAL ancillary trial (~25,000 older adults, median 5-year follow-up), vitamin D with or without omega-3s produced about a 20% relative reduction in newly diagnosed autoimmune diseases.
supporting_quote: among the 25 000 patients over about a median of five years of follow-up patients randomized to vitamin d with or without omega-3s did experience about a 20 relative reduction in the incidence of newly diagnosed self-reported autoimmune diseases of any kind

episode_title: #325 Hotcakes: Fluid Wars revisited, Sleep & Weight Loss, Vitamin D, Pneumonia and HBV Vaccine Updates
statement: Despite a positive relative reduction, the vitamin D autoimmune-disease result had an absolute risk reduction of about 0.03% and a number needed to treat of roughly 700 over 5 years, suggesting any real effect is very small.

episode_title: #325 Hotcakes: Fluid Wars revisited, Sleep & Weight Loss, Vitamin D, Pneumonia and HBV Vaccine Updates
statement: When appraising trials, verify primary outcomes against the ClinicalTrials.gov registry, since studies reusing the same patients for different outcomes risk a type I error (false positive by chance).

episode_title: #325 Hotcakes: Fluid Wars revisited, Sleep & Weight Loss, Vitamin D, Pneumonia and HBV Vaccine Updates

episode_title: #309 USPSTF Quick Update: Prediabetes & Type 2 Diabetes Screening
statement: The updated USPSTF recommendation is to screen all adults ages 35 to 70 who have overweight or obesity (BMI ≥25) for prediabetes and type 2 diabetes.

episode_title: #309 USPSTF Quick Update: Prediabetes & Type 2 Diabetes Screening

episode_title: #309 USPSTF Quick Update: Prediabetes & Type 2 Diabetes Screening

blood sugar at two hours.

episode_title: #309 USPSTF Quick Update: Prediabetes & Type 2 Diabetes Screening

episode_title: #216 Homeless Healthcare with Stefan Kertesz MD

episode_title: #216 Homeless Healthcare with Stefan Kertesz MD
statement: Homelessness is a state, not a trait; roughly 60-80% of people who experience homelessness have single episodes that end, so it comes and goes rather than being permanent.
supporting_quote: homelessness is a state not a trait it's something that comes and goes so you have to kind of be sensitive the idea that people go in and out of it

episode_title: #216 Homeless Healthcare with Stefan Kertesz MD
statement: In the literature, preferred terminology has shifted toward 'persons experiencing homelessness' to reinforce that homelessness comes and goes and to keep the focus on the person.

episode_title: #216 Homeless Healthcare with Stefan Kertesz MD
statement: Mortality rates among individuals experiencing homelessness are three to nine times higher than age-matched populations in the same region.
supporting_quote: mortality rates are three to nine times higher than age matched population in the same region or area

episode_title: #216 Homeless Healthcare with Stefan Kertesz MD

episode_title: #216 Homeless Healthcare with Stefan Kertesz MD

episode_title: #216 Homeless Healthcare with Stefan Kertesz MD
statement: Building a trustworthy relationship by focusing on the patient's imminent concerns should take priority over completing standard checklists like eye exams or labs in early visits.

episode_title: #216 Homeless Healthcare with Stefan Kertesz MD

episode_title: #216 Homeless Healthcare with Stefan Kertesz MD
statement: Insulin storage is relatively robust if not in extreme heat; store it refrigerated or at least in a part of the backpack not facing the sun, per the National Healthcare for the Homeless Council.

episode_title: #216 Homeless Healthcare with Stefan Kertesz MD

episode_title: #216 Homeless Healthcare with Stefan Kertesz MD

episode_title: #216 Homeless Healthcare with Stefan Kertesz MD
statement: Harm reduction matters: any reduction in substance use, not just abstinence, can be a meaningful win that increases the chance a patient stays alive and has more chances to change.
supporting_quote: if we reduce the number of hard liquor benders to one every three months from one a month we've increased the chance that my patients going to stay alive and have more chances to figure out a new pathway

episode_title: #216 Homeless Healthcare with Stefan Kertesz MD
statement: Foot problems are highly prevalent (9-65% in cross-sectional samples), so examine the feet; low-tech interventions like providing new socks and shoes can help significantly.
supporting_quote: between 9 and 65% of cross-sectional samples of homeless individuals have significant feet problems typically calluses corns and pathologies infections

episode_title: #216 Homeless Healthcare with Stefan Kertesz MD
statement: When examining a patient's feet, ask permission and position your head lower than the patient's as a gesture of humility, being mindful of embarrassment and concerns about smell.
supporting_quote: getting down so that your head is lower than the patient's had and that you're looking at their feet is actually a gesture of humility and it helps the patient understand that you care for them and you don't hold yourself above them

episode_title: #216 Homeless Healthcare with Stefan Kertesz MD
statement: Failure to perform a foot exam risks missing serious pathology; anchoring on a social history like alcohol withdrawal can delay recognition of florid sepsis or gas gangrene in the foot.
supporting_quote: rather than sort of not not by not doing the foot exam they miss the one who actually had gas gangrene in the foot there was a surgical urgency if not emergency

episode_title: #216 Homeless Healthcare with Stefan Kertesz MD
statement: Hepatitis C can be treated with oral therapy in homeless patients with high success rates (over 80% SVR in one Boston study), but patients must be counseled that reinfection is possible even after cure.
supporting_quote: there was a study from Boston by I think it's Josh Baracus which showed a very high success rate ib over eighty percent

episode_title: #216 Homeless Healthcare with Stefan Kertesz MD
statement: The presence of serious medical illness like cirrhosis can be used as leverage to obtain housing resources, since managing such conditions is nearly impossible without stable housing.
supporting_quote: the focus should be on trying to use the presence of medical illness to wrangle resources to get that individual housed by presenting that gosh we're going to have almost an impossible time taking care of this individual unless we accommodate them

episode_title: #216 Homeless Healthcare with Stefan Kertesz MD
statement: Congregate living settings like shelters are ripe for COVID-19 outbreaks, with 30-50% testing positive when a cluster is identified, versus relatively low rates when no cluster exists yet.
supporting_quote: in some cities where there has been an identified cluster of cases they'll suddenly test everyone in a shelter and find that 30 or 50 percent are positive

episode_title: #216 Homeless Healthcare with Stefan Kertesz MD
statement: Tailoring primary care for homeless patients (extra visit time, on-site social workers, clothing closet, shower) improves patient experience and longitudinal relationships, though evidence for better health outcomes is weaker.
supporting_quote: we don't have much data yet that a tailored service model necessarily results in better health but it does result in a better experience for the patient and that often means better longitudinal relationships

episode_title: #216 Homeless Healthcare with Stefan Kertesz MD
statement: Homeless medical respite programs serve patients too sick for shelters or streets but too well for acute hospitals, and can bill Medicaid in expansion states; over 50 exist nationally.
supporting_quote: there are people who are too sick to be on the streets or in a congregate living situation and too well to be in an acute care hospital if you're in a community that's willing to do it they can invest in something called a homeless medical respite program there are over 50 such programs around the country

episode_title: #216 Homeless Healthcare with Stefan Kertesz MD
statement: Customize treatment decisions to the patient's situation by directly asking whether the proposed plan will work for them, as adult patients have common sense about what is feasible.
supporting_quote: hey I'm thinking of prescribing a B and C and proposing this treatment do you think that's going to work patients are very if they're adults they have a lot of common sense about those things

episode_title: #215 Medical Myths: Challenge Dogma with Dr. Douglas Paauw

episode_title: #215 Medical Myths: Challenge Dogma with Dr. Douglas Paauw

episode_title: #215 Medical Myths: Challenge Dogma with Dr. Douglas Paauw

episode_title: #215 Medical Myths: Challenge Dogma with Dr. Douglas Paauw

episode_title: #215 Medical Myths: Challenge Dogma with Dr. Douglas Paauw

episode_title: #215 Medical Myths: Challenge Dogma with Dr. Douglas Paauw

episode_title: #215 Medical Myths: Challenge Dogma with Dr. Douglas Paauw

episode_title: #114 High Value Care: Assess Quality, Mitigate Diagnostic Uncertainty, Overcome Barriers
statement: Value in healthcare is defined as quality divided by cost, where quality includes both immediate and long-term benefits, and cost includes both financial costs and non-financial costs like time out of work, discomfort, and suffering.
supporting_quote: value has a simple definition actually it just means quality divided by cost but I think that the nuance can get lost and that's what makes the topic a little bit harder so quality here means all of the benefit that can be derived from a particular treatment or procedure both the immediate benefits and then the longer-term benefits to the patient and then cost is really the tricky side of the equation

episode_title: #114 High Value Care: Assess Quality, Mitigate Diagnostic Uncertainty, Overcome Barriers
statement: US healthcare spending reached 3.3 trillion dollars in 2016, roughly ten thousand dollars per person and about 18 percent of GDP, with major waste contributors being unnecessary services, excess administration costs, and inefficiency.
supporting_quote: the the spending in 2016 the last time we have full numbers from was three point three trillion dollars which is and it's sort of like seeum sounds like monopoly money it's so so much it ends up being that ends up being like ten thousand dollars a person and I think a number a lot of physicians that care about this topic are familiar with is the percentage GDP number which is 18 percent of the u.s. GDP and it comes from a lot of different a lot of different causes a lot of from contributors and there's there's kind of a couple big ones unnecessary services access administration costs and an inefficiency are the three major contributors

episode_title: #114 High Value Care: Assess Quality, Mitigate Diagnostic Uncertainty, Overcome Barriers
statement: Over-ordering of tests can be categorized into three areas: knowledge/cognitive factors (like diagnostic uncertainty), process/systems factors (where it is easier to order than not), and culture (institutional and patient-driven).
supporting_quote: The the major way that we sort of categorize it is to think about three different areas where you could end up over ordering one is the knowledge and cognitive side of things and that's gonna include things like like diagnostic uncertainty for sure not knowing enough about a particular topic and maybe not have enough time to look it up and then the the other sort of categories are gonna be process related things the system's related issues where it's just easier to order things then not order it

episode_title: #114 High Value Care: Assess Quality, Mitigate Diagnostic Uncertainty, Overcome Barriers
statement: A resident-led QI project reduced unnecessary daily labs by combining EHR changes to make reordering harder with a rounds checklist item to actively discuss lab necessity with the attending each day.
supporting_quote: they said let's deal with the EHR side of things and make it a little harder to to reorder the tests let's let's make it a priority culture wise so they actually added it in as like a checklist item on rounds to remind people like we need to talk about it with the attending every day hey is it cool if we don't get a c.b.c tomorrow or hey we decided they don't need a CBC convince me otherwise and they were able to make a change a difference with that

episode_title: #114 High Value Care: Assess Quality, Mitigate Diagnostic Uncertainty, Overcome Barriers
statement: An uninsured patient facing an ACL surgery faces the entire face value bill including hospital, doctor, anesthesia, and OR fees, running in the range of $20,000 to $50,000, not including physical therapy and equipment.
supporting_quote: for an uninsured patient you know she's facing a bill for the entire face value of the surgery that's gonna include like hospital fees doctors fees anesthesia fees the o.r fees it's gonna run in the range of like 20 to $50,000 and that's not probably not including the cost of like physical therapy crutches whatever else equipment she needs down the line

episode_title: #114 High Value Care: Assess Quality, Mitigate Diagnostic Uncertainty, Overcome Barriers
statement: Government-based insurances (Medicaid and Medicare) achieve the best price negotiations because they insure so many people, giving them the biggest negotiating power to get prices down to the lowest amounts.
supporting_quote: the insurance type which gets the best negotiations is going to be the government based insurances so Medicaid for people under a certain income amount and then Medicare for people over 65 they have the biggest because they insure so many people they had the biggest negotiating power so they can really get the prices down to the lowest amount

episode_title: #114 High Value Care: Assess Quality, Mitigate Diagnostic Uncertainty, Overcome Barriers
statement: Insurance coverage is associated with better financial security, better self-reported health, earlier diagnosis and treatment (e.g., cervical cancer), more curative surgeries for colon cancer, improved blood pressure, and decreased depression.
supporting_quote: we saw that full they have an earlier earlier time to diagnosis an earlier referral to treatment for cervical cancer which has outcome implications and then also more people with colon cancer we're going for potentially curative surgeries so that prevented those preventive services seem to be making real outcome differences from the more recent Medicaid expansion data where it's a little bit mixed but the two things that they have shown at least so far is that patients that are insured and that are more insured with you know more coverage they have evidence of improved blood pressure which can have an outcome implications down the road that we may be just can't see yet and then also decreased depression

episode_title: #114 High Value Care: Assess Quality, Mitigate Diagnostic Uncertainty, Overcome Barriers

episode_title: #114 High Value Care: Assess Quality, Mitigate Diagnostic Uncertainty, Overcome Barriers

episode_title: #114 High Value Care: Assess Quality, Mitigate Diagnostic Uncertainty, Overcome Barriers

episode_title: #114 High Value Care: Assess Quality, Mitigate Diagnostic Uncertainty, Overcome Barriers

supporting_quote: for her jvd the likelihood ratio if you see it is gonna be 5.1 which which is is a is high but it's not that much higher than your overall initial clinical just Saltz the highest likelihood ratio in the in the heart failure exam is actually the third heart sound which is you know harder to hear but but that's the one that's gonna be sort of the the biggest driver and then for the testing if you have a BNP over 200 the likelihood ratio is three point seven

episode_title: #114 High Value Care: Assess Quality, Mitigate Diagnostic Uncertainty, Overcome Barriers
statement: Likelihood ratios do not multiply the probability of disease directly; roughly, an LR of 2 raises probability by about 15%, an LR of 5 by about 30%, and an LR of 10 by about 45%.

episode_title: #114 High Value Care: Assess Quality, Mitigate Diagnostic Uncertainty, Overcome Barriers
statement: A likelihood ratio nomogram lets you draw a straight line from pretest probability through the likelihood ratio to obtain post-test probability without calculations; a symptomatic outpatient with risk factors starting at 27% pretest probability of heart failure rises into the high 50s post-test with a BNP over 200.

episode_title: #139 Genetic Testing in Primary Care
statement: Direct-to-consumer BRCA testing (e.g., 23andMe) only screens for the three Ashkenazi Jewish founder mutations, so a negative result is useless and potentially misleading for non-Ashkenazi individuals.
supporting_quote: the problem with that is that they're only looking at the Ashkenazi Jewish mutations in brca1 and 2 so if you're not asking Ozzie Jewish that testing is going to be completely useless to you but you may interpret it as the fact that oh I've had brca1 and 2 testing and it was negative so that I'm fine I'm not at risk for that and that's actually not true

episode_title: #139 Genetic Testing in Primary Care
statement: The 2008 Genetic Information Nondiscrimination Act (GINA) prevents health insurers and employers from discriminating based on genetic testing, but it does NOT cover disability, long-term care, or life insurance.
supporting_quote: there's a law called Gina the genetic information non-discrimination Act which is a law that was put in place in 2008 and it says that medical insurers and employers cannot discriminate against you for a positive genetic test if you have a genetic diagnosis but that law does not is not in place for disability insurer long-term care or life insurance

episode_title: #139 Genetic Testing in Primary Care
statement: GINA protections do not apply to military members or those with military insurance, and the military collects and keeps a DNA profile on file.
supporting_quote: for individuals who are in the military or have military insurance this rule does not apply Gina does not apply to those who it doesn't protect them

episode_title: #139 Genetic Testing in Primary Care
statement: Medicare will not cover BRCA1/2 testing unless the patient has a personal history of cancer, regardless of family history or Ashkenazi Jewish ancestry.
supporting_quote: patients with Medicare will not be covered for brca1 and 2 testing unless they themselves have had a history of cancer it does not matter how concerning their family history is it doesn't matter if they're Ashkenazi Jewish and everyone but them has had cancer it is not covered by Medicare

episode_title: #139 Genetic Testing in Primary Care
statement: For deciding who meets criteria for hereditary cancer genetic testing, the NCCN guidelines (NCCN.org) provide free, summarized breast and colon cancer high-risk criteria.
supporting_quote: there is guidelines that are published that are published by the National Comprehensive Cancer Network or NCCN they have a website MC NCCN org you can sign up for free you can look at their recommendations for detection of high-risk cancer so they have one for breast cancer and one for colon cancer

episode_title: #139 Genetic Testing in Primary Care
statement: GeneReviews (genereviews.org) functions like UpToDate for genetic conditions, providing concise summaries of features, treatment, and differential diagnosis for rare diseases.
supporting_quote: gene reviews GE and E reviews org I think is an excellent it's kind of like the up to date for genetic conditions or the Google for genetic conditions where you go you put in a condition if it's in there it gives you a nice summary

episode_title: #139 Genetic Testing in Primary Care
statement: Because interpretation drives cost more than sequencing itself, cancer gene panels are usually the same price whether they include 15 genes or 2,000 genes, so a comprehensive panel is often the better choice.
supporting_quote: most of the times the labs the panels are all the same price because it doesn't actually change it's not that expensive to actually do the testing it's more about the interpretation that you're paying for so you can do 15 genes or 2,000 genes it's usually the same price

episode_title: #139 Genetic Testing in Primary Care
statement: Some insurers (e.g., Cigna, and reportedly United) now require documented genetic counseling before they will pay for genetic testing.
supporting_quote: some insurers require that the patient has had genetic counseling before they'll pay for the genetic tests and that's like Sigma is an insurer that requires that and United I think is moving in that direction

episode_title: #139 Genetic Testing in Primary Care
statement: In hereditary cancer risk assessment, a second primary cancer is genetically more concerning than a recurrence of the same cancer.
supporting_quote: if you have a second cancer versus a recurrence of cancer those are two different things coming from a genetics perspective and a second diagnosis is more concerning than a recurrence of cancer

episode_title: #139 Genetic Testing in Primary Care
statement: Whole exome/genome sequencing is analyzed based on the patient's clinical information to seek a diagnosis, so testing a healthy person hoping to 'find something' generally does not work.
supporting_quote: when you do whole exome sequencing you put in a bunch of clinical information so what are the medical history of my patient what are the surgeries that they've had what are the dysmorphic features that they have what is the family history and that guides how the lab analyzes the exome

episode_title: #139 Genetic Testing in Primary Care

supporting_quote: we talk to patients about this list of what's called secondary findings so it's a 59 gene list of conditions that are probably unrelated to the reason that the person's getting the test so those are things like brca1 and 2 other cancer genes some of the arrhythmia genes like long QT syndrome

episode_title: #169 PGY3 and Beyond with Alia Chisty MD
statement: During intern year, focus on becoming a competent internist and mastering the basics rather than prematurely planning your career path.
supporting_quote: the most important thing she could do is focus on her intern year figuring out the basics of being a newly practicing physician where her signature matters and that she can start to be open and receptive to the things that she really enjoys

episode_title: #169 PGY3 and Beyond with Alia Chisty MD
statement: Seek out multiple mentors for different needs (clinical, research, work-life balance) rather than relying on one mentor for everything; mentors can be attendings, chief residents, or near-peers.
supporting_quote: it helps the the resident actually have more than one mentor so they're not putting all their eggs maybe in one basket but they're getting different perspective from viewpoints

episode_title: #169 PGY3 and Beyond with Alia Chisty MD
statement: Be an effective mentee by bringing concrete questions to meetings and understanding your mentor's preferred communication style, which makes the relationship successful for both parties.
supporting_quote: having specific things that you kind of want addressed or help thinking through certain things or a specific advice that you're looking for that can actually really help to make that that meeting and that relationship really successful

episode_title: #169 PGY3 and Beyond with Alia Chisty MD
statement: PGY-2 is the critical year to decide on fellowship versus a generalist career, since fellowship applications occur in the summer of the third year.
supporting_quote: pgy 2 is really critical for career development because this is probably the year where she's gonna realize if she wants to pursue a fellowship if she and because the timeline is really important she's gonna need to apply at the end of you know in the summer of her third year

episode_title: #169 PGY3 and Beyond with Alia Chisty MD
statement: Signing a job contract during second year is generally too early; review any offer with a mentor first, as new graduates may not know how to advocate for themselves.
supporting_quote: I think it's a little early I mean some people I guess if you know you know

episode_title: #169 PGY3 and Beyond with Alia Chisty MD
statement: For the job search, polish your CV, cover letter, and references over the summer (July-August), then start applying in September-October to be in the first wave of interviews and offers.
supporting_quote: I usually say around September to October you want to start thinking about where you're going to be applying

episode_title: #169 PGY3 and Beyond with Alia Chisty MD
statement: If you are geographically limited, start your job search earlier and apply more broadly to all possible jobs in your target area, being prepared to be more flexible about the role.
supporting_quote: if you are geographically limited start earlier and apply kind of more broadly and apply to all of the possible jobs in that area

episode_title: #169 PGY3 and Beyond with Alia Chisty MD
statement: Involve your program director in your job search, as they can provide introductions and serve as your best advocate; a warm introduction increases the likelihood your email is prioritized.
supporting_quote: the best person who can do this for you and set up relationships for you or introductions is actually your program director

episode_title: #169 PGY3 and Beyond with Alia Chisty MD
statement: Use professional society and journal career centers (such as the ACP and New England Journal of Medicine) to explore available jobs and learn about CV polishing and employment contracts.
supporting_quote: either going to like the ACP website some of the other like the New England Journal has like a Career Center so you can actually look for different professional societies or I guess journals and actually see what the Career Center looks like

episode_title: #169 PGY3 and Beyond with Alia Chisty MD
statement: Recruiters can present opportunities you might not have considered, but remember they work for the hiring institution, not for you, and their reimbursement may come from what could have been offered to you.
supporting_quote: the recruiter works for an institution they don't work for you and there is just a thought process that maybe that person's reimbursement is gonna come from potentially part of what might not what might have been offered to you

episode_title: #169 PGY3 and Beyond with Alia Chisty MD
statement: For phone interviews, prepare a 30-second elevator pitch describing who you are, your skills, and what you're looking for in a job.
supporting_quote: I would encourage you to have a little bit of what we call an elevator pitch a 30-second spiel about who you are what your skills are what you're kind of looking for in a job

episode_title: #169 PGY3 and Beyond with Alia Chisty MD
statement: On interview day, wear comfortable shoes since you may walk a lot, bring several copies of your CV, and research the institution and interviewers beforehand.
supporting_quote: definitely wear comfortable shoes cuz you might have to walk around a whole lot I to bring few copies of your CV

episode_title: #169 PGY3 and Beyond with Alia Chisty MD
statement: Being overly polished and rehearsed in interviews is a red flag; stay authentic and don't say yes to everything just to secure a job, as the role must match who you are.
supporting_quote: when you are so polished and it seems like every single word is you're so careful choosing which word you're gonna say that you're not being authentic to who you who you are

episode_title: #169 PGY3 and Beyond with Alia Chisty MD
statement: A 1.0 clinical FTE equals a full-time job, with each 0.1 FTE representing one half-day of clinic per week; anything less than 1.0 is part-time, with the remainder typically going to admin or academic time.
supporting_quote: each 0.1 on FTE is one half day so if you are a 0.8 FTE with no inpatient essentially you're seeing clinic eight half days per week and then two half days of whatever

episode_title: #169 PGY3 and Beyond with Alia Chisty MD
statement: Academic promotion and tenure criteria are highly institution-dependent; check the institution's website and ask more junior faculty or alumni about these sensitive questions rather than only the division chief.
supporting_quote: a lot of institutions also have like have this actually available on their website so maybe you can try googling it and seeing it for that specific institution first

episode_title: #169 PGY3 and Beyond with Alia Chisty MD
statement: To ease the transition to attending, ask ahead of time to be paired with a junior faculty clinical mentor who knows the new system, since much of the challenge is learning the system rather than the medicine.
supporting_quote: try to identify ahead of time if you can like a clinical mentor just because you might be going to a place with a new system and asking your division chief to maybe pair you up with someone who's a young junior faculty member who works in the clinic

episode_title: #169 PGY3 and Beyond with Alia Chisty MD
statement: As an attending it is acceptable and normal to ask for help and not know everything; you can admit uncertainty and look things up together rather than needing to show off knowledge.
supporting_quote: People suddenly think that when they become attending they have to like be that didn't know at all like they have to know everything and that if they don't know something oh my god but that's we we ask questions

episode_title: #169 PGY3 and Beyond with Alia Chisty MD
statement: Reflect on the behaviors of your favorite educators to identify practices you can emulate as you transition into being a teaching attending.
supporting_quote: one of the best things you can do is actually reflect on the behaviors of your favorite educators what are the behaviors that they they embrace what are the different things that they're doing because those are going to be the things that you will want to emulate

episode_title: #169 PGY3 and Beyond with Alia Chisty MD
statement: Be aware that potential employers will review your social media (LinkedIn, Facebook, Twitter); anything that could discredit the institution can be a hard stop in hiring.

episode_title: #58: Concussion, traumatic brain injury, and post-concussive syndrome

episode_title: #181 The Multimorbidity Games with Josh Uy MD

episode_title: #182 Becoming a PGY3: Not Just a Repeat of PGY2

episode_title: #182 Becoming a PGY3: Not Just a Repeat of PGY2

episode_title: #182 Becoming a PGY3: Not Just a Repeat of PGY2

episode_title: #182 Becoming a PGY3: Not Just a Repeat of PGY2

episode_title: #182 Becoming a PGY3: Not Just a Repeat of PGY2

episode_title: #182 Becoming a PGY3: Not Just a Repeat of PGY2

episode_title: #186 Sarcopenia: Raising the Bar in Primary Care
statement: Sarcopenia is now considered a muscle-specific disease state (analogous to chronic skeletal muscle failure), with the definition shifting emphasis from absolute muscle mass toward muscle strength.

episode_title: #186 Sarcopenia: Raising the Bar in Primary Care

episode_title: #186 Sarcopenia: Raising the Bar in Primary Care

episode_title: #186 Sarcopenia: Raising the Bar in Primary Care

episode_title: #186 Sarcopenia: Raising the Bar in Primary Care
statement: In COPD patients, quadriceps strength is a better predictor of mortality than FEV1, and resistance training may be more feasible and beneficial than aerobic activity.
supporting_quote: in the individual COPD they're quadricep strength is actually a better predictor of mortality than fev1 is we have some data to to support that and so I would my bias for them would be to get them to resistance trained

episode_title: #186 Sarcopenia: Raising the Bar in Primary Care
statement: The 2018 Physical Activity Guidelines recommend twice-weekly resistance training plus 150–300 minutes of moderate-intensity or 75–150 minutes of vigorous-intensity aerobic activity per week, achievable in 5-minute bouts.
supporting_quote: all adults should engage in twice weekly resistance training and in addition achieve 150 to 300 minutes of moderate-intensity vigorous or moderate intensity aerobic training or 75 to 150 minutes of vigorous intensity aerobic training

episode_title: #186 Sarcopenia: Raising the Bar in Primary Care
statement: Exercise intensity can be communicated via the talk test: able to talk in complete sentences but not sing = moderate intensity; able only to speak in short sentences with breathlessness = vigorous intensity.
supporting_quote: if you can talk in complete sentences but not but not sing that qualifies as moderate intensity aerobic training and then if you can barely speak in short sentences so a little bit more breathlessness that qualifies as vigorous intensity aerobic training

episode_title: #186 Sarcopenia: Raising the Bar in Primary Care
statement: The 10,000 steps-per-day target is based on marketing, not evidence; over 4,000 steps/day is associated with lower all-cause mortality and CVD, with further benefit at 7,500 steps/day.
supporting_quote: the 10,000 steps per day thing is not based on evidence that's based on marketing the only data we have on steps that if you achieve over 4,000 steps per day that seems to be associated with lower risk of all-cause mortality and AAS CVD but then actually that's like the minimum threshold and then if you increase that to 7500 steps per day you get in further reduction in AAS CVD

episode_title: #186 Sarcopenia: Raising the Bar in Primary Care
statement: The injury risk from resistance training is very low (about 1–2 injuries per 1,000 participation hours), comparable to walking (about 1.2 per 1,000 hours), and recommending walking alone is an underdosed intervention for sarcopenia.
supporting_quote: the injury risk from resistance training is extremely low about one to two injuries per thousand participation hours which is exceedingly low compared to the risks of being sedentary or not engaging in the physical activity and further you go further there's actually a non zero risk of engaging in like walking walking as injury rates as high as one point two injuries per thousand participation hours

episode_title: #186 Sarcopenia: Raising the Bar in Primary Care
statement: The APTA Choosing Wisely guidelines advise against prescribing underdosed resistance training programs to older adults, as underdosing is a common problem with medically prescribed exercise.
supporting_quote: if you go to the choosing wisely guidelines from the American Physical Therapy Association one of their specific choosing wisely guidelines is don't prescribe under dosed resistance training programs to older adults

episode_title: #186 Sarcopenia: Raising the Bar in Primary Care
statement: For sarcopenic CKD patients advised to limit protein, favor protein sources high in essential amino acids, or consider essential amino acid supplementation, which has shown benefit with resistance training in small RCTs.
supporting_quote: if you have an older patient with chronic kidney disease and you know the recommendation they get from their nephrologist is to decrease their dietary protein intake you would probably make a stronger case for eating protein sources that have a lot of essential amino acids so that may include a supplement or they might actually supplement with essential amino acids there's actually been studied in a handful of randomized controlled trials

episode_title: #186 Sarcopenia: Raising the Bar in Primary Care
statement: High protein intake is not associated with harm in healthy individuals; long-term data show intakes up to 4.4 g/kg/day for a year without adverse health outcomes (though GI distress may occur).
supporting_quote: with respect to dietary protein intake we have long-term data this is up to a year where people were actually in 4.4 grams per kilogram body weight of protein per day so a lot so if you had 100 kilo person they're eating 440 grams of dietary protein per day

episode_title: #186 Sarcopenia: Raising the Bar in Primary Care
statement: When recommending protein supplements, look for a cGMP stamp (contents match label, low contamination risk) and, for drug-tested athletes, an NSF or Informed Sport certification.
supporting_quote: you would want a stamp from the certificate of good manufacturing processes so cGMP that basically means that a what's on the label it's in the thing and nothing else so contamination is low concern there and then if you happen to have a master's level athlete or other you know athletic person who might be drug test a theory you would want an additional certification from like the NSF or inform sport

episode_title: #186 Sarcopenia: Raising the Bar in Primary Care
statement: A practical way to communicate protein targets is a palm-sized portion (~25-35 g / 4-5 oz protein) at each of 3-5 meals per day to reach recommended intake.
supporting_quote: if you have a palm-sized portion of protein at every meal ideally you're having you know three to four or up to five meals per day that'll give you about four to five ounces of protein and each one of those is gonna be about 25 2 to 35 grams of protein

episode_title: #186 Sarcopenia: Raising the Bar in Primary Care
statement: Chronic inflammatory and disease states cause anabolic resistance—a subnormal muscle-building response to dietary protein and exercise—which makes secondary sarcopenia harder to treat and often requires increasing the dose of intervention.
supporting_quote: any disease state particularly like inflammatory diseases you will not only be caught it can can be a sort of causative element for sarcopenia by increasing what's known as anabolic resistance effectively means the patient has a sub normal response to anabolic stimuli either dietary or exercise related so effectively they build less muscle mass

episode_title: #186 Sarcopenia: Raising the Bar in Primary Care
statement: Even with underdosed resistance training (bands and light ankle weights) plus essential amino acids, an RCT showed sarcopenic patients gained ~3% muscle mass, ~9% muscle strength, and normalized gait speed within 3 months.
supporting_quote: within a year are started the this one particular study which was the first follow-up was at 3 months they gained 3% muscle mass 9% muscle strength and decrease or increase their gait speed to be normal based on the the gait speed test

episode_title: #186 Sarcopenia: Raising the Bar in Primary Care
statement: There is large inter-individual variability in response to resistance training across ages and sexes; setting achievable physical function goals is more useful than promising specific strength gains.
supporting_quote: what we see consistently across everything is this enormous inter-individual variability in terms of responsiveness to training meaning that some people you know if you put a hundred people on a given training program you might see some people not do so well some people might do remarkably well and most people fall somewhere in between

episode_title: REBOOT Depression and Physician Suicide w/Dr Poorman
statement: Depression and suicide are occupational hazards of training and practicing medicine, given the inherently traumatic nature of caring for the sick and dying.
supporting_quote: depression and suicide are occupational hazards of training and practicing medicine

episode_title: REBOOT Depression and Physician Suicide w/Dr Poorman
statement: Physicians should connect with a therapist proactively—starting in medical school and before a crisis—since it can take time to find someone you connect with and you may lack energy once depressed.
supporting_quote:
everybody going into medicine and and
actually starting in medical school
should connect with a therapist knowing
that they're going to you know face
these kind of traumatic situations

episode_title: REBOOT Depression and Physician Suicide w/Dr Poorman
statement: Substance abuse is common among physicians beginning in residency; clinicians should reflect on the role alcohol is playing in their lives, especially during training.
supporting_quote:
substance abuse is incredibly
common in in physicians starting and
residency

episode_title: REBOOT Depression and Physician Suicide w/Dr Poorman

28
states ask if you have ever been treated

episode_title: REBOOT Depression and Physician Suicide w/Dr Poorman
statement: At the start of intern year only about 4% of interns meet criteria for clinical depression, but at some point during intern year 41.8% will have clinical depression.

when we start intern year only 4% of us

episode_title: REBOOT Depression and Physician Suicide w/Dr Poorman
statement: Sleep deprivation is an independent risk factor for suicidal thoughts, which should inform how we structure work hours and whether we shame residents for napping.
supporting_quote:
what sleep deprivation is is an
independent risk factor for suicidal
thoughts particularly so I think when

episode_title: REBOOT Depression and Physician Suicide w/Dr Poorman
statement: By the time a colleague is visibly making mistakes or struggling at work, they have likely been sick for a long time, because physicians prioritize work above everything else.

by the time people are making

episode_title: REBOOT Depression and Physician Suicide w/Dr Poorman

taking that extra step to also just say like hey are you having

episode_title: REBOOT Depression and Physician Suicide w/Dr Poorman
statement: Depression and suicidal thoughts often coexist but do not have to; passive suicidal ideation—reckless behavior or wishing something would happen—is common and reported by many physicians during training and early career.

suicidal
thoughts and depression often coexist

episode_title: REBOOT Depression and Physician Suicide w/Dr Poorman
statement: Highest burnout on surveys is typically seen in younger, early-career physicians and trainees.

the highest burnout is typically seen in

episode_title: REBOOT Depression and Physician Suicide w/Dr Poorman
statement: The doubling time of medical knowledge has shrunk from about 7 years in 1980 to closer to 73 days, contributing to work compression and the strain on physicians.
supporting_quote:
in 1980
and you know doubling time medical
