episode_title: REBOOT Depression and Physician Suicide w/Dr Poorman
statement: Residents spend roughly seven minutes of face time per patient while doing about two hours of documentation for every hour of clinical care.

residents
spent just seven minutes of face time

episode_title: REBOOT Depression and Physician Suicide w/Dr Poorman
statement: The term 'burnout' can be a dangerous euphemism when patients are actually clinically depressed, and it can give administrations cover to treat the problem as less serious; naming depression conveys appropriate severity.

I sometimes see people who are clearly

episode_title: REBOOT Depression and Physician Suicide w/Dr Poorman
statement: On licensing applications, do not lie about mental health history but disclose only what is required; being appropriately treated for depression does not mean you cannot safely practice medicine.

don't lie because that puts you in a

episode_title: REBOOT Depression and Physician Suicide w/Dr Poorman
statement: Debriefing sessions for residents should be run by mental health professionals who have no evaluative role in the residency, so trainees are not pressured about what they share.

these sessions importantly

episode_title: REBOOT Depression and Physician Suicide w/Dr Poorman
statement: Program directors and chief residents should not require permission or notification to attend therapy, as such requirements become unintentional barriers that deter people who most need care.

program directors

episode_title: #152 Dare to Lead: Becoming a PGY2
statement: Set clear, explicit expectations with your team upfront, as frustration most often arises when an unsaid expectation goes unmet.
supporting_quote: I think the time when when interns our students become most frustrated with their residents is when they haven't met an unsaid expectation

episode_title: #152 Dare to Lead: Becoming a PGY2
statement: Adapt your leadership approach based on whether an intern is able and/or willing: build up the confidence of the able-but-unwilling, avoid micromanaging the able-and-willing, and set specific triggers/close supervision for the unable-but-willing, who are the most dangerous combination.
supporting_quote:
the ones who aren't able for whatever
reason yet are willing are
probably the most dangerous combination

episode_title: #152 Dare to Lead: Becoming a PGY2
statement: For interns you supervise closely, give explicit call parameters (e.g., hypotension, tachycardia, angry family, angry consultant, crucial lab value) so they know when to reach you.
supporting_quote:
I like to be called for hypotension below
this tachycardia above this angry
family's angry consultants crucial lab
value you know for my own neuroses these
are the things I want to hear about

episode_title: #152 Dare to Lead: Becoming a PGY2
statement: Avoid pre-labeling interns as 'good' or 'bad'—ability and willingness are situational and rotation-specific, not intrinsic properties, so meet each learner where they are.
supporting_quote:
labeling the intern as a good
intern a bad intern the strong intern
the A+ intern because it's so
situational the intern that is
completely able and willing on their
cardiology rotation may be a completely
different person on the nephrology

episode_title: #152 Dare to Lead: Becoming a PGY2
statement: Be deliberate about which rooms you enter to teach and what the teaching purpose is; teaching every teachable moment makes rounds too long, while missing them means no teaching happens.
supporting_quote:
sometimes there's so many teachable
moments that if you take advantage of
all of them
rounds go too long the interns are you
know will start resenting you because
they need to finish their notes

episode_title: #152 Dare to Lead: Becoming a PGY2
statement: Always explicitly label feedback as feedback, because if you're not explicit people mistake it for casual conversation or compliments.
supporting_quote:
if you're not
absolutely explicit about that people
think that is conscious passing you're

their presentation was

episode_title: #152 Dare to Lead: Becoming a PGY2
statement: When addressing an intern who is struggling, first assess their level of insight with open then specific questions, and frame the conversation around whether the patient received our best rather than the learner's ego.

if we bring it back to did

episode_title: #152 Dare to Lead: Becoming a PGY2
statement: Frame corrective feedback as a collaborative 'next time try' rather than 'you did this wrong,' inviting the learner to problem-solve alongside you.

instead of you did

episode_title: #152 Dare to Lead: Becoming a PGY2
statement: The highest-yield studying strategy is reading about your own patients and their specific unanswered clinical questions, which sticks far better than reading a textbook cover to cover.

read about your patients and I really do believe in
that I think that sometimes in in med

episode_title: #152 Dare to Lead: Becoming a PGY2
statement: Review articles such as Annals 'In the Clinic' and NEJM/Annals reviews are excellent for building the breadth of basics and clinical reasoning, before drilling into primary literature.
supporting_quote:
I love the review articles whether
they're new england journal or annals i

episode_title: #152 Dare to Lead: Becoming a PGY2
statement: Residents and students should focus on question banks, textbook knowledge, and review articles rather than trying to scan the primary literature—keeping up with primary journals is largely an attending-level task.
supporting_quote:
medical
students and residents should largely be

episode_title: #152 Dare to Lead: Becoming a PGY2
statement: Always close the loop when you assign a learner to look something up; if they never get to report back, they will stop doing the reading.

episode_title: #152 Dare to Lead: Becoming a PGY2
statement: As a resident-leader, keep the intern approachable to you—if trainees stop bringing you problems, they either think you don't care or can't help, so respond to every problem with 'thank you.'

if your troops stop bringing you their

episode_title: #121 HIV Care for the Internist
statement: Anyone who is sexually active should be screened for HIV at least once, because the test is straightforward, false positives are vanishingly rare, and early diagnosis has strong benefits.

my short answer is that anyone

episode_title: #121 HIV Care for the Internist
statement: For screening in a longitudinal primary care setting, order the standard fourth-generation HIV 1/2 antibody/antigen test, which detects both established infection and the P24 antigen present in acute infection.

I would just order the standard it's

episode_title: #121 HIV Care for the Internist

so what should what would you

episode_title: #121 HIV Care for the Internist
statement: The P24 antigen turns positive around 15 to 20 days (as early as 12 days) after infection, HIV RNA around day 10, and antibody around day 30.

statement: Rapid oral HIV tests are close but slightly less accurate (about 98%) than fourth-generation serum tests (99.5-99.7%); a negative rapid test in a higher-risk patient should be followed up with a standard test.

episode_title: #121 HIV Care for the Internist

episode_title: #138 Inflammatory Bowel Disease: Crohn’s and Ulcerative Colitis

episode_title: #138 Inflammatory Bowel Disease: Crohn’s and Ulcerative Colitis

episode_title: #138 Inflammatory Bowel Disease: Crohn’s and Ulcerative Colitis

episode_title: #138 Inflammatory Bowel Disease: Crohn’s and Ulcerative Colitis

episode_title: #138 Inflammatory Bowel Disease: Crohn’s and Ulcerative Colitis

episode_title: #138 Inflammatory Bowel Disease: Crohn’s and Ulcerative Colitis

episode_title: #138 Inflammatory Bowel Disease: Crohn’s and Ulcerative Colitis

episode_title: #138 Inflammatory Bowel Disease: Crohn’s and Ulcerative Colitis

episode_title: #138 Inflammatory Bowel Disease: Crohn’s and Ulcerative Colitis

episode_title: #138 Inflammatory Bowel Disease: Crohn’s and Ulcerative Colitis

episode_title: #138 Inflammatory Bowel Disease: Crohn’s and Ulcerative Colitis

episode_title: #138 Inflammatory Bowel Disease: Crohn’s and Ulcerative Colitis

episode_title: #138 Inflammatory Bowel Disease: Crohn’s and Ulcerative Colitis

episode_title: #138 Inflammatory Bowel Disease: Crohn’s and Ulcerative Colitis

episode_title: #138 Inflammatory Bowel Disease: Crohn’s and Ulcerative Colitis

episode_title: #138 Inflammatory Bowel Disease: Crohn’s and Ulcerative Colitis

episode_title: #60: Masters of MKSAP on Medical Education

episode_title: #60: Masters of MKSAP on Medical Education

episode_title: #124 The Shoulder – Simplify Your Approach
statement: Rotator cuff disease is the most common cause of shoulder pain presenting to primary care; if unsure, guessing rotator cuff pathology will be correct the vast majority of the time.
supporting_quote: if you guessed rotator cuff pathology in your primary care patient you're going to be right like the vast majority of the time it's a good place to start if you're not quite sure just figure it's probably the rotator cuff

episode_title: #124 The Shoulder – Simplify Your Approach
statement: Shoulder differential can be crafted by age: patients under 40 with cuff problems more likely have tendinitis, while at 40 and older the risk of an atraumatic rotator cuff tendon tear rises.
supporting_quote: a younger person under 40 would most likely if they have cuff problems rotator cuff problems would have tendinitis but as you get to be 40 and older the risk of tearing your rotator cuff even in the a traumatic setting goes up

episode_title: #124 The Shoulder – Simplify Your Approach
statement: A recent shoulder dislocation in a patient over 40 carries a very high risk of a torn rotator cuff tendon, while a distant history of dislocation raises concern for shoulder arthritis.
supporting_quote: anyone who's had a shoulder dislocation whether if recent and they're over 40 they have a very very high risk of having torn a rotator cuff tendon if distant history of shoulder dislocation of course you worry about shoulder arthritis

episode_title: #124 The Shoulder – Simplify Your Approach
statement: Pain that worsens reaching overhead suggests shoulder pathology, whereas cervical radiculopathy pain improves with the hand above/on top of the head (relieving nerve tension), and any pain radiating past the elbow points away from the shoulder.

shoulder pain
it gets worse when you reach overhead I think we all realize that you know people say oh you know when I'm cooking or reaching in the cabinet in the kitchen it'll hurt a cervical radiculopathy will get better when you put your hand above your head and people will sometimes describe actually putting their hand on top of their head to provide relief and so that's a real tip-off that this is a cervical problem

episode_title: #124 The Shoulder – Simplify Your Approach
statement: Localize shoulder pain by location: impingement/rotator cuff disease usually causes pain deep to the deltoid laterally, AC joint pain is on top of the shoulder, and posterior shoulder pain (rhomboids, neck, scapula) should prompt evaluation of the cervical spine.

most impingement syndrome and rotator cuff disease manifests as pain laterally so deep to the deltoid where as a SI joint pain will be on the top of the shoulder
and posterior shoulder pain you should really start thinking about cervical spine ideologies so anything around the rhomboids the back of the neck or the scapula start to think about the neck

episode_title: #124 The Shoulder – Simplify Your Approach
statement: Use the HIP-ROT framework for musculoskeletal exam: History, Inspection, Palpation, Range of motion, Other tests.
supporting_quote: the framework I like to use is called hip rot which stands for history inspection palpation range of motion other tests so hip rot which can be applied to anything

episode_title: #124 The Shoulder – Simplify Your Approach
statement: When both active AND passive range of motion are decreased in an atraumatic shoulder in clinic, the diagnosis is essentially either glenohumeral joint arthritis or frozen shoulder; get an x-ray to distinguish these and rule out rare causes like tumor.
supporting_quote:
if both their active and passive range of motion is decreased then you really only have to diagnosis again in the clinic setting that you'd be worried about one would be glenohumeral joint arthritis and the other would be frozen shoulder
and you know you can distinguish those things easily with a radiograph

episode_title: #124 The Shoulder – Simplify Your Approach
statement: Frozen shoulder typically shows a normal x-ray, whereas glenohumeral osteoarthritis shows joint space narrowing, osteophytes, subchondral cysts, and subchondral sclerosis.
supporting_quote: frozen shoulder actually would tend to have a normal x-ray whereas going to humeral joint arthritis would have your typical x-ray findings of osteoarthritis so joint space narrowing osteophytes subchondral assists and subchondral sclerosis

episode_title: #124 The Shoulder – Simplify Your Approach
statement: Frozen shoulder affects about 20% of diabetics, is 5 times more common in women (typically ages 40–60), and progresses through freezing, frozen, and thawing stages each lasting roughly 6–9 months, taking up to 2–3 years to resolve.
supporting_quote: frozen shoulder affects twenty percent of diabetics

episode_title: #124 The Shoulder – Simplify Your Approach
statement: In frozen shoulder, a steroid injection helps pain but does not normalize range of motion or expedite recovery, so if a patient's range of motion becomes normal after a steroid shot, the diagnosis was actually impingement syndrome, not frozen shoulder.
supporting_quote: in frozen shoulder it actually takes a year or two for the range of motion to normalize the steroid injection helps the pain but it does not normalize the range of motion so in that case I know that I got it wrong and it was actually impingement syndrome and the cortisone did it it helped their pain and now they can fully range

episode_title: #124 The Shoulder – Simplify Your Approach
statement: Calcific tendonitis presents with extremely acute severe pain (patients often go to the ER) and resolves quickly and effectively with NSAIDs or a cortisone shot; the injection can even make the calcium go away and be curative.

that's that's calcific tendonitis calcific tendonitis has
and extremely acute onset people will wake up and it will be so painful they actually go to the hospital and then it resolves very quickly and effectively with n sets or with a cortisone shot

episode_title: #124 The Shoulder – Simplify Your Approach

there is some evidence with frozen shoulder that an ultrasound guided

episode_title: #124 The Shoulder – Simplify Your Approach
statement: The key primary care task in atraumatic shoulder pain with intact passive range of motion is to identify full-thickness rotator cuff tears (which need surgical referral); everyone else can go to physical therapy.
supporting_quote: the mission is to refer the patient with the full thickness rotator cuff tear to the surgeon and everyone else can go to physical therapy

episode_title: #124 The Shoulder – Simplify Your Approach
statement: In the empty can (Jobs) test, weakness raises concern for a full-thickness tear, while pain alone suggests tendinitis or a partial tear; the arm should be positioned about 30 degrees anterior to abduction (roughly 10 o'clock on a clock face).
supporting_quote: the idea is if they're weak you're worried about a full thickness tear if they just have pain and you can call it tendonitis or a partial tear so that's empty can some people call that the job's test

episode_title: #124 The Shoulder – Simplify Your Approach
statement: The internal and external rotation lag tests—where the examiner positions the arm and the patient fails to hold it against gravity—have a high positive predictive value for a full-thickness rotator cuff tear.
supporting_quote: the lag test is basically the exam and removes the patient's hand into that position and then let's go and asks the patient to maintain that position and if your patient falls out of the position that means they have a positive internal rotation lag test and it turns out that that has a very high positive predictive value for a full thickness rotator cuff tear

episode_title: #124 The Shoulder – Simplify Your Approach
statement: No rotator cuff physical exam test reliably maps to a specific muscle/tendon; for primary care purposes, interpret a positive test as indicating a full-thickness tear rather than trying to localize which tendon.
supporting_quote: none of the rotator cuff tests have been very well validated to map to a particular tendon and furthermore you know we're not in the job of fixing those tendons like you know you're gonna get an MRI for that part of the job

episode_title: #124 The Shoulder – Simplify Your Approach
statement: The painful arc maneuver—passive abduction producing pain between 60 and 120 degrees—increases the likelihood of rotator cuff (cuff) disease and should be done before the tests for full-thickness tear.
supporting_quote: the painful arc is one which essentially as what happens in the pain Park is the examiner passively abducts the arm and you're looking for the patient to have pain between 60 and 120 degrees of abduction because that's the area of where the rotator cuff starts to pinch it and have pain

episode_title: #124 The Shoulder – Simplify Your Approach
statement: Hawkins and Neer's tests assess for impingement/bursitis, with a positive test producing pain deep to the deltoid.
supporting_quote: so if that's negative then i'll go on into a second impingement in maneuver which is Neer's and I remember nears because it's near by the ear

episode_title: #124 The Shoulder – Simplify Your Approach
statement: For impingement syndrome without a full-thickness tear, either physical therapy or a steroid injection is reasonable; a VA study showed both approaches resulted in patients getting at least 50% better over the course of a year.

there was a study out of the VA showing that you could do either you could give physical therapy or you could give injections and basically

episode_title: #124 The Shoulder – Simplify Your Approach

episode_title: #124 The Shoulder – Simplify Your Approach
statement: Follow up shoulder patients treated conservatively at 6–8 weeks; if they are not improving, consider an MRI to look for a full-thickness tear and referral, since patients can recruit other muscles to fake out exam maneuvers.
supporting_quote: I like to see these patients back in 6 to 8 weeks to make sure that they're improving and the reason to do that is that you know we have these physical exam maneuvers but people can fake you out because you can recruit other muscles to pass these exam tests

episode_title: #124 The Shoulder – Simplify Your Approach
statement: Palpate the AC joint on all patients (especially middle-aged and older) because AC joint arthritis is a very common, often asymptomatic radiographic finding and a great mimicker that can make impingement maneuvers and range of motion painful; cross-body adduction reproduces AC joint pain.
supporting_quote: it's really important to palpate that on all of your patients especially our middle-aged and older patients because it is an extremely common radiographic finding often asymptomatic

episode_title: #124 The Shoulder – Simplify Your Approach
statement: Cross-body (cross-arm) adduction that reproduces pain in a patient tender over the AC joint essentially confirms AC joint pathology.
supporting_quote: if you're patient points to their AC joint they're tender there and they have positive cross body adduction that's like great you're done

episode_title: #124 The Shoulder – Simplify Your Approach
statement: Refer a full-thickness rotator cuff tendon tear to a surgeon promptly, because with time the tendon retracts, the muscle becomes atrophic and fat-infiltrated, and the repair becomes more likely to fail.
supporting_quote: the longer the tendon is stored the harder it is to repair and so for that reason you know when you identify a full thickness rotator cuff tendon tear I think it's good to put in a referral to the surgeon at that time

episode_title: #124 The Shoulder – Simplify Your Approach

episode_title: #101: Cirrhosis: Medications, decompensation, complications
statement: Ascites results from increased hydrostatic pressure in the hepatic sinusoids from portal hypertension combined with low oncotic pressure from decreased albumin production by the failing liver.
supporting_quote: if you increase hydrostatic pressure in a capillary what's it going to do it's going to push more fluid out of that capillary and then you combine that with decreased oncotic pressure because what is the major oncotic aging of the blood albumin and who makes albumin the liver

episode_title: #101: Cirrhosis: Medications, decompensation, complications
statement: A strict low-sodium diet alone is often enough to control mild ascites; if not, add diuretics.
supporting_quote: a very strict low sodium diet alone is often enough to control mild ascites okay but if that doesn't control it then the next thing we move to our diuretics

episode_title: #101: Cirrhosis: Medications, decompensation, complications
statement: For ascites, a standard starting diuretic regimen is 40 mg furosemide plus 100 mg spironolactone daily, kept in a 40:100 ratio to maintain potassium balance, then titrated up (e.g., 80/200, 120/300, 160/400).
supporting_quote: step one diuretics is like 40 milligrams of furosemide and 100 milligrams a day of spironolactone and that's what we'll put them on we like that ratio because it's in seems to keep people's potassium level fairly stable

episode_title: #101: Cirrhosis: Medications, decompensation, complications
statement: In ascites you would not start furosemide by itself; spironolactone alone is preferred as a starting agent and is a very powerful drug for ascites, though it can cause hyperkalemia, sexual dysfunction, and gynecomastia.
supporting_quote: I don't typically start puros amide by itself if anything you would you would with an acidic patient you would start spironolactone by itself

episode_title: #101: Cirrhosis: Medications, decompensation, complications
statement: Ammonia levels do not correlate well with hepatic encephalopathy and are essentially useless in cirrhotic patients; encephalopathy is a clinical diagnosis.
supporting_quote: in a cirrhotic patient ammonia levels and drawing ammonia levels is essentially pointless and worthless that do not correlate well with levels of hepatic encephalopathy

episode_title: #101: Cirrhosis: Medications, decompensation, complications
statement: Drawing an ammonia level is appropriate in an encephalopathic patient with no obvious liver disease, and it has prognostic significance in acute liver failure for predicting brainstem herniation risk.
supporting_quote: if you have a patient whose encephalopathic with no obvious underlying liver disease ammonia level is appropriate to draw in that circumstance and it also has prognostic significance in a acute liver failure patient it can tell us who's at risk for brain stem herniation

episode_title: #101: Cirrhosis: Medications, decompensation, complications
statement: Rifaximin is second-line for hepatic encephalopathy (mainly due to cost), dosed 550 mg twice daily, with strong data showing decreased encephalopathy episodes.
supporting_quote: the second line agent is rifaximin rifaximin is a wonderful drug okay a habit ologist little rifaximin clearly shown in the big study in the New England Journal to decrease encephalopathy episodes

episode_title: #101: Cirrhosis: Medications, decompensation, complications
statement: Diuretics, lactulose, and rifaximin are not started prophylactically at cirrhosis diagnosis; they are started after ascites develops (diuretics) or after covert or overt encephalopathy develops.
supporting_quote: we don't we don't typically start those medicines until someone has developed encephalopathy whether its covert encephalopathy or overt encephalopathy

episode_title: #101: Cirrhosis: Medications, decompensation, complications
statement: An early sign of hepatic encephalopathy is reversed sleep-wake cycles; ask if patients are up all night and sleeping all day.
supporting_quote: one of the earliest things you'll see is what reverse hold the sleep/wake cycles so so I always ask them are you up all night watching cooking shows and then you sleep all day

episode_title: #101: Cirrhosis: Medications, decompensation, complications
statement: Per Baveno VI guidance, discontinue beta blockers in cirrhosis if systolic blood pressure is less than 90, sodium is below 120, or the patient has acute kidney injury.
supporting_quote: you should discontinue the beta blocker if your patients systolic blood pressure is less than 90 or their sodium levels with some 120 or they have acute kidney injury

episode_title: #101: Cirrhosis: Medications, decompensation, complications
statement: Beta blockers in cirrhosis are used for variceal prophylaxis, and must be non-selective so that beta-2 blockade produces unopposed alpha-mediated splanchnic vasoconstriction, decreasing portal inflow and pressure.
supporting_quote: the data to blockade will actually lead to unopposed alpha basal constriction of that dilated splanchnic mesenteric vasculature that I've talked about and that decreases portal inflow and decreases portal pressure and that's why we use non-selective beta blockers

episode_title: #101: Cirrhosis: Medications, decompensation, complications
statement: Midodrine plus octreotide is reserved for type 1 hepatorenal syndrome; most of the world uses terlipressin, but it is not approved in the United States.
supporting_quote: mitad you're in and certainly mitad renowned octreotide a reserved for the patient with type one without a renal syndrome

episode_title: #101: Cirrhosis: Medications, decompensation, complications
statement: Treating a hepatitis C patient with early cirrhosis will likely decrease or eliminate the need for transplant and decreases the risk of liver cancer, so early detection by primary care is paramount.
supporting_quote: treatment of a hepatitis C patient with early cirrhosis will likely decrease or eliminate their need for transplant it decreases their risk for liver cancer getting to these folks early is key

some of the

episode_title: #196 LIVE! Refugee Health with Tanuja Devaraj MD
statement: Refugees, asylees/asylum seekers, and migrants differ legally: a refugee has fled persecution and been granted legal protected status, an asylee is fleeing persecution but has not yet been granted protection and is going through a prolonged legal process, and a migrant moves for educational, economic, or family opportunities.
supporting_quote: so refugee is anyone who has fled their country and due to persecution or war or violence once they have fled to a neighboring country they are granted refugee status by the UNHCR or by the host country and they're protected legally to have basic rights to shelter education and health care

episode_title: #196 LIVE! Refugee Health with Tanuja Devaraj MD
statement: The pre-departure visa medical examination is a broad general physical and mental exam looking for active TB, serious mental illness, and other glaring illnesses; it does not screen for latent TB or chronic diseases.
supporting_quote: it's the initial checkpoint or examined is a very like broad generalized exam making sure that there's no active TB or like schizophrenia or like obvious and glaring illnesses that prevent someone from successfully migrating and also to protect like the population from transmitting infectious disease so it's a very general physical and mental exam they don't screen for latent TB or chronic diseases or anything like that

episode_title: #196 LIVE! Refugee Health with Tanuja Devaraj MD
statement: The CDC initial domestic medical exam screens for latent/active TB, hepatitis B, and syphilis, tests for chlamydia and gonorrhea in high-risk patients, updates vaccinations, and may include a CBC and BMP with glucose; HIV is no longer recommended pre-departure but is recommended post-arrival.
supporting_quote: it's looking for latent TB or active TB HIV is no longer recommended pre-departure but it is recommended post arrival hepatitis B and syphilis and then for chlamydia gonorrhea for high-risk and then you can consider a CBC a BMP with glucose to look for chronic illness or diseases and then updating their vaccinations as well

episode_title: #196 LIVE! Refugee Health with Tanuja Devaraj MD
statement: Since records of prior screening are often unavailable and guidelines are inconsistently implemented, providers should try to obtain records and repeat tests or preventive screenings that cannot be confirmed.
supporting_quote: I keep in mind what's recommended and I actually repeat and most providers do who do primary care for this population will try to get records and what they can't confirm will repeat a lot of these tests or initial preventive screenings

episode_title: #196 LIVE! Refugee Health with Tanuja Devaraj MD
statement: For vaccinations, Tdap and MMR have usually been given prior to departure, but hepatitis B, varicella, Pneumovax, and flu shots often have not; for hepatitis B and varicella, check titers rather than automatically revaccinating.
supporting_quote: like Tdap and MMR for adults most patients have received those prior to departure just because of the programs and healthcare opportunities in their refugee camps but hepatitis B is not often given varicella is not often given Pneumovax --is flu shots are

episode_title: #196 LIVE! Refugee Health with Tanuja Devaraj MD
statement: Refugees have federal medical assistance for about eight months after resettlement, after which many become uninsured or struggle to apply for Medicaid, leading to fragmented care and often presenting with uncontrolled chronic conditions and multiple complaints.
supporting_quote: often when after their initial domestic examination they have welfare or health coverage for eight months as part of their resettlement into the US and after that there's a huge period where big people become uninsured or don't know how to apply for Medicaid or how to get insurance and so there's a lot of fragmented and interrupted care

episode_title: #196 LIVE! Refugee Health with Tanuja Devaraj MD
statement: Use official interpreters rather than family members when caring for refugee patients.
supporting_quote: definitely use official interpreters and even when you do that there's certain understandings of disease

episode_title: #196 LIVE! Refugee Health with Tanuja Devaraj MD
statement: Mental health conditions (anxiety, adjustment disorder, depression, PTSD) are among the most common chronic problems in refugees; PTSD and depression rates may be as high as 30%, and suicide rates are about twice that of US-born individuals.
supporting_quote: some say there the rates of PTSD and as population is as high as 30 percent and depression is also as high as 30 percent and the rates of suicide is twice that of a u.s. born individual

episode_title: #196 LIVE! Refugee Health with Tanuja Devaraj MD
statement: Depression and anxiety in refugees often present as somatic symptoms such as headache, fatigue, abdominal pain, or insomnia rather than classic complaints of sadness.
supporting_quote: the the presentation is usually somatic symptoms of headache or fatigue or abdominal pain insomnia

episode_title: #196 LIVE! Refugee Health with Tanuja Devaraj MD
statement: Validated trauma screening tools for refugees include the Harvard Trauma Questionnaire and the Hopkins Symptom Checklist; the PCL-5 (developed for veterans and mapped to DSM-5), GAD-7, and PHQ-9 can also be used.
supporting_quote: the the tools that are available is the Harvard trauma questionnaire and the Hopkins symptom checklist which I haven't used but it's it's validated in different cultures and different languages for refugee population you have to purchase them they're not always easily available there so you can use the I use the pcl5 which was developed for veterans and it's a guide to the PTSD diagnosis as per the dsm-5

episode_title: #196 LIVE! Refugee Health with Tanuja Devaraj MD
statement: The CDC recommends screening all refugees for trauma with two questions: whether they experienced violence or trauma in their former country, and whether they would like to talk about it.
supporting_quote: the CDC has a simple like to question steps that you can do and one is have you ever experienced violence violence or trauma in the former country that you resided in if you did would you like to talk about it and leaving it open-ended

episode_title: #196 LIVE! Refugee Health with Tanuja Devaraj MD
statement: Chronic illnesses such as diabetes and hypertension present at younger ages in refugee populations, so check an A1c and lipid profile even in younger refugee patients rather than following standard USPSTF age thresholds.
supporting_quote: the chronic illnesses present differently and often present at a younger age it's not uncommon to see a 35 year old been woman with raging diabetes or uncontrolled hypertension so it's a little different from our population and what the u.s. PTF recommends so I would definitely check an a1c in a lipid profile for younger refugee populations

episode_title: #196 LIVE! Refugee Health with Tanuja Devaraj MD
statement: Screen refugees for hepatitis B and C, especially those from sub-Saharan Africa, Hmong populations in Thailand, or Burmese populations, since rates are high and screening provides a chance to prevent hepatocellular carcinoma.
supporting_quote: populations from sub-saharan Africa or the Hmong population in Thailand or the Burmese population the rates of hepatitis B and C very high and this is your chance to prevent HCC so always always screen and link to care

episode_title: #196 LIVE! Refugee Health with Tanuja Devaraj MD
statement: Most refugees are empirically treated for helminths before migration; test stool for ova and parasites and schistosomiasis only in those from endemic water-rich areas, and use serologic testing for Strongyloides since it is more sensitive than stool.
supporting_quote: so most refugees should be empirically treated prior to their migration to the US and I don't empirically treat for helmets or it's just a sohmas or strangle IDs and we have our ID expert in the audience but uh so it's I do test you do a stool test for onp and for Sisto only if they're in an endemic area where they if we go in contact with like water rich areas and then strong G you do the sarah logic testing as well

episode_title: #196 LIVE! Refugee Health with Tanuja Devaraj MD
statement: Strongyloides is important to detect because it persists in the same host and can cause a hyperinfection syndrome with high mortality if the patient becomes immunocompromised.
supporting_quote: stronger ladies is important or the reason why feared is if patients become immune compromised and they have a chronic infection the hyper infection syndrome can be very with the high mortality

episode_title: #196 LIVE! Refugee Health with Tanuja Devaraj MD
statement: A migration history is a key part of the social history: ask openly about where the patient was born, when and how long they lived in refugee camps, access to food/education/healthcare, the migration process, and the resettlement experience.
supporting_quote: it's just starting very broadly and open-ended asking about where the patient was born and if you know that they're a refugee what year did they migrate to the refugee camps how long did they live in the refugee camps what was life like and what was their access to to education or to food security to healthcare

episode_title: #196 LIVE! Refugee Health with Tanuja Devaraj MD
statement: When taking a past medical history, refugees may answer that they have no medical problems, so ask indirectly—whether they were taking medications, when they last saw a doctor, or had surgeries.
supporting_quote: when you ask someone what medical problems they have they may say oh nothing I was fine and the answer is is not what you're looking for and so asking it in a different way were you taking any medications before when was the last time you saw a doctor did you have any surgeries

episode_title: #184 Update in Sleep Medicine: LIVE from CHEST 2019!
statement: For adults, at least 7 hours of sleep per night is generally recommended; less than 5 hours or greater than 10 hours is considered pathologic and warrants evaluation.
supporting_quote: for adults you know it's generally recommended that we get greater than seven hours of sleep every night

episode_title: #184 Update in Sleep Medicine: LIVE from CHEST 2019!
statement: Insomnia and short sleep are associated with cardiovascular disease; in one Norwegian study of over 50,000 patients naive to cardiac disease, those with insomnia plus short sleep hours were four times at risk for an acute MI.
supporting_quote: there was a Norwegian study over 50,000 patients and they followed them for 11 and a half years and these were patients who were naive to cardiac disease and what they found was those who were suffering from insomnia plus having short sleep hours were four times at risk for getting an acute MI

episode_title: #184 Update in Sleep Medicine: LIVE from CHEST 2019!
statement: First-line therapy for chronic insomnia is cognitive behavioral therapy for insomnia (CBT-I), recommended by both the American Academy of Sleep Medicine and the American College of Physicians, rather than sedatives or alcohol.
supporting_quote: the first-line therapy recommended by again they count ASMR the American Academy of Sleep Medicine as well as American College of Physicians is a CBT I actually cognitive behavioral therapy that's probably the most effective means of treating chronic insomnia

episode_title: #184 Update in Sleep Medicine: LIVE from CHEST 2019!
statement: Online CBT-I programs (e.g., SHUTi) and a free VA app called the Sleep Coach are effective resources when access to in-person CBT-I is limited.
supporting_quote: there is a movement toward online CBT because exactly for what you say is this axis of care

episode_title: #184 Update in Sleep Medicine: LIVE from CHEST 2019!
statement: Because women metabolize zolpidem differently and have higher levels with residual driving impairment, the FDA recommended in 2013 lowering the ambien dose in women to 5 mg (immediate release) or 6.25 mg (extended release).
supporting_quote: the FDA came out in 2013 and recommended that for women you should stick with the lower dose of ambien five milligrams the long-acting 6.25 milligrams these are young women

episode_title: #184 Update in Sleep Medicine: LIVE from CHEST 2019!
statement: Z-drugs (non-benzodiazepine hypnotics) are intended for short-term use, and their most worrisome side effects are parasomnias such as sleep walking, sleep eating, and sleep driving.
supporting_quote: the side effects of ambien and the sea drugs is most worrisome as the parasomnias the night time to seek walking sleep eating I mean we've had patients report they just get in their cars

episode_title: #184 Update in Sleep Medicine: LIVE from CHEST 2019!
statement: CBT-I can help patients taper down or discontinue chronic sleep aids, and after years of use the effect is often more psychological than physiologic.
supporting_quote: CBT I can help with people you know decreasing the dose or you know the escalating or getting off of chronic sleep aids

episode_title: #184 Update in Sleep Medicine: LIVE from CHEST 2019!
statement: Prophylactic naps should generally be kept short (20 to 40 minutes) to improve alertness for at least two hours; longer naps risk sleep inertia and interfere with nighttime sleep.
supporting_quote: they should be kept short you know 20 to 40 minutes those you know those types of naps have shown to help with alertness for at least two hours after than that anything longer than that you risk what we call sleep inertia

episode_title: #184 Update in Sleep Medicine: LIVE from CHEST 2019!
statement: Melatonin is best used for circadian rhythm disorders, and physiologic doses as low as 0.5 mg are effective, whereas common over-the-counter doses of 3, 5, or 10 mg are supraphysiologic.
supporting_quote: you really only need point five milligrams so 0.5 milligrams to have an effect on educating for them so you know we're talking walking super physiologic doses of melatonin

episode_title: #184 Update in Sleep Medicine: LIVE from CHEST 2019!
statement: When melatonin is used for insomnia, it should be taken at the same time every night, since inconsistent timing shifts the circadian rhythm and can worsen sleep.
supporting_quote: I would just make sure they're taking at the same time every night because you know the way you get in trouble is it take it you know you know at a p.m. one time at lum p.m. the other night

episode_title: #184 Update in Sleep Medicine: LIVE from CHEST 2019!
statement: Testosterone is secreted during sleep and peaks about three hours in; sleep deprivation, particularly in the first three hours, lowers testosterone, and improving sleep may raise levels.
supporting_quote: testosterone becomes is secreted during your sleep it starts peaking about three hours into your sleep and then it probably Peaks maximally at you know early morning hours or so and studies have shown that if you're sleep-deprived particularly in the first three hours so you have more of a sleep onset type of problem with sleep then your testosterone levels are much lower

episode_title: #184 Update in Sleep Medicine: LIVE from CHEST 2019!
statement: In OSA, low testosterone is driven primarily by obesity rather than the apnea itself; weight loss increases testosterone, while CPAP effects on testosterone are equivocal.
supporting_quote: it's not really the sleep apnea per se that causes low testosterone this it's actually obesity so obesity and OS they often go you know go together

episode_title: #184 Update in Sleep Medicine: LIVE from CHEST 2019!
statement: Adolescents need more sleep than commonly assumed (at least 10 and up to 12 hours) and tend to be phase-delayed, contributing to chronic sleep deprivation with early school start times.
supporting_quote: they need much longer hours of sleep at least ten hours maybe even up to 12 hours of sleep surprisingly an adolescence is a time where a lot of a lot of them become phase shift delayed

episode_title: #184 Update in Sleep Medicine: LIVE from CHEST 2019!
statement: Pathologically phase-delayed adolescents can be effectively treated to advance their circadian rhythm using melatonin and light therapy.
supporting_quote: we can do that with melatonin we can do that with light therapy these are highly effective ways and it's actually really quite effective and easy to shift

episode_title: #184 Update in Sleep Medicine: LIVE from CHEST 2019!
statement: Shift work has been declared a probable carcinogen and is associated with increased cardiovascular disease and cancer, affecting an estimated 10 to 15 percent of the US workforce.
supporting_quote: 10 to 15 percent of the u.s. workforce or shift workers and shift work has been shown to be associated with more cardiovascular disease but also cancer surprisingly and and the it's been actually shift work has been declared a carcinogenic probable carcinogenic

episode_title: #184 Update in Sleep Medicine: LIVE from CHEST 2019!
statement: Women often have REM-predominant OSA, and because REM sleep apnea may drive cardiovascular outcomes, women matched to men for age and BMI have equivalent REM apnea-hypopnea severity, risking underdiagnosis when only total AHI is used.
supporting_quote: women are more likely to have what we call remin predominant OSA so they have a lot of sleep apnea during REM sleep and why is this important well you know just studies in the past three four years have come out that suggests that you know all that cardiovascular outcomes that we're seeing associated with sleep apnea may be more related to REM sleep apnea

episode_title: #184 Update in Sleep Medicine: LIVE from CHEST 2019!
statement: Consumer sleep trackers are reasonably good at detecting sleep but poor at detecting wake and inaccurate at distinguishing sleep stages, so patients should not rely on their sleep-stage data.
supporting_quote: they're pretty good at detecting sleep but poor and detecting wake so the device might say your weight but you're really actually asleep and on all of these devices are really poor at detecting the different sleep stages

episode_title: REBOOT #191 Lipids and Cardiovascular Risk Reduction with Erin Michos MD

episode_title: REBOOT #191 Lipids and Cardiovascular Risk Reduction with Erin Michos MD

episode_title: REBOOT #191 Lipids and Cardiovascular Risk Reduction with Erin Michos MD

episode_title: #116 Geriatric Psychiatry – Dementia Pearls
statement: Agitation and paranoia accompanying dementia are major drivers of nursing home placement, so it is important to treat these behavioral symptoms.
supporting_quote: the agitation and the and the paranoia that goes along with the dementia is a big factor in why people end up in nursing homes so you do really want to treat this

episode_title: #54: Upper Respiratory Infections: Coughs, colds, gargling, and antibiotic underuse?!
statement: Abstinence is not the only valid goal in treating alcohol use disorder; reducing drinking days, binge drinking, or overall consumption still improves outcomes, including decreased mortality.
supporting_quote:
even if you
decrease the number of um drinking days
or decrease the number of binge drinking
days or the the percentage of the week
spent drinking, all these sort of other
alternate metrics that are not just
strictly absence, these patients still
do better. Their mortality is decreased.

episode_title: #122 Headaches Advanced Class: Migraines, medication overuse, and more!
statement: Migraine is defined as a headache lasting 4 to 72 hours, requiring either photophobia and phonophobia or nausea/vomiting, plus at least two of four pain characteristics: unilaterality, throbbing quality, moderate-to-severe intensity, and worsening with or avoidance of activity.
supporting_quote:
migraine lasts from between 4 hours and
72 hours. And she told us that she has
nausea, and the classification criteria
require that a patient have either both
photophobia and phonophobia or nausea or
vomiting as an associated feature of
their headaches. But in order to confirm
a diagnosis of migraine, we need at
least two of four pain characteristics.
Those are
unilaterality, which she has already
confirmed,
a throbbing or pounding quality to the
pain,
severity of mild to I'm sorry, a
severity of moderate to severe, and then
either worsening with activity or
causing the person to avoid activity.

episode_title: #122 Headaches Advanced Class: Migraines, medication overuse, and more!
statement: Chronic migraine is defined by frequency (more than 15 headache days per month), not duration, whereas episodic migraine is fewer than 15 days per month.
supporting_quote:
chronic migraine is
more than 15 days per month.

episode_title: #122 Headaches Advanced Class: Migraines, medication overuse, and more!
statement: Medication overuse headache requires more than 15 days per month for acetaminophen or NSAIDs like naproxen, but only 10 or more days per month for butalbital combinations, triptans, ergots, and opioids.
supporting_quote:
So, for something like naproxen alone,
the same would be true for something
like acetaminophen, it's more than 15
days
per month, but if it's most of the other
medications that we use for acute
treatment, things like butalbital
combination medications, of which um
Fioricet is one, triptans, ergots,
opioids, it's 10 days a month or more to
contribute to medication overuse
headache.

episode_title: #122 Headaches Advanced Class: Migraines, medication overuse, and more!
statement: Chronic migraine requires at least eight days per month that are either migrainous or aborted by a migraine-specific medication and believed by the patient to be migrainous.
supporting_quote:
the um diagnostic
criteria for chronic migraine require
that the patient have at least eight
days a month that are either migrainous
or are aborted by treatment with a
migraine medication uh and believed by
the patient to be migrainous.

episode_title: #122 Headaches Advanced Class: Migraines, medication overuse, and more!
statement: Only about 50% of patients with medication overuse and headache improve after stopping the overused medication, and we cannot predict which patients will respond, so it is worth a trial of discontinuation but not reasonable to withhold preventive treatment during that process.
supporting_quote:
only about 50% of
the people who have medication overuse
and have headache will see their
headaches improve once the overused
medication is stopped. And we can't
predict ahead of time which uh 50% our
patient is going to fall into.
So, it's always worth a trial of getting
patients off of their overused acute
medication, but I don't think it's
reasonable to withhold other treatments
such as prevention while we are in that
process.

episode_title: #122 Headaches Advanced Class: Migraines, medication overuse, and more!
statement: Risk factors for migraine chronification include tobacco use, obesity, depression, sleep problems or sleep apnea, stressful life events, and low socioeconomic status; the transition back to episodic migraine is favored by being on and compliant with prevention and avoiding medication overuse.
supporting_quote:
risk factors
for chronification include tobacco use,
uh obesity, depression, um
difficult sleep or uh sleep apnea, and a
snoring, um stressful life events, low
socioeconomic status. There's a number
of other things. And the factors that
largely contribute to um
a transition back to episodic migraine
include being on prevention, being
compliant with uh medication, uh and and
avoiding medication overuse.

episode_title: #122 Headaches Advanced Class: Migraines, medication overuse, and more!
statement: There is no evidence that any one preventive works better than another for medication overuse headache, so preventive choice should be individualized to the patient.
supporting_quote:
it can be any migraine
preventive. There's no evidence that one
preventive works better for medication
overuse headache than another. So, it's
very specific to the patient.

episode_title: #122 Headaches Advanced Class: Migraines, medication overuse, and more!
statement: Abrupt discontinuation of butalbital combination medications at significant dosage risks withdrawal seizure because butalbital is a barbiturate; a phenobarbital taper can be used and hospitalization may be needed.
supporting_quote:
if it's a butalbital
combination medication that's being used
at significant dosage, there is a risk
of withdrawal seizure because butalbital
is a barbiturate. So, um
phenobarbital taper can actually be used
for that. Um and sometimes we even have
to bring people into the hospital.

episode_title: #122 Headaches Advanced Class: Migraines, medication overuse, and more!
statement: For acute treatment during withdrawal, agents that do not cause medication overuse headache include metoclopramide and hydroxyzine; acetaminophen and NSAIDs are considered lower risk than triptans, ergots, or butalbital.
supporting_quote:
there are some acute treatments that
don't cause medication overuse headache.
Things like metoclopramide,
hydroxyzine can be used this way.

episode_title: #122 Headaches Advanced Class: Migraines, medication overuse, and more!
statement: Migraine aura typically lasts 5 to 60 minutes, is visual in about 95% of people, and can also be sensory, motor (hemiplegic migraine), or speech-related.
supporting_quote:
aura
being the neurologic event that happens
typically before the migraine itself,
lasting from between 5 to 60 minutes
classically. Um often has a spreading
kind of quality.
It's visual in about 95% of people who
get aura of any kind, but can also be
sensory. It can be motor if uh the
diagnosis is hemiplegic migraine.

episode_title: #122 Headaches Advanced Class: Migraines, medication overuse, and more!
statement: Retinal migraine involves cortical spreading depression in the retina itself, producing aura in a unilateral eye rather than a unilateral visual field, and is fairly rare; ophthalmic migraine almost always just means migraine with visual aura.
supporting_quote:
Retinal migraine
is an actual diagnosis. It's when
there's cortical spreading depression,
which is the underlying pathophysiology
of migraine aura, that happens in the
retina itself. And what you will see is
a migraine aura that is in the
unilateral eye, as opposed to the
unilateral visual field,

episode_title: #122 Headaches Advanced Class: Migraines, medication overuse, and more!
statement: Triptans can be used safely in migraine with visual, sensory, or dysphasic aura; concern about triptan use remains only for hemiplegic migraine and brainstem (basilar) aura, both more severe phenotypes.
supporting_quote:
it's fine to use triptans in migraine
with visual aura, uh, migraine with
sensory aura, um, migraine with
dysphasic aura, or things like that.
There's there's no contraindication to
using triptans for for that. It's only
hemiplegic migraine and basilar migraine
or brainstem aura that that we worry
about it a little bit.

episode_title: #122 Headaches Advanced Class: Migraines, medication overuse, and more!
statement: Triptans work by preventing release of nociceptive neurotransmitters (including CGRP) from peripheral pain neurons, not through intracranial vasoconstriction.
supporting_quote:
in terms of how triptans work, um it
doesn't actually seem to be related to
intracranial vasoconstriction.
They act by preventing the release of um
nociceptive neurotransmitters from
peripheral
uh pain neurons to the deeper pain
structures in the brain.

episode_title: #122 Headaches Advanced Class: Migraines, medication overuse, and more!
statement: For migraine aura, magnesium (over-the-counter 400 to 500 mg a day) can be used for prevention and also as acute treatment for prolonged aura, and lamotrigine may particularly help aura even though it is not very effective for reducing overall migraine frequency.
supporting_quote:
magnesium.
Just over-the-counter 400 to 500 mg a
day seems to be useful for prevention of
migraine aura, and it can also be used
as an acute treatment if somebody is
having a prolonged aura. And uh, the
anti-epileptics may also be particularly
helpful for aura, um, particularly
lamotrigine, which is not all that
helpful for the reducing the frequency
of migraine itself, but for some reason
it does seem to help with aura,
particularly.

episode_title: #122 Headaches Advanced Class: Migraines, medication overuse, and more!
statement: For acute migraine in urgent or emergency settings, consider migraine-specific therapy such as subcutaneous sumatriptan (especially if only oral was tried at home) or dihydroergotamine, rather than defaulting to opioids.
supporting_quote:
If a
patient hasn't taken a triptan or has
it's been a little while since they've
had their triptan, try subcutaneous
sumatriptan, particularly if they only
had the oral at home. And I don't think
that's used as much as it should be. If
they haven't taken a triptan, the other
thing to consider is dihydroergotamine.

episode_title: #122 Headaches Advanced Class: Migraines, medication overuse, and more!
statement: Opioids, including tramadol, are not recommended for migraine because they can cause central sensitization that lowers the threshold for the next migraine, and guidelines show no opioids are very effective for migraine.
supporting_quote:
any opiate
is not really a good idea um for
treatment of migraine. All of the
opiates have the risk of causing central
sensitization
hypersensitivities that can then lower
the threshold for having the next
migraine and the
guidelines for
the efficacy of acute treatment also
show that no opiates
are really all that effective for
migraine anyway.

episode_title: #122 Headaches Advanced Class: Migraines, medication overuse, and more!
statement: Despite a black box warning, co-prescription of SSRIs/SNRIs with triptans is not supported by good evidence for causing serotonin syndrome, and headache specialists use them together, viewing SSRI use as not a contraindication to triptans.
supporting_quote:
There is a black box warning
on the co-prescription of those with the
triptans because the triptans are active
at serotonin receptors.
And there's this concern, this
theoretical concern about serotonin
syndrome. Um there is actually no real
good evidence-based for serotonin
syndrome as a byproduct of this
co-prescription.

episode_title: #122 Headaches Advanced Class: Migraines, medication overuse, and more!
statement: Venlafaxine has level B evidence for migraine prevention per the 2012 American Headache Society/American Academy of Neurology guidelines and may treat comorbid depression and migraine together.
supporting_quote:
I might actually go
straight for venlafaxine which has level
B evidence for efficacy of prevention of
migraine according to the 2012
American Headache Society American
Academy of Neurology guidelines.

episode_title: #122 Headaches Advanced Class: Migraines, medication overuse, and more!
statement: Women with migraine with aura already have about twice the risk of ischemic stroke, and high-dose estrogen-containing contraceptives roughly double that risk again, so estrogen-containing contraceptives are not recommended; progestogen-only formulations have no contraindication.
supporting_quote:
Women who have migraine with aura are
already at about twice the risk of
having ischemic stroke compared to women
who don't have migraine or who have
migraine without aura. So, aura itself
doubles the risk of of stroke. And then
the uh high-dose estrogen um
doubles the risk again. And for that
reason, we recommend that women who have
migraine with aura do not use
estrogen-containing
um contraceptives. The progesterone
component is fine.

episode_title: #122 Headaches Advanced Class: Migraines, medication overuse, and more!
statement: OnabotulinumtoxinA is FDA approved for prevention of chronic migraine only (15 or more days per month); trials for episodic migraine were negative, and insurance typically requires failure of three to four oral preventives first.
supporting_quote:
onabotulinumtoxinA
is FDA approved for the preventive
treatment of chronic migraine only.
The trials for episodic migraine and
again, chronic migraine is 15 days a
month or more and episodic is less than
15 days a month and the trials for
episodic migraine were conclusively
negative.

episode_title: #122 Headaches Advanced Class: Migraines, medication overuse, and more!
statement: CGRP monoclonal antibodies are approved for both episodic and chronic migraine (any frequency), but are typically reserved for patients who have failed oral preventives given uncertain long-term safety.
supporting_quote:
The CGRP monoclonal antibodies are approved
for both episodic and chronic migraine.
So, basically they're any frequency of
migraine um might qualify.

episode_title: #122 Headaches Advanced Class: Migraines, medication overuse, and more!
statement: A successful preventive migraine trial is defined as a 50% reduction in headache days, and treatment should not be called a failure if a 50% reduction is achieved.
supporting_quote:
a positive trial of a preventative
medication uh is a 50% reduction in
headache days. Which means that if a
patient starts at 30 and they go down to
15, we we think we're on the right
track.

episode_title: #122 Headaches Advanced Class: Migraines, medication overuse, and more!
statement: The most important lifestyle factor to address for headache patients is sleep, including adequate duration, good quality, and consistent sleep and wake times, since shifts in circadian rhythm can provoke headaches.
supporting_quote:
if I could pick
one lifestyle feature to fix for a
patient who has headaches of any kind
really, it would be sleep. Um adequate
duration of sleep, good quality sleep,
going to sleep and waking up at the same
time every day because sometimes shifts
in circadian rhythm can actually provoke
headaches for some patients.

episode_title: #122 Headaches Advanced Class: Migraines, medication overuse, and more!
statement: Trigeminal autonomic cephalalgias (TACs) share accompanying autonomic features (such as lacrimation, rhinorrhea, ptosis, miosis, facial flushing, conjunctival injection), unilaterality, and a severe stabbing quality, typically ocular or frontal in location.
supporting_quote:
And these are the hallmarks of a family
of medications called the trigeminal
autonomic cephalalgias, or um it's
abbreviated TAC, so we we call them
TACs.
And the thing they all have in common is
these accompanying autonomic features,
but they have some other clinical
characteristics in common as well,
including unilaterality and typically a
very severe and particularly stabbing
kind of quality to the pain.

episode_title: #122 Headaches Advanced Class: Migraines, medication overuse, and more!
statement: Among the TACs, paroxysmal hemicrania responds well to indomethacin, cluster headache responds best to verapamil, and SUNCT/SUNA respond to agents like lamotrigine.
supporting_quote:
the um, paroxysmal hemicrania, the
one in the middle, responds well to
indomethacin. Um, the other two, uh,
cluster headache and SUNCT or SUNA,
typically do not. Cluster headache tends
to respond best to verapamil and SUNCT
or SUNA to things like, um, lamotrigine.

episode_title: #122 Headaches Advanced Class: Migraines, medication overuse, and more!
statement: Cluster headache lasts about 15 minutes to 3 hours (once every other day to eight times daily), paroxysmal hemicrania lasts seconds to a few minutes, and SUNCT/SUNA are the shortest but most frequent.
supporting_quote:
Cluster headache is, uh, from about 15
minutes to 3 hours, and can happen, um,
from once every other day to eight times
a day. And then the the middle one is
paroxysmal hemicrania, which lasts from,
uh,
a number of seconds to a few minutes,

episode_title: #122 Headaches Advanced Class: Migraines, medication overuse, and more!
statement: Headaches provoked by exercise, or thunderclap headaches (maximal from onset), warrant vascular imaging (MRA) in addition to structural imaging.
supporting_quote:
The other thing
to consider is when you would do
vascular imaging um or an MRA. Uh and
for any patient with an exertional
headache, or a patient who has
thunderclap onset, so a headache that
just kind of comes on out of nowhere and
is maximal from the beginning, we would
order vascular imaging for that patient
as well.

episode_title: #122 Headaches Advanced Class: Migraines, medication overuse, and more!
statement: MRI without contrast is preferred over CT for headache workup because it better visualizes the posterior fossa and avoids cumulative radiation; contrast is reserved for concern for malignancy or low CSF pressure (which may show dural enhancement).
supporting_quote:
I typically order an MRI without
contrast for most of my patients. MRI is
strongly preferred to CT of the head for
a couple of reasons. One is that MRI I
gives us a better look at the um
cerebellum and brain stem.

episode_title: #122 Headaches Advanced Class: Migraines, medication overuse, and more!
statement: It is very rare to have increased intracranial pressure with a normal funduscopic exam, so if clinical suspicion is high and you cannot examine the discs yourself, get someone to look for papilledema.
supporting_quote:
It's
possible, but very rare to have
increased increased intracranial
pressure with a normal funduscopic exam.

episode_title: #122 Headaches Advanced Class: Migraines, medication overuse, and more!
statement: Even in patients with obesity and chronic headaches where idiopathic intracranial hypertension is considered, the vast majority of headaches are still migraine.
supporting_quote:
the vast majority of headaches that
you're going to see in people with
obesity are still going to be migraine.

episode_title: #122 Headaches Advanced Class: Migraines, medication overuse, and more!
statement: The rate of transition from high-frequency episodic migraine to chronic migraine (transformed migraine) is about 2.5% per year, with a slightly lower rate of reversion back to episodic.
supporting_quote:
It's about
2 and 1/2% per year
um going in the direction of episodic
migraine to chronic migraine and a
little bit lower coming back in the
other direction.

episode_title: #225 Women in Medicine: COVID Edition
statement: The 'motherhood penalty' means women returning from maternity leave often get a pay cut, whereas men returning from leave experience a 'fatherhood benefit' with a halo effect and pay raise.
supporting_quote:
there's a lot of ample research that we know on
the motherhood penalty it's not going to show you about this versus the
fatherhood benefit and that's documented
in the economics literature that women
returning from leave maternity leave get
a pay cut whereas men returning from
leave will often have a halo effect and
get a pay raise over time

episode_title: #225 Women in Medicine: COVID Edition
statement: The pandemic exacerbated publication bias, with data showing more first-author and senior-author publications by men than women during the pandemic due to unequal childcare burdens.
supporting_quote:
dr. Richmond jobs and
colleagues have proven this phenomenon
on publication bias binding an effective
more first author and more senior author
publications by men rather than women
during the pandemic

episode_title: #225 Women in Medicine: COVID Edition
statement: When making work decisions for pregnant colleagues or underrepresented physicians during a pandemic, ask and listen rather than assume; risk is individual and job responsibilities should not be shifted without consent.
supporting_quote:
I do caution people to whether
it's right to pregnancy or people from
groups underrepresented medicine to just
ask and listen and be respectful of the

episode_title: #225 Women in Medicine: COVID Edition
statement: The 'minority tax' worsens during dual pandemics, as institutions increasingly ask physicians of color to produce diversity content and organize events, driving burnout even higher.
supporting_quote:
women physicians of color are the

these events to write these statements
and that's driving burnout and even

episode_title: #225 Women in Medicine: COVID Edition
statement: Women physicians are at higher risk for burnout and suicide at baseline, and the pandemic's erosion of boundaries exacerbates these risks.
supporting_quote:
recent data has
shown that women
additions are more high-risk of Bernard
higher risk for suicide and the societal

episode_title: #225 Women in Medicine: COVID Edition
statement: The 'breadwinner-homemaker bias' is an outdated structural assumption baked into systems like the school day ending at 2:50 PM and 5 PM meetings, which disproportionately burdens working parents.
supporting_quote:
it's naming the fact that most of the

episode_title: #225 Women in Medicine: COVID Edition
statement: Emotional labor and mental load can be made visible and equalized by writing tasks down on a shared list, effectively running the household like a business partnership.
supporting_quote:
I
encourage you to write those things down

episode_title: #225 Women in Medicine: COVID Edition
statement: Don't assume a colleague without children can take extra shifts; they may be managing a personal or partner's health condition or fear of caring for COVID patients.
supporting_quote:
you can't
assume that the person without kids is

that's a form of inequity that I see as
well it's people saying things like well
that person is single they can step up

episode_title: #225 Women in Medicine: COVID Edition
statement: Consolidating telehealth into specific days and using video conferencing can eliminate long commutes and provide flexibility for working parents, and these tools are likely to persist beyond the pandemic.
supporting_quote:
telehealth and consolidate
consolidating telehealth into specific

episode_title: #225 Women in Medicine: COVID Edition
statement: Documenting 'COVID contributions' on the CV—including new volunteer activities, paused work, and caregiving responsibilities—provides a concrete framework to capture unpaid labor for future promotion review.

this kind of this concept of Kovach

episode_title: #225 Women in Medicine: COVID Edition
statement: Institutions should facilitate on-site or off-site supported childcare options for essential workers, and emergency daycare programs are wildly successful where they exist.

we need to start a conversation about

episode_title: #203 How to Create an Online Curriculum for Medical Education
statement: Providing food to boost conference attendance is costly and of limited value; one analysis found it costs about $46 per additional resident seat filled.
supporting_quote:
I came
across a great article called number

what impact food made and they
determined it cost forty six dollars per

episode_title: #203 How to Create an Online Curriculum for Medical Education
statement: WordPress with an associated domain name is an accessible, affordable backbone for an online medical curriculum, costing roughly $300 a year for hosting and domain.
supporting_quote:
the
backbone of this is a wordpress account
with an associated domain name and I
think together it cost us about three
hundred dollars a year for for the
hosting and the domain

episode_title: #203 How to Create an Online Curriculum for Medical Education
statement: Hosting video on a third-party site like Vimeo rather than directly on your website reduces strain on the site and offers useful playback options like accelerated speed, at about $240 per year extra.
supporting_quote:
we did use Vimeo specifically
because the playback options on it are
so well suited to what the learners want
if they've got a 90-minute to to our

the most out of their time so that was
where Vimeo came in and it was worth the

hundred and forty a year

episode_title: #203 How to Create an Online Curriculum for Medical Education
statement: Password-protecting an educational website protects patient information (e.g., M&M conferences), respects speakers who don't want content widely shared, and enables individualized login tracking of attendance and scores.
supporting_quote:
so part of it
was that not every member who was being

noon conference felt comfortable having
it out to the wider audience we were
still dealing with some semi proprietary
border new material any time we covered
anything patient information related
especially when we were doing our

episode_title: #203 How to Create an Online Curriculum for Medical Education
statement: The ABIM certifying exam blueprint is a freely accessible tool that lists exam topics with their relative weighting (e.g., ~14% cardiovascular disease vs. <1% allergy), letting educators allocate teaching time proportionally and helping learners identify knowledge gaps.
supporting_quote:
that blueprint has 14% of the board exam
being cardiovascular disease whereas
less than 1% of the exam is allergy if
I'm remembering correctly off the top of

those gaps in the curriculum a lot more
direct

episode_title: #203 How to Create an Online Curriculum for Medical Education
statement: Short-answer questions that force active recall and self-retrieval are evidence-based learning strategies worth incorporating alongside traditional multiple-choice vignette questions.
supporting_quote:
the other arm of
the self assessment tools we made were
more short answer type questions that
through the literature stress active
recall and self retrieval practices a
lot more in a lot stronger fashion

episode_title: #203 How to Create an Online Curriculum for Medical Education
statement: Twine, a free open-source interactive story tool, can build branching clinical scenarios (e.g., rapid response, code stroke) that let learners safely practice high-stakes decision-making and see outcomes of good and bad choices.
supporting_quote:
this is something
called trying so this is the only thing
we outsource twine or org it's
open-source it's an interactive story
creator

episode_title: #203 How to Create an Online Curriculum for Medical Education
statement: Free WordPress plugins can build quizzes: WordPress Pro Quiz for multiple-choice questions and Quiz Cards for short-answer flashcards, both with automatic scoring.
supporting_quote:
one that we one that we use

something called WordPress Pro quiz

episode_title: #203 How to Create an Online Curriculum for Medical Education
statement: Zoom can rapidly replace in-person didactics, with recorded lectures uploading to Vimeo; a paid plan (~$180/year for ~100 participants) allows larger meetings, cloud recording, and remote mobile access.

we we needed
about a hundred participants for our 50

episode_title: #203 How to Create an Online Curriculum for Medical Education
statement: Video-recorded lectures need not show the speaker; recording only the slides with audio (as via Zoom screen share) reassures reluctant speakers and meets learner needs.
supporting_quote:
so as soon as you
reassured everybody that no no the

not going to be on it everybody cooled

episode_title: #203 How to Create an Online Curriculum for Medical Education
statement: You can build a functional (if unpolished) educational website in about a week; the front-end setup requires the biggest time investment, after which month-to-month maintenance is modest.
supporting_quote:
You can make a pretty ugly WordPress
website and about a week it won't be
nice it won't be well laid out but it'll

episode_title: #203 How to Create an Online Curriculum for Medical Education
statement: A central, trusted website that only program leadership can update serves as disaster preparedness, providing a single accurate clearinghouse for information during rapidly changing circumstances.
supporting_quote:
given that our
program directors and chiefs could be
the only ones to update the website and
disseminate information it really helped
us control this is the accurate updated
information workflows schedules that
residents needed to pay attention to so

episode_title: #203 How to Create an Online Curriculum for Medical Education
statement: To engage otherwise passive learners in online sessions, requiring at least one meaningful chat contribution to count for attendance is a practical workaround.
supporting_quote:
sort of forcing everybody into
the chats and requiring that if you're

episode_title: #203 How to Create an Online Curriculum for Medical Education
statement: Adequate video capture can be achieved cheaply; an entry-level camera, tripod, and SD cards totaled about $120, and a built-in camera microphone can suffice.
supporting_quote:
a very entry-level $75 camera with
a tripod that we use for positioning and
of course make sure the camera works

episode_title: #174 Dominate Perioperative Medication Management
