supporting_quote: a new study a great position paper that's out fairly recently continue it looking at the pharmacology if you have an opiate that has a high affinity for them you receptor you can overcome the blockade fentanyl hydromorphone

episode_title: #174 Dominate Perioperative Medication Management
statement: Continue antiretroviral therapy and transplant medications perioperatively, taking them on the morning of surgery, since interrupting a dose could be catastrophic.
supporting_quote: so HIV on antiretroviral therapy keep patients on it same thing to transplant medications the risk of interrupting a dose could be catastrophic and there that's gonna be something to take when they're NPO in the morning of surgery

episode_title: #174 Dominate Perioperative Medication Management
statement: Continue SSRIs and SNRIs perioperatively, but MAO inhibitors must be stopped because they are dangerous with anesthesia.
supporting_quote: I'm keeping SSRIs SNRIs I'm keeping people on even though there's a your data about platelet inhibition and maybe bleeding risk mao eyes which i almost never see you got a stop they are dangerous with anesthesia

episode_title: #174 Dominate Perioperative Medication Management
statement: Continue levothyroxine perioperatively, taking it on the morning of surgery, and use the visit as an opportunity to check when thyroid function was last assessed.
supporting_quote: I would continue that medication

episode_title: REBOOT #151 Anaphylaxis w Dr. Olajumoke Fadugba
statement: Anaphylaxis can be diagnosed via NIAID/FAAN criteria: acute onset with skin/mucosal involvement PLUS either respiratory compromise or reduced blood pressure/end-organ dysfunction.
supporting_quote: a patient um the first one a patient who has an acute onset of symptoms typically within minutes to a few hours that involves the skin and or mucosa so microcutaneous symptoms that is itching flushing hives angioedema okay so that's one and either respiratory symptoms or reduced blood pressure and it's associated and organ manifestations

episode_title: REBOOT #151 Anaphylaxis w Dr. Olajumoke Fadugba
statement: A second diagnostic pathway for anaphylaxis: two or more organ systems involved (skin/mucosa, respiratory, GI, cardiovascular) after a likely allergen exposure.
supporting_quote: so if somebody has two or more of the following and i like to think of these uh things as organ systems so one skinner mucosa is one so that's rash hives or angioedema of the lips that's that's mean cucutaneous that's one system second system is respiratory third system is uh is um gi and fourth is the cardiovascular system so if you have two or more of these systems involved and you have a likely allergen exposure

episode_title: REBOOT #151 Anaphylaxis w Dr. Olajumoke Fadugba
statement: A third diagnostic pathway: in a patient with a known allergy, reduced blood pressure alone after exposure qualifies as anaphylaxis—they need not have hives or other systems involved.
supporting_quote: the third is someone eats something or is exposed to something they have a known history of an allergic reaction to all they need is one manifestation and that is evidence of reduced blood pressure they eat a cashew nut that they have had hives from before and this time they eat it by mistake and then they pass out okay they don't have to have hives

episode_title: REBOOT #151 Anaphylaxis w Dr. Olajumoke Fadugba
statement: IgE-mediated anaphylaxis requires prior sensitization, so an anaphylactic-type reaction on first exposure to an agent is likely non-IgE mediated.
supporting_quote: so if somebody has an anaphylactic type of reaction the first time they're exposed to that agent it's probably not ige mediated it's like anaphylaxis right because they didn't have time to make ige

episode_title: REBOOT #151 Anaphylaxis w Dr. Olajumoke Fadugba
statement: Itching is the key feature that distinguishes an allergic (anaphylactic) reaction with GI symptoms from food poisoning.
supporting_quote: so if you eat and you start vomiting profusely maybe it is food poisoning but if you are itching that is the main distinction and that is what clues you in to an allergic rather than a toxic event

episode_title: REBOOT #151 Anaphylaxis w Dr. Olajumoke Fadugba
statement: Older patients with comorbidities such as lung/airway disease, cardiovascular disease, or beta-blocker use are at higher risk of severe anaphylaxis and poor outcomes.
supporting_quote: there is we think there is an age association but it tends to be older people okay who who succumb to anaphylaxis usually because they have comorbid disease like lung disease or airway disease or cardiovascular disease and they're all in beta blockers

episode_title: REBOOT #151 Anaphylaxis w Dr. Olajumoke Fadugba
statement: In a study of over 4,000 anaphylaxis patients, about 5% (1 in 20) developed a biphasic reaction, with a median onset around 12 hours and a range of 1 to 72 hours.
supporting_quote: basically looked at over 4 000 patients with anaphylaxis and about 200 200 of them developed a biphasic event so that's about one in 20 as i said five percent and with in that study the median time to onset of a biphasic reaction was 12 hours of the studies have shown it to be around closer to eight hours the range is one to 72 hours

episode_title: REBOOT #151 Anaphylaxis w Dr. Olajumoke Fadugba
statement: Patients who initially present with low blood pressure and an unknown trigger were more likely to have a biphasic reaction.
supporting_quote: they found that um patients who initially presented with a low blood pressure with unknown trigger were more likely to have a biphasic reaction

episode_title: REBOOT #151 Anaphylaxis w Dr. Olajumoke Fadugba
statement: Epinephrine is first-line for anaphylaxis and should not be replaced by antihistamines or corticosteroids, which take too long to act (antihistamines/steroids act over 30 minutes to hours, while epinephrine acts within seconds).
supporting_quote: epinephrine is first line it can't should not be replaced by antihistamines or corticosteroids

episode_title: REBOOT #151 Anaphylaxis w Dr. Olajumoke Fadugba
statement: Epinephrine has broad beneficial effects: it stops mast cell histamine release, raises blood pressure, promotes bronchodilation, and reduces mucosal edema.
supporting_quote: it sort of stops mast cells from releasing more histamines that's a big one it um increases blood pressure it promotes uh bronchodilation reduces mucosal edema

episode_title: REBOOT #151 Anaphylaxis w Dr. Olajumoke Fadugba
statement: There is no absolute contraindication to epinephrine in anaphylaxis; even elderly patients with hypertension or cardiovascular disease should receive it, as they are at highest risk of dying and the benefit outweighs the risk.
supporting_quote: there there is no absolute contraindication to giving epinephrine um if the patient is elderly they have hypertension they have cardiovascular disease you can still give epinephrine

episode_title: REBOOT #151 Anaphylaxis w Dr. Olajumoke Fadugba
statement: Epinephrine should be dispensed as a twin pack; a second dose can be given 5 to 15 minutes after the first if symptoms persist.
supporting_quote: epinephrine should always be dispensed as a twin pack two of them and the reason you have two the actual reason you have two is because you may have symptoms that don't go away and you can use a second dose within five to 15 minutes of the first

episode_title: REBOOT #151 Anaphylaxis w Dr. Olajumoke Fadugba
statement: Epinephrine autoinjectors are given into the outer thigh through clothing and held in place (about 10 seconds); patients who use it should then go to the emergency room for monitoring.
supporting_quote: put it in your outer thigh through your clothing you don't have to take off your pants or anything like that and you put it in there keep it in there some devices you have to keep it in there for 10 seconds some shorter i just say 10 seconds

episode_title: REBOOT #151 Anaphylaxis w Dr. Olajumoke Fadugba
statement: Intramuscular epinephrine is dosed at 1:1000 concentration, whereas intravenous epinephrine is the more dilute 1:10,000 and requires monitoring.
supporting_quote: if you're giving an im it's a one to one thousand which is more concentrated than the intravenous is the one sorry a one to ten thousand which which makes sense exactly and uh the intravenous of course is gonna require monitoring

episode_title: REBOOT #151 Anaphylaxis w Dr. Olajumoke Fadugba
statement: Systematic reviews found no good evidence supporting a role for corticosteroids in anaphylaxis, though they are commonly given as adjunctive therapy.
supporting_quote: there there have been two sort of large systematic reviews on the question on the role of corticosteroids in anaphylaxis the uh the conclusion is that there is no uh good role uh uh for the corticosteroids in nfl access

episode_title: REBOOT #151 Anaphylaxis w Dr. Olajumoke Fadugba
statement: A serum tryptase above approximately 11 micrograms/liter supports a diagnosis of anaphylaxis; the best time to draw it is within 1 to 3 hours of symptom onset, as levels peak around 90 minutes and normalize by 5 to 8 hours.
supporting_quote: a level above approximately 11 micrograms per liter may be supportive of a diagnosis of anaphylaxis now tryptase levels rise pretty quickly after symptom onset they peak around 90 minutes and they're usually back down to normal within five to eight hours so the best time to get a trip taste level is within one to three hours of symptom onset

episode_title: REBOOT #151 Anaphylaxis w Dr. Olajumoke Fadugba
statement: A normal tryptase does not rule out anaphylaxis; it is more likely elevated in severe, hypotensive episodes or those due to medications or bee stings, so clinical judgment remains essential.
supporting_quote: a trip case is not always elevated during anaphylaxis so if somebody has a normal tryptase it does not rule out anaphylaxis and so the clinical scenario is really important it's more likely to be high if it was a really severe episode and they were hypotensive or if it was from a medication or from a bee sting

episode_title: REBOOT #151 Anaphylaxis w Dr. Olajumoke Fadugba
statement: Anaphylactoid (non-IgE, non-immunologic) reactions tend to be more responsive to premedication such as diphenhydramine before contrast, opioids, or vancomycin, whereas IgE-mediated reactions typically do not respond to premedication.
supporting_quote: sort of anaphylactoid reactions tend to be more responsive to pre-medication and and sort of preventing the symptom from pre-medication we don't fully understand why that is so we often pre-treat for contrast mediated reactions sometimes we'll give benadryl before opioids we get benadryl before vancomycin meanwhile sort of ige mediated reactions don't tend to respond to that type of pre-treatment regimen

episode_title: REBOOT #151 Anaphylaxis w Dr. Olajumoke Fadugba
statement: The case mortality rate of anaphylaxis is very low (0.001%), but because severe outcomes are unpredictable, patients who meet criteria should be treated early.
supporting_quote: the case mortality rate of anaphylaxis is point zero zero one percent so most people don't die that's good thankfully of anaphylaxis right so why am i talking about all this is because it's not predictable

episode_title: REBOOT #151 Anaphylaxis w Dr. Olajumoke Fadugba
statement: Red man syndrome from vancomycin is a non-IgE reaction that is typically mild; slowing the infusion (with or without diphenhydramine) is usually adequate.
supporting_quote: red man syndrome is typically mild you can as you know slow down the infusion sometimes that's adequate sometimes you need to slow down the infusion and pre-treat with diphenhydramine

episode_title: #136 Sickle Cell Disease Management & Complications
statement: Sickle cell 'anemia' refers to the most severe genotypes (SS and S beta-zero thalassemia), while 'sickle cell disease' encompasses all types including SC and S beta-plus thalassemia.
supporting_quote: when when a sickle cell doc says sickle cell anemia, they're talking about people who are homozygous for SS disease or S beta zero thalassemia.

episode_title: #136 Sickle Cell Disease Management & Complications
statement: On HPLC, sickle trait shows about 40% S and 60% A, whereas S beta-plus thalassemia shows about 60% S and 40% A and manifests as disease—a distinction that is commonly confused.
supporting_quote:
if you have sickle trait, you will make
40% S and 60% A.

episode_title: #136 Sickle Cell Disease Management & Complications
statement: Patients with sickle beta-plus thalassemia have more S than A on HPLC and will have manifestations of disease, unlike those with trait who have more A than S and no disease.
supporting_quote:
they will
have more hemoglobin A than hemoglobin
S, but people with S beta plus thal will
have more S than A. And they will have
manifestations of sickle cell disease.

episode_title: #136 Sickle Cell Disease Management & Complications
statement: Assume any adult with SS sickle cell disease has no functional spleen (auto-infarcted) and vaccinate against encapsulated bacteria (pneumococcus, meningococcus, H. flu) plus influenza.
supporting_quote:
you can
just assume that an adult who walks into
your office with sickle cell disease
does not have a functional spleen. And
that means they need to be immunized
against encapsulated bacteria. So,
pneumococcal vaccinations, meningococcal
vaccinations, H flu, um and then the
influenza vaccine, too

episode_title: #136 Sickle Cell Disease Management & Complications
statement: Serum creatinine is a poor measure of renal function in sickle cell disease because patients hyperfilter and hypersecrete creatinine; screen annually for proteinuria/microalbuminuria, and a rising creatinine signals significant renal loss.
supporting_quote:
the serum
creatinine is an incredibly poor measure
of renal dysfunction in people with
sickle cell disease.

episode_title: #136 Sickle Cell Disease Management & Complications
statement: Sickle cell patients need annual eye exams to screen for retinal disease, which increases the risk of vision loss.
supporting_quote:
annual eye exams looking for signs of
retinal disease, which increases the
risk of vision loss, is also an
additional recommendation for this
patient population.

episode_title: #136 Sickle Cell Disease Management & Complications
statement: A new atypical crisis (different from the patient's usual crisis) is a warning sign that something serious may be occurring.
supporting_quote:
when a patient presents it's an
atypical crisis, that's a warning sign
that something bad might be going on
that you really have to think about.

episode_title: #136 Sickle Cell Disease Management & Complications
statement: Ask about lifetime transfusion history because more than 20-50 units suggests risk of iron overload, and ask about prior antibodies since transfused patients may develop alloantibodies not detected on a same-day type and screen.
supporting_quote:
getting over 20, 30,
40, 50 units of blood suggests that they
might be at risk for iron overload. And
then I ask them whether or not they've
anyone's ever had trouble finding blood
for them or if they know they have any
antibodies

episode_title: #136 Sickle Cell Disease Management & Complications
statement: Confirm the genotype with hemoglobin electrophoresis/HPLC in a new patient, because patients may not know their true type and some carry an unconfirmed 'trait' label when they actually have disease.
supporting_quote:
if I don't have the test, I
will repeat it cuz
often patients don't know exactly what
they have
and and it's useful for me to make sure

episode_title: #136 Sickle Cell Disease Management & Complications
statement: Life expectancy has improved: about 95% of children now survive to age 18, and average adult life expectancy is into the 50s-60s, though still decreased by roughly 25-30 years versus the general African-American population.
supporting_quote:
the average life
expectancy is into the 50s or 60s.

episode_title: #136 Sickle Cell Disease Management & Complications
statement: Hydroxyurea is the number one intervention for sickle cell disease; it increases fetal hemoglobin, lowers white counts and adhesion molecules, acts as an NO donor, decreases crises and acute chest, reduces transfusion need, and improves survival in observational trials.
supporting_quote:
we know it
decreases crisis frequency, it decreases
risk of acute chest, it decreases the
need for transfusion, and two
observational trials have shown uh
improved overall survival for people on
hydroxyurea.

episode_title: #136 Sickle Cell Disease Management & Complications
statement: Hydroxyurea can be prescribed by primary care and is titrated to maximally tolerated dose, pushing the ANC to between 2,000 and 4,000 (closer to 2,000) while keeping platelets and absolute reticulocyte count above 90,000, with monthly count monitoring initially.
supporting_quote:
pushing the ANC down, absolute
neutrophil count down to between 2 and
4,000, really closer to the 2,000 than
the 4,000, while maintaining a platelet
count of greater than 90,000 and an

episode_title: #136 Sickle Cell Disease Management & Complications
statement: Chronic transfusion therapy has its best evidence in primary stroke prevention in children with abnormal transcranial Dopplers, targeting hemoglobin S less than 30%.
supporting_quote:
the best evidence-based for the use

primary stroke prevention in children
who have had abnormal transcranial

episode_title: #136 Sickle Cell Disease Management & Complications
statement: Chronic simple transfusions risk iron overload (since the only way to lose iron is bleeding), whereas exchange transfusions can produce a net-negative iron load and even cause iron deficiency.
supporting_quote:
the most
concerning is iron overload, which can
happen with simple transfusions, right?
Cuz the only way you really can get rid

episode_title: #136 Sickle Cell Disease Management & Complications
statement: Avoid transfusing sickle cell patients to a hemoglobin that is too high (generally not above 10 in SS disease) because increased viscosity can cause strokes, crises, or other complications.
supporting_quote:
If you transfuse patient with
sickle cell disease to a hemoglobin

episode_title: #136 Sickle Cell Disease Management & Complications
statement: Ferritin correlates poorly with iron overload in sickle cell disease because it is an acute phase reactant; follow monthly trends and use annual or semi-annual specialized MRI to quantify liver and cardiac iron.
supporting_quote:
ferritin is an acute phase reactant. It doesn't

episode_title: #136 Sickle Cell Disease Management & Complications
statement: Neurocognitive dysfunction—including silent cerebral infarcts affecting executive function—is common in sickle cell disease; consider neuropsychological testing in patients who seem frustratingly nonadherent.
supporting_quote:
Those are the people who

episode_title: #136 Sickle Cell Disease Management & Complications
statement: There is no objective laboratory measure of a vaso-occlusive crisis; the gold standard for diagnosing a crisis is the patient reporting they are having one, and their labs may look identical to baseline.

there is no objective
measure of crisis. There just isn't one.

episode_title: #136 Sickle Cell Disease Management & Complications
statement: Patients with sickle cell disease who don't present often should have short-acting opioids (e.g., oxycodone) at home for a stepwise self-management approach; avoid Tylenol-containing combinations to limit acetaminophen exposure.

I like to

episode_title: #136 Sickle Cell Disease Management & Complications
statement: For admitted vaso-occlusive crises, PCA is preferred over bolus therapy, initial bolus dosing should be given, and pain must be maximized within the first 24 hours; reassess every 3-4 hours and calculate the 24-hour opioid dose to track therapy.
supporting_quote:
the real key when a patient gets admitted

episode_title: #136 Sickle Cell Disease Management & Complications
statement: When starting to wean a PCA, always calculate the total 24-hour opioid dose, since a seemingly tiny per-dose amount can add up to a large total (e.g., 50 mg IV Dilaudid/24h) and patients may push the button more to offset weaning.

you
must calculate the 24-hour dose that

episode_title: #136 Sickle Cell Disease Management & Complications
statement: Opioid-induced itching can be treated with oral antihistamines (avoiding IV Benadryl); adding a small amount of Narcan to the PCA (studied in pediatrics) can decrease itching and other opioid side effects.

putting some um
Narcan in the PCA actually can really

episode_title: #136 Sickle Cell Disease Management & Complications
statement: Do not transfuse simply because a patient is having a crisis (a Choosing Wisely recommendation); transfuse for symptomatic anemia, hypoxia, or being more anemic than usual without an appropriate reticulocyte response.

It is

episode_title: #136 Sickle Cell Disease Management & Complications
statement: The perioperative transfusion goal for sickle cell anemia is a hemoglobin of 10, achieved by simple or exchange transfusion.

if you have

episode_title: #136 Sickle Cell Disease Management & Complications
statement: Fever in a sickle cell patient requires in-person evaluation because functional asplenia predisposes to severe life-threatening infection; do a full workup and cover empirically with antibiotics only if the patient looks toxic or a source is found.

he needs to come in and be evaluated um

episode_title: #136 Sickle Cell Disease Management & Complications
statement: Acute chest syndrome is a clinical diagnosis requiring a new infiltrate on chest x-ray, fever, and respiratory symptoms; it typically develops a couple of days into an admission for vaso-occlusive crisis rather than at presentation.

it requires um
a new infiltrate on chest x-ray, a

episode_title: #136 Sickle Cell Disease Management & Complications
statement: Not all acute chest syndrome requires transfusion—in a prospective study of over 600 cases, only 72% required transfusion; give blood for hypoxia/symptomatic anemia (e.g., tachycardic at 120 on 4L satting 92%), not for mild cases.

in the prospective study that looked

episode_title: #136 Sickle Cell Disease Management & Complications
statement: Incentive spirometry helps prevent acute chest syndrome, especially in patients receiving opioids, so every admitted sickle cell patient should get an incentive spirometer.

if you give them

episode_title: #136 Sickle Cell Disease Management & Complications
